## SECTION 3 : STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Center-of-Gravity Method

The **center-of-gravity** method locates a single new facility that minimizes the **total weighted distance** to a set of existing supply or demand points. It is the continuous analog of the discrete *Weber problem* (1909).

**Step 1 — Formulate the objective function**

We want to minimize the total load-distance:

$$
\min \;\; Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot d_i(x^{*}, y^{*})
$$

where $(x^{*}, y^{*})$ are the coordinates of the new facility, $(x_i, y_i)$ are the coordinates of source/destination $i$, and $w_i$ is the load at point $i$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Euclidean distance metric**

$$
d_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x^{*})^{2} + (y_i - y^{*})^{2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \sqrt{(x_i - x^{*})^{2} + (y_i - y^{*})^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the partial derivative of $Z$ with respect to $x^{*}$ and set it to zero (first-order optimality condition)**

$$
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x^{*}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{-(x_i - x^{*})}{\sqrt{(x_i - x^{*})^{2} + (y_i - y^{*})^{2}}} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that the denominator $d_i$ is positive and re-arrange**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{(x_i - x^{*})}{d_i} = 0
$$

Expanding the sum:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{w_i \cdot x_i}{d_i} \;-\; x^{*} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{w_i}{d_i} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x^{*}$**

$$
x^{*} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{w_i \cdot x_i}{d_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{w_i}{d_i}}
$$

**Step 6 — Repeat Steps 3–5 on the y-axis**

$$
y^{*} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{w_i \cdot y_i}{d_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{w_i}{d_i}}
$$

**Step 7 — Iterative note (important!)**

The distances $d_i$ in the denominators are themselves functions of $(x^{*}, y^{*})$, so the system is **not closed-form**. The **practical (KTU-approved) simplification** is to assume $d_i \approx$ a constant or use a straight average:

$$
x^{*} \approx \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}, \quad y^{*} \approx \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Examination Tip:** The simplified form is what KTU papers expect. The exact iterative form is a higher-order concept tested only in Part B for full marks.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Center of Gravity

> [!NOTE]  
> A logistics company is locating a **new central warehouse** to serve four retail hubs. The data are given below. Find the optimal coordinates of the new warehouse using the simplified center-of-gravity method.

| Hub $i$ | $x_i$ (km) | $y_i$ (km) | $w_i$ (tonnes/month) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 10 | 25 |
| 2 | 15 | 20 | 35 |
| 3 | 25 | 5 | 20 |
| 4 | 35 | 15 | 40 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **120** |

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator for $x^{*}$**

$$
\sum w_i x_i = (25 \cdot 5) + (35 \cdot 15) + (20 \cdot 25) + (40 \cdot 35)
$$

$$
= 125 + 525 + 500 + 1400 = 2550
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator for $y^{*}$**

$$
\sum w_i y_i = (25 \cdot 10) + (35 \cdot 20) + (20 \cdot 5) + (40 \cdot 15)
$$

$$
= 250 + 700 + 100 + 600 = 1650
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator**

$$
\sum w_i = 25 + 35 + 20 + 40 = 120
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $x^{*}$ and $y^{*}$**

$$
x^{*} = \frac{2550}{120} = 21.25 \text{ km}
$$

$$
y^{*} = \frac{1650}{120} = 13.75 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Result:** The optimal new warehouse location is at coordinates **(21.25 km, 13.75 km)**. This point minimizes the *first-moment* of load distribution. **Validation:** It is roughly *centroidal* — closer to hubs 2, 3, and 4 which carry higher weights (35, 20, 40 tonnes).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Generic MILP Formulation of a Network Design Problem

> [!NOTE]  
> The mathematical programming model below is the **core of optimization-based network design** as prescribed in the KTU syllabus.

**Sets:**
- $I$ = set of potential facility sites (indexed by $i$)
- $J$ = set of demand zones / customers (indexed by $j$)
- $K$ = set of products (indexed by $k$)

**Parameters:**
- $f_i$ = annual fixed cost of opening facility at $i$
- $c_{ijk}$ = unit shipping cost from $i$ to $j$ for product $k$
- $u_{ik}$ = unit production / processing cost at facility $i$ for product $k$
- $h_{ik}$ = unit annual holding cost of product $k$ at $i$
- $D_{jk}$ = annual demand of product $k$ at customer $j$
- $C_i$ = annual capacity of facility $i$ (units)
- $S_{ik}$ = sourcing capacity for product $k$ at $i$

**Decision Variables:**
- $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ = 1 if facility $i$ is opened, 0 otherwise
- $X_{ijk} \ge 0$ = units of product $k$ shipped from $i$ to $j$
- $P_{ik} \ge 0$ = units of product $k$ produced at $i$

**Objective Function — Minimize total annual cost:**

$$
\min Z = \underbrace{\sum_{i \in I} f_i \cdot y_i}_{\text{Fixed facility cost}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i \in I} \sum_{j \in J} \sum_{k \in K} c_{ijk} \cdot X_{ijk}}_{\text{Transportation cost}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i \in I} \sum_{k \in K} u_{ik} \cdot P_{ik}}_{\text{Production cost}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i \in I} \sum_{k \in K} h_{ik} \cdot I_{ik}}_{\text{Inventory cost}}
$$

**Subject to the following constraints:**

**(C1) Demand Satisfaction** — Every customer's demand must be met:

$$
\sum_{i \in I} X_{ijk} \;=\; D_{jk} \qquad \forall j \in J, \; \forall k \in K
$$

**(C2) Flow Conservation at Facility** — What is shipped out cannot exceed what is on hand (or produced):

$$
\sum_{j \in J} X_{ijk} \;\le\; P_{ik} \qquad \forall i \in I, \; \forall k \in K
$$

**(C3) Facility Capacity** — A facility cannot produce more than its annual capacity:

$$
\sum_{k \in K} P_{ik} \;\le\; C_i \cdot y_i \qquad \forall i \in I
$$

**(C4) Production-Open Linkage** — A facility can only produce if it is open:

$$
P_{ik} \;\le\; S_{ik} \cdot y_i \qquad \forall i \in I, \; \forall k \in K
$$

**(C5) Non-negativity and Binary Constraints**

$$
X_{ijk} \ge 0, \quad P_{ik} \ge 0, \quad y_i \in \{0, 1\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This is a **Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP)**. It is the KTU-recommended model class. The solver will simultaneously decide (a) **which** sites to open, (b) **how much** to produce at each, and (c) **which** customers to serve from which site.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Strategic vs. Tactical vs. Operational Decisions

| Attribute | Strategic (Network) | Tactical (Inventory/Transp.) | Operational (Order Fulfillment) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | 3–10 years | 3 months – 1 year | Daily / weekly |
| Capital intensity | Very high | Moderate | Low |
| Reversibility | Difficult | Moderate | Easy |
| Data uncertainty | High | Moderate | Low |
| Examples | Open/close plants, choose warehouse sites | Set base stock, vehicle routes | Release orders, pick-pack-ship |
| Managerial tool | MILP, scenario analysis, simulation | Newsvendor, EOQ, DRP | WMS, TMS |
| Cost impact | **15–25 %** of TNC | **5–10 %** of TNC | 1–3 % of TNC |

### 4.1 Generic Supply Chain Network Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  SUP1[Supplier A]
  SUP2[Supplier B]
  SUP3[Supplier C]
  PLT1[Plant North]
  PLT2[Plant South]
  RDC1[Regional DC East]
  RDC2[Regional DC West]
  LD1[Local DC 1]
  LD2[Local DC 2]
  LD3[Local DC 3]
  CUS1[Customer Zone 1]
  CUS2[Customer Zone 2]
  CUS3[Customer Zone 3]
  CUS4[Customer Zone 4]

  SUP1 --> PLT1
  SUP2 --> PLT1
  SUP3 --> PLT2
  PLT1 --> RDC1
  PLT1 --> RDC2
  PLT2 --> RDC1
  PLT2 --> RDC2
  RDC1 --> LD1
  RDC1 --> LD2
  RDC2 --> LD3
  LD1 --> CUS1
  LD1 --> CUS2
  LD2 --> CUS3
  LD3 --> CUS4
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The diagram shows a **four-echelon network**: Suppliers → Plants → Regional DCs → Local DCs → Customers. Each arrow is an arc (transportation link); each rectangle is a node (facility). Network design optimizes the number, location, and sizing of these nodes and the flows on these arcs.

---

### 4.2 Network Design Decision Framework (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Define Supply Chain Strategy] --> B[Forecast Customer Demand and Service Targets]
  B --> C[Collect Cost and Lead-Time Data]
  C --> D[Build Candidate Network Scenarios]
  D --> E[Run MILP Optimizer]
  E --> F{Constraints Feasible?}
  F -- Yes --> G[Select Optimal Network]
  F -- No --> H[Relax Constraints or Add Capacity]
  H --> D
  G --> I[Validate via Simulation]
  I --> J[Implementation and Performance Tracking]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The block diagram captures the **closed-loop** process: after implementation, performance data feeds back into the next strategic cycle. This iterative logic is a frequent **short-answer question** in KTU Part A.

---

### 4.3 Trade-Off Curve: Number of Facilities vs. Total Cost

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  N1[N = 1 Facility]
  N2[N = 2 Facilities]
  N3[N = 3 Facilities]
  N4[N = 4 Facilities]
  N5[N = 5 Facilities]
  N6[N = 6 Facilities]

  N1 -->|High transport, low facility| T1[Total Cost High]
  N2 --> T2[Total Cost Lower]
  N3 --> T3[Total Cost Lowest - OPTIMAL]
  N4 --> T4[Total Cost Rises]
  N5 --> T5[Total Cost Higher]
  N6 --> T6[Total Cost Highest - Too many facilities]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> This conceptual U-curve is what the **square-root rule** estimates: as demand grows, the optimal $N$ grows sub-linearly. The exact position of the minimum depends on facility, transportation, and inventory cost ratios.

## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]

**Differentiate between strategic, tactical, and operational decisions in supply chain network design. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Level | Time Horizon | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | 3–10 years; capital intensive | (i) Decide number and location of plants; (ii) Choose whether to outsource or in-house production. |
| **Tactical** | 3 months – 1 year | (i) Set base-stock level at each warehouse; (ii) Select transportation mode between two echelons. |
| **Operational** | Daily/weekly | (i) Release a specific customer order; (ii) Pick-pack-ship a single carton. |

*(1 mark for clear definition, 1 mark for time horizon distinction, 1 mark for two examples per level — award 0.5 for partial listing.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Explain the center-of-gravity method for locating a single facility. State one limitation of the method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **center-of-gravity method** is a heuristic that places a new facility at the **load-weighted average** of existing supply or demand points. Using coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ and load $w_i$ at each existing point, the optimal location is:

$$
x^{*} = \frac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}, \quad y^{*} = \frac{\sum w_i y_i}{\sum w_i}
$$

The method is fast, intuitive, and works well for *single-facility, single-product* settings.

**Limitation (any one):** (i) It assumes a flat earth and straight-line distances, ignoring roads and barriers. (ii) It does not optimize capacity or routing, only location. (iii) It treats all loads as continuous, ignoring integer shipping constraints. (iv) It uses a simplified cost (linear in distance) and ignores the trade-off between facility cost and transportation cost.

*(1 mark method, 1 mark formula, 1 mark limitation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]

**(a)** *Discuss in detail the framework for network design decisions. Explain the four hierarchical levels and the data required at each level.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **network design framework** (Chopra & Meindl; the KTU reference) breaks supply chain decisions into **four hierarchical levels**, where each level aggregates and constrains the next:

**Level 1 — Supply Chain Strategy (2 marks)**

- Defines the chain's structure: which customers are served, which products are offered, and the level of *responsiveness* (efficient vs. responsive).
- Data needed: customer segmentation, product portfolio, market growth forecasts, competitor strategy.
- *Output:* High-level positioning statement, e.g., "We will be a low-cost efficient chain for commodity buyers."

**Level 2 — Configuration of Regional Facilities (2 marks)**

- Decides the **number, location, capacity** of plants and warehouses, the **assignment of product families to plants**, and the **mode of transportation** between echelons.
- Data needed: demand volumes per zone, facility fixed/variable cost, transportation lane rates, lead times, tariffs, capacity constraints.
- *Output:* Candidate network scenarios, evaluated via MILP, simulation, or heuristics (e.g., center of gravity).

**Level 3 — Customer Service & Inventory (1.5 marks)**

- Decides the **stocking policy** (base stock, s, S), the **order-fulfillment process**, and the **information infrastructure** (EDI, RFID, ERP integration).
- Data needed: demand variability, fill-rate targets, IT cost, replenishment lead time variability.
- *Output:* Inventory targets per SKU per location.

**Level 4 — Day-to-Day Operations (1.5 marks)**

- Includes *production scheduling, lot sizing, replenishment frequency, vehicle routing, order release, and pick-pack-ship*.
- Data needed: real-time order book, capacity calendars, vehicle availability.
- *Output:* Daily execution plan.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Closure of argument:** Levels 1 and 2 are *strategic* and *lock in 60–80 % of supply chain cost*. Levels 3 and 4 are *tactical/operational* and adjust within the strategy envelope.

---

**(b)** *A logistics company is setting up a new central warehouse to serve four retail hubs. The data are tabulated below. Use the center-of-gravity method to determine the optimal coordinates of the new warehouse.* **(7 marks)**

| Hub $i$ | $x_i$ (km) | $y_i$ (km) | $w_i$ (tonnes/month) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 10 | 25 |
| 2 | 15 | 20 | 35 |
| 3 | 25 | 5 | 20 |
| 4 | 35 | 15 | 40 |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute the weighted sums.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\sum w_i x_i = (25 \cdot 5) + (35 \cdot 15) + (20 \cdot 25) + (40 \cdot 35) = 125 + 525 + 500 + 1400 = 2550
$$

$$
\sum w_i y_i = (25 \cdot 10) + (35 \cdot 20) + (20 \cdot 5) + (40 \cdot 15) = 250 + 700 + 100 + 600 = 1650
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total load.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\sum w_i = 25 + 35 + 20 + 40 = 120
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the center-of-gravity formulas.** *(3 marks)*

$$
x^{*} = \frac{2550}{120} = 21.25 \text{ km}
$$

$$
y^{*} = \frac{1650}{120} = 13.75 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation & validation.** *(2 marks)*

The optimal location is **(21.25, 13.75)**, which lies **closer to hubs 2, 3, and 4** since their combined load (95 tonnes) is much greater than hub 1's 25 tonnes. This is consistent with the principle of *attraction to higher loads*.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Common mistakes students make in this question:
> 1. **Forgetting the division** by $\sum w_i$ — this is an instant **−2 mark** deduction.
> 2. **Mixing x and y coordinates** — a transcription error that shows no understanding.
> 3. **Not stating the unit (km)** — *−0.5 mark* deduction in the final valuation.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation step** — KTU examiners reward the "what does the answer mean" sentence for the final mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]

**(a)** *Formulate the Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) model for a supply chain network design problem involving facility location, capacity allocation, and customer assignment. Clearly define the sets, parameters, decision variables, objective function, and constraints.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sets (1 mark):**
- $I$ = set of potential facility sites
- $J$ = set of customer demand zones
- $K$ = set of products

**Parameters (1 mark):**
- $f_i$ = annual fixed cost of opening site $i$
- $c_{ijk}$ = per-unit shipping cost from $i$ to $j$ for product $k$
- $C_i$ = annual capacity of site $i$
- $D_{jk}$ = annual demand of product $k$ at customer $j$

**Decision Variables (1 mark):**
- $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ = 1 if site $i$ is opened
- $X_{ijk} \ge 0$ = units of product $k$ shipped from $i$ to $j$

**Objective Function — Minimize total cost (2 marks):**

$$
\min Z = \sum_{i \in I} f_i y_i + \sum_{i \in I} \sum_{j \in J} \sum_{k \in K} c_{ijk} X_{ijk}
$$

**Constraints (2 marks):**
- Demand satisfaction: $\sum_{i \in I} X_{ijk} = D_{jk}, \quad \forall j, k$
- Capacity limit: $\sum_{j \in J} \sum_{k \in K} X_{ijk} \le C_i y_i, \quad \forall i$
- Non-negativity: $X_{ijk} \ge 0$, $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

---

**(b)** *Explain the role of demand forecasting, scenario planning, and sensitivity analysis in supply chain network design. Why are these techniques particularly important when designing a long-term network?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Demand Forecasting (2.5 marks):**  
The network's *facility count, capacity, and transportation lanes* must match *future* demand. A forecast of demand by **zone, product, and growth rate** is the single most influential input. The forecasting horizon typically equals the *useful life* of the facility (10+ years for a plant). Methods used:
- **Time-series methods** (moving average, exponential smoothing) for stable categories.
- **Causal/regression models** for markets linked to GDP, population, or industrial output.
- **Bottom-up aggregation** of SKU-level forecasts up to category and zone levels.

**Scenario Planning (2.5 marks):**  
Because long-term demand is uncertain, the manager builds **2–4 contrasting scenarios** (e.g., high-growth, base, low-growth, disruption). For each scenario, the same MILP is re-solved. The *robust* network is the one that performs acceptably across all scenarios — it may not be optimal for any one, but is **less fragile** in the face of reality diverging from the forecast.

**Sensitivity Analysis (1.5 marks):**  
The solver's output is tested for sensitivity to:
- A 10–20 % change in demand volumes
- A 10 % change in fuel/lane rates
- A change in the fixed cost of opening a new plant
- A change in capacity (e.g., a 1-month commissioning delay)

The output is a list of *tornadoes* — bars showing which inputs most affect the total cost. These help the manager *focus* risk-management effort on the most influential parameters.

**Why critical for long-term network design (0.5 mark):**  
Network decisions are *irreversible in the short term* (5–10 year asset life). A 20 % demand miss can leave the firm with **idle capacity** (over-investment) or **stockouts and lost customers** (under-investment). Forecasting + scenario + sensitivity together give the manager a *risk-aware* decision.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write a generic essay on these techniques without tying them to *network design*. The examiner gives marks **only when the connection to facility location, capacity, or transportation is explicit**. Always end each point with: *"This matters for network design because…"*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Analysing and Designing the Supply Chain Network**

- ☐ **Three major decisions in network design:** Facility *role*, facility *location*, and *capacity allocation*.
- ☐ **Four hierarchical decision layers:** Strategy → Configuration → Service & Inventory → Operations.
- ☐ **Network design is strategic:** Horizon 3–10 years; affects **15–25 % of total chain cost**; capital-intensive; difficult to reverse.
- ☐ **Trade-off:** More facilities ⇒ higher facility + inventory cost, but lower transportation cost. The optimal is where the marginal saving equals the marginal increase.
- ☐ **Total cost function:** $TNC = FC + VC_{trans} + VC_{inv} + VC_{op}$.
- ☐ **Center of gravity formulas:** $x^{*} = \sum w_i x_i / \sum w_i$, $\;y^{*} = \sum w_i y_i / \sum w_i$. It is a *heuristic*, not an exact optimizer.
- ☐ **Load-Distance score:** $LD = \sum L_{ij} d_{ij}$ — used to compare alternative networks on a single comparable metric.
- ☐ **MILP formulation:** Three components — *sets, parameters, decision variables*; *objective function*; *constraints* (demand, capacity, flow conservation, binary).
- ☐ **Square-Root Rule:** Optimal number of warehouses scales as $k\sqrt{D}$ of demand.
- ☐ **Inputs to a network model:** Demand, facility cost, transportation cost, inventory cost, tariffs, lead times, mode characteristics.
- ☐ **Techniques to handle uncertainty:** Demand forecasting, scenario planning, sensitivity analysis.
- ☐ **Solver tools:** Gurobi, CPLEX, or open-source alternatives solve the MILP; the manager validates the result by simulation before implementation.
- ☐ **Validation step:** Always cross-check the optimizer's output with a *rough heuristic* (e.g., center of gravity) to ensure sanity.
- ☐ **Common exam trap:** Confusing *operational* decisions (daily) with *tactical* (quarterly) — know the time horizon, the cost impact, and the reversibility.
- ☐ **Unit check:** Always carry the unit through ($x^{*}$ in km, $w_i$ in tonnes, $Z$ in ₹/year) — KTU examiners explicitly mark for this.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Distribution Network Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Distribution Network Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Distribution Network Design (DND)** is the strategic process of determining the optimal configuration of a supply chain's logistical infrastructure — including the **number, location, and capacity** of facilities (plants, warehouses, distribution centers, and cross-docks) — along with the allocation of customer demand to these facilities and the selection of transportation modes, in order to achieve desired customer service levels at minimum total system cost.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the *Factors Affecting Distribution Network Design* are the exogenous (external) and endogenous (internal) variables that constrain, drive, or modify these strategic location-allocation decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> A distribution network design problem is essentially a **location-allocation problem** under uncertainty. The factors discussed below are the **input parameters** of this problem, and the objective function typically minimizes: **(Total Cost) = Facility Cost + Transportation Cost + Inventory Cost + Operating Cost − Service Level Benefit.**

## 1.2 The Six Pillars of DND Decisions

Every DND decision reduces to answering six core questions, each of which is influenced by specific factors:

| Decision Variable | Question to Answer | Primary Influencing Factors |
|---|---|---|
| **Facility Role** | What does each facility *do*? | Product characteristics, customer needs |
| **Facility Location** | *Where* should each facility be? | Infrastructure, costs, regulations |
| **Facility Capacity** | *How large* should each facility be? | Demand volume, inventory policy |
| **Market Allocation** | Which facility serves *which* customer? | Transportation cost, service level |
| **Transportation Mode** | How will goods *move*? | Distance, cost, speed, reliability |
| **Inventory Strategy** | *Where* will stock be held? | Lead time, demand variability |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bloodstream Analogy"

Imagine your **body's cardiovascular system** as a company's distribution network:

- The **heart** = Central warehouse / Plant (the pumping organ)
- The **arteries and capillaries** = Outbound transportation lanes (delivery vehicles)
- The **organs (liver, kidney)** = Regional distribution centers (processing & re-routing nodes)
- **Red blood cells** = The products flowing through the system
- **Body cells** = End customers demanding oxygen (products)
- **Blood viscosity** = Inventory holding (resistance to flow)
- **Heart rate & artery width** = Capacity of facilities and vehicles

Just as a cardiologist must consider **age, weight, cholesterol, stress, and genetics** before prescribing a treatment, a supply chain designer must weigh **customer factors, product factors, cost factors, infrastructure, competition, and regulations** before designing the network. Change one factor (e.g., customer demand doubles), and the entire network must be re-tuned — just like a heart responds to stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Most Part A (3-mark) questions on this topic ask students to *list* the factors. To score full marks, you must not merely list them — you must also state **how** each factor **influences the decision variable** (location, number, size, or allocation).

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Factors — The Master Map

The KTU 2024 syllabus clusters all factors affecting distribution network design into **six broad categories**:

1. **Customer-Related Factors** (demand level, demand variability, service requirements)
2. **Product-Related Factors** (SKU variety, value-density, perishability, substitutability)
3. **Cost-Related Factors** (facility, inventory, transportation, operating, labor)
4. **Infrastructure & Operational Factors** (transportation networks, labor, IT systems)
5. **Competitive & Strategic Factors** (competitor location, strategic fit)
6. **Government, Regulatory & Macro Factors** (taxes, trade zones, environmental laws, geopolitical risk)

Each category will be unpacked in subsequent sections.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Six Factor Categories

### Category A — Customer-Related Factors

These factors define the **demand side** of the network design problem.

**A1. Demand Level (Volume)**
- High aggregate demand → favors **fewer, larger, centralized facilities** to capture economies of scale.
- Low aggregate demand → favors **many, small, decentralized facilities** to reduce outbound transportation cost and improve response time.

**A2. Demand Variability (Uncertainty)**
- High variability (e.g., fashion, seasonal goods) → requires **safety stock buffers**, often necessitating more facilities or larger inventory capacity.
- Low variability (e.g., cement, salt) → permits lean, centralized inventory.

**A3. Customer Density & Geographic Dispersion**
- Clustered customers → **single regional DC** suffices.
- Dispersed customers → multiple DCs required to maintain service-level promises.

**A4. Service-Level Requirements (Response Time / Fill Rate)**
- Aggressive promises such as **next-day delivery** or **98% fill rate** force facility locations *closer* to customers.
- Lower service expectations → enables cheaper, remote centralization.

**A5. Customer-Channel Preferences**
- B2B customers may tolerate longer lead times; B2C e-commerce customers demand **fast, free shipping** — driving micro-fulfillment centers.

---

### Category B — Product-Related Factors

**B1. SKU Variety (Product Assortment)**
- Large assortment → requires facilities capable of handling **wider inventory mix** and **slotting complexity**.
- Often resolved by building *specialty* facilities.

**B2. Value-Density (Value-to-Weight Ratio)**
$$ \text{Value-Density} = \frac{\text{Product Selling Price}}{\text{Product Weight (kg)}} \quad \left[ \frac{\$}{\text{kg}} \right] $$

- **High value-density** (jewelry, electronics) → long-distance shipping is justified; centralized inventory acceptable.
- **Low value-density** (sand, beverages) → shipping is costly per unit value; decentralized production/storage is preferred.

**B3. Perishability & Shelf Life**
- Short shelf-life products (dairy, fresh produce, pharmaceuticals) → require **fast, short-haul networks**; multiple cold-chain DCs near consumption zones.

**B4. Substitutability & Product Interrelationships**
- Highly substitutable products → customer will switch if out-of-stock → network must guarantee high fill rate.
- **Complementary products** must be co-located to enable single-stop fulfillment.

**B5. Special Handling Needs**
- Hazmat, oversized items, fragile goods → impose constraints on facility design (ventilation, dock-door count, racking).

---

### Category C — Cost-Related Factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cost Trade-Off Triangle:** Any network design decision is a balance between **Inventory**, **Transportation**, and **Facility** costs. Improving one worsens the others. This is the **central economic intuition** behind every DND factor.

**C1. Facility (Fixed) Costs**
- Land cost, construction, equipment, licensing.
- Influences *number* and *size* of facilities.

**C2. Inventory Holding Costs**
- Capital cost, obsolescence, insurance, warehousing.
- Higher holding cost → favors fewer facilities and pooled inventory (risk pooling effect).

**C3. Inbound vs. Outbound Transportation Costs**
- Asymmetric transport costs alter optimal location dramatically.
- A plant near raw-material sources (e.g., steel mill near iron-ore mine) but far from markets → favored if outbound cost $\ll$ inbound cost.

**C4. Labor & Operating Costs**
- Wage rates, productivity, availability of skilled workforce.
- Lead to **off-shoring** or **near-shoring** decisions.

**C5. Order Processing / IT Costs**
- Lower with fewer, larger facilities.

---

### Category D — Infrastructure & Operational Factors

- Quality of road/rail/port/airport connectivity
- Telecommunications & IT infrastructure (ERP/WMS readiness)
- Energy availability (electricity, fuel)
- Workforce availability and skill level
- Availability of reliable suppliers

---

### Category E — Competitive & Strategic Factors

- **Competitor facility locations** — may force strategic pre-positioning.
- **Strategic fit** with corporate long-term vision (offensive vs. defensive expansion).
- **Channel partner preferences** — exclusive dealerships may dictate regional inventory placement.

---

### Category F — Government, Regulatory & Macro Factors

- **Tax incentives** (SEZs, GST zones, sales-tax nexus)
- **Trade barriers & tariffs** (import duties → favors local manufacturing)
- **Environmental regulations** (emissions limits, packaging laws)
- **Political stability and geopolitical risk**
- **Currency exchange rates** for multi-country networks

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Logistics Cost (TLC)** | $TLC = F + T + I + O$ | Sum of Facility, Transport, Inventory, Operating cost | Currency per period |
| **Facility (Fixed) Cost** | $F = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot y_i$ | $f_i$ = fixed cost of opening facility $i$, $y_i \in \{0,1\}$ | $n$ = number of candidate facilities |
| **Transportation Cost** | $T = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{m} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ = unit transport cost from $i$ to $j$, $x_{ij}$ = flow | Currency per unit × flow |
| **Inventory Holding Cost** | $I = h \cdot Q$ | $h$ = holding cost per unit per period, $Q$ = average stock | Currency per period |
| **Safety Stock (with variability)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | $z$ = service-level factor, $\sigma_{LT}$ = std. dev. of lead-time demand | Units |
| **Reorder Point** | $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ | $d$ = avg daily demand, $L$ = lead time in days | Units |
| **Value-Density** | $VD = \dfrac{P}{W}$ | $P$ = selling price, $W$ = weight | $\$/\text{kg}$ |
| **Stock-out Probability** | $\beta = 1 - \dfrac{CSL}{100}$ | $CSL$ = cycle service level | Dimensionless |
| **Number of DCs Heuristic (Square-Root Rule)** | $N_{DC} \approx N_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{\text{inv}}}{C_{\text{trans}}}}$ | Inventory vs. transport cost trade-off | $N_0$ = baseline count |
| **Warehouse Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{Q_{\text{annual}}}{D_{\text{oper}} \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}}$ | $Q$ = annual quantity, $D_{\text{oper}}$ = operating days, $t$ = cycle time | Units per day |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice that **none of the above formulas explicitly contain the factors** themselves (e.g., demand variability, perishability). Instead, the factors *modify the input parameters* (demand $d$, holding cost $h$, transport cost $c_{ij}$, service-level factor $z$). This is exactly how you should frame your answer in KTU answers: *factors → parameters → objective function*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Total Logistics Cost Function as a Decision Framework

Every factor affecting distribution network design ultimately enters the **Total Logistics Cost (TLC)** objective function. Let us derive it step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define the Decision Variables

Let:
- $n$ = number of candidate facility locations (warehouses / DCs)
- $m$ = number of customer zones
- $y_i$ = binary variable: $y_i = 1$ if facility $i$ is opened, $0$ otherwise
- $x_{ij}$ = fraction of demand from customer $j$ served by facility $i$

### Step 2: Identify the Cost Components Influenced by DND Factors

A network design decision triggers four cost streams:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Facility Cost} \quad F &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot y_i \\[4pt]
\text{Transportation Cost} \quad T &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} c_{ij} \cdot D_j \cdot x_{ij} \\[4pt]
\text{Inventory Holding Cost} \quad I &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i \cdot Q_i \\[4pt]
\text{Operating Cost} \quad O &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} o_i \cdot Q_i
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $f_i$ = fixed cost of opening facility $i$ (influenced by **infrastructure, regulatory, labor** factors)
- $c_{ij}$ = unit transport cost from $i$ to $j$ (influenced by **infrastructure, distance, fuel price**)
- $D_j$ = demand at customer zone $j$ (the **customer-related factor**)
- $h_i$ = inventory holding cost per unit at facility $i$ (influenced by **product characteristics, regional taxes**)
- $Q_i$ = average inventory held at facility $i$ (influenced by **demand variability, lead time**)
- $o_i$ = unit operating cost at $i$ (influenced by **labor, energy, regulations**)

### Step 3: Aggregate the Total Cost

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } TLC &= F + T + I + O \\[6pt]
\text{Minimize } TLC &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot y_i \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} c_{ij} \cdot D_j \cdot x_{ij} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i \cdot Q_i \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} o_i \cdot Q_i
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **objective function**. Every factor discussed in Section 2 enters by altering the *coefficients* ( $f_i$, $c_{ij}$, $h_i$, $o_i$, $D_j$, $Q_i$ ).

### Step 4: Apply the Constraints

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Supply Constraint at Facility } i &: \quad \sum_{j=1}^{m} D_j \cdot x_{ij} \le C_i \cdot y_i \\[4pt]
\text{Demand Fulfillment at Customer } j &: \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \\[4pt]
\text{Non-negativity} &: \quad x_{ij} \ge 0 \\[4pt]
\text{Binary} &: \quad y_i \in \{0, 1\}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $C_i$ = capacity of facility $i$ (influenced by **land cost, zoning regulations, investment**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: How the Square-Root Inventory Rule Quantifies the Trade-off

A classic KTU-style problem asks: *If transportation cost doubles, how many facilities should we have?* The Square-Root Rule answers it.

### Step 1: Set Up the Baseline

Assume the baseline network has $N_0$ identical distribution centers, each holding identical safety stock $SS$ to meet a target Cycle Service Level $CSL$.

### Step 2: Total Safety Stock in Baseline

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Safety Stock}_0 &= N_0 \cdot SS_0 \\[4pt]
SS_0 &= z \cdot \sigma_{LT,0} = z \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Apply Risk Pooling

When the same $N_0$ DCs are consolidated into $N$ fewer DCs, **risk pooling** reduces the pooled safety stock. Under the assumption of independent demand across customers served by a single DC:

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{\text{pooled}} &= z \cdot \sigma_{LT,\text{pooled}} = z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L \cdot \sigma_d^2 \cdot D_{\text{total}}}{D_{\text{per DC}}}}
\end{aligned}$$

For $N$ DCs each serving $D_{\text{total}}/N$ demand:

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{\text{pooled}} &= z \cdot \sqrt{N \cdot L \cdot \sigma_d^2 / N} = z \cdot \sqrt{N} \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — careful: **consolidation *increases* safety stock per facility but decreases the *number* of facilities.** Let us re-derive properly.

If we move from $N_0$ facilities to $N$ facilities (consolidation), each facility now serves $N_0/N$ times as many customers. Pooled variance grows as the *square root* of customer count, while total inventory at the system level falls as $1/\sqrt{N_0/N}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{\text{system}} &= SS_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{N_0}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Equate the Holding Cost Savings to the Transportation Cost Increase

Holding cost savings $\Delta H$ from pooling:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &= h \cdot SS_0 \cdot \left(1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{N_0}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Transportation cost increase $\Delta T$ from longer outbound hauls:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_0}{N} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $\Delta H = \Delta T$ at the optimum:

$$\begin{aligned}
h \cdot SS_0 \cdot \left(1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{N_0}}\right) &= T_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_0}{N} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Solving in the limit where $N/N_0$ is small and using first-order approximations yields the classical **Square-Root Rule**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,N \;\approx\; N_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{\text{inv}}}{C_{\text{trans}}}}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation (KTU exam-ready):**
- If inventory holding cost is **high**, $\sqrt{C_{\text{inv}}/C_{\text{trans}}}$ is large → fewer, larger DCs are optimal.
- If transportation cost is **high**, the ratio is small → many small DCs are optimal.
- This formula **encapsulates the trade-off** between inventory and transportation costs — and both are *themselves* functions of the six factor categories.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A firm currently operates **9** regional warehouses across Kerala. The aggregate annual inventory holding cost is **₹18 crore** and the aggregate transportation cost is **₹2 crore**. Management is considering a network redesign. Compute the **new optimal number of warehouses** if a new road-tax policy doubles transportation cost to ₹4 crore while inventory cost drops by 50% to ₹9 crore.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Identify the baseline parameters.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_0 &= 9 \text{ warehouses} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{inv, baseline}} &= ₹18 \text{ crore} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{trans, baseline}} &= ₹2 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the new cost parameters.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{inv, new}} &= ₹18 \times 0.50 = ₹9 \text{ crore} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{trans, new}} &= ₹2 \times 2.00 = ₹4 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Square-Root Rule.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{new}} &= N_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{\text{inv, new}}}{C_{\text{trans, new}}}} \\[6pt]
N_{\text{new}} &= 9 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{9}{4}} \\[6pt]
N_{\text{new}} &= 9 \cdot \sqrt{2.25} \\[6pt]
N_{\text{new}} &= 9 \cdot 1.5 \\[6pt]
\boxed{\,N_{\text{new}} &= 13.5 \,\rightarrow\, \text{round to } 14 \text{ warehouses}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Managerial Interpretation.**
Even though transportation cost *doubled* (favoring consolidation), the simultaneous **50% drop in inventory cost** (from better warehouse automation under the new policy) favors **decentralization**. Hence, the optimal number of warehouses actually *increases* from 9 to 14. This illustrates how factors interact non-trivially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This is exactly the kind of "calculate and interpret" question that appears in Part B (14-mark) questions. Full marks require: (i) the numerical answer, (ii) the formula quoted, (iii) one or two sentences explaining the *real-world reason* behind the result.

---

## 3.4 The Factor-to-Decision Mapping Matrix

The following analytical matrix maps each factor directly to the network-design decision it influences.

| Factor Category | Specific Factor | DND Decision Affected | Direction of Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Customer | High demand volume | Number of facilities | ↓ Number (consolidate) |
| Customer | High demand variability | Inventory capacity | ↑ Safety stock at each DC |
| Customer | High service-level target | Facility location | Closer to customer |
| Product | High value-density | Facility location | Centralized (long shipping OK) |
| Product | Low shelf-life | Facility location | Decentralized (short shipping) |
| Product | High SKU variety | Facility role | Specialized DCs |
| Cost | High facility cost | Number of facilities | ↓ Number |
| Cost | High inventory cost | Number of facilities | ↓ Number |
| Cost | High outbound transport cost | Number of facilities | ↑ Number |
| Infrastructure | Poor road network | Facility location | Near accessible hubs |
| Regulatory | Tax-free zone available | Facility location | Inside the zone |
| Strategic | Competitor near customer | Facility location | Pre-empt (closer to customer) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Six Factor Categories and Their Influence Pathways

```mermaid

    F2[Product Related Factors]:::prod --> DND
    F3[Cost Related Factors]:::cost --> DND

    F5[Competitive Factors]:::comp --> DND

    DND --> DV1[Facility Location]

    DND --> DV3[Facility Capacity]
    DND --> DV4[Market Allocation]
    DND --> DV5[Transport Mode]
    DND --> DV6[Inventory Strategy]
    DV1 --> OBJ[Minimize Total Logistics Cost]
    DV2 --> OBJ
    DV3 --> OBJ
    DV4 --> OBJ
    DV5 --> OBJ
    DV6 --> OBJ
    OBJ --> OUT[Optimal Network Configuration]

    classDef prod fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#2E8B57,color:#000

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Trade-off Triangle (Cost Triangle)

flowchart LR

    B --- C[Facility Cost]:::fac
    C --- A
    A -->|FACTOR High Inventory Cost| D[Centralize Fewer DCs]:::dec
    B -->|FACTOR High Transport Cost| E[Decentralize More DCs]:::dec
    C -->|FACTOR High Facility Cost| F[Reduce Number of DCs]:::dec
    D --> G[Optimal Network]:::goal
    E --> G
    F --> G
    classDef inv fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef fac fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#fff
    classDef dec fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Customer-Factor Influence Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CR[Customer Related Factors]:::root --> DL[Demand Level]
    CR --> DV[Demand Variability]
    CR --> CD[Customer Density]
    CR --> SL[Service Level Requirements]
    CR --> CH[Channel Preferences]
    DL --> DEC1[Decide Number of DCs]:::dec
    DV --> DEC2[Decide Safety Stock]:::dec
    CD --> DEC3[Decide Geographic Spread]:::dec
    SL --> DEC4[Decide Facility Location Proximity]:::dec
    CH --> DEC5[Decide Micro vs Regional DCs]:::dec
    classDef root fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Product Factors]:::root --> V[Value Density]
    P --> S[Shelf Life]
    P --> K[SKU Variety]
    P --> H[Special Handling]
    V --> R1{VD High Centralize}:::rule
    V --> R2{VD Low Decentralize}:::rule
    S --> R3{Short Shelf Life Many Small DCs Near Market}:::rule
    K --> R4{Wide Variety Specialty or Full Line DCs}:::rule
    H --> R5{Special Facilities With Compliance}:::rule
    classDef root fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#2E8B57,color:#000
    classDef rule fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — How Factors Feed into the Design Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT[INPUT LAYER Six Factor Categories] --> PROCFAC[Process Factor Data]
    INPUT --> PROCVAL[Assign Parameter Values]
    PROCFAC --> ESTCOST[Estimate Cost Coefficients f i c ij h i]
    PROCVAL --> ESTDEM[Estimate Demand d j and Variability sigma d]
    ESTCOST --> OBJFN[Build Objective Function Min TLC]
    ESTDEM --> OBJFN
    OBJFN --> SOLVER[Solver Location Allocation Heuristic]
    SOLVER --> OUTPUT1[Optimal Number of Facilities]
    SOLVER --> OUTPUT2[Optimal Facility Locations]
    SOLVER --> OUTPUT3[Optimal Market Assignments]
    SOLVER --> OUTPUT4[Optimal Inventory Levels]
    OUTPUT1 --> FINAL[Final Network Configuration]
    OUTPUT2 --> FINAL
    OUTPUT3 --> FINAL
    OUTPUT4 --> FINAL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for Students:**
> - The first diagram shows the **big-picture mapping** from factors to design decisions.
> - The second diagram illustrates the **central economic trade-off** in any DND problem.
> - The third and fourth diagrams drill into **customer** and **product** factors.
> - The fifth diagram is a **functional flow** of how a real-world design algorithm processes the factors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any four factors affecting distribution network design.**

**Model Answer:**
Distribution network design decisions are influenced by multiple internal and external factors. Four key factors are:

1. **Customer-related factors** such as demand level, demand variability, geographic dispersion of customers, and required service level. High demand dispersion and tight service-level promises force firms to open more facilities closer to customers.

2. **Product-related factors** such as value-density, shelf life, and SKU variety. Perishable or low-value-density products require decentralized networks, whereas high-value products tolerate long-distance shipping from centralized warehouses.

3. **Cost-related factors** such as facility cost, inventory holding cost, and transportation cost. The relative magnitudes of these cost components determine whether the network should be centralized or decentralized.

4. **Infrastructure-related factors** such as road/rail connectivity, port access, IT systems, and labor availability. These factors constrain the feasible set of facility locations.

*(Any 4 well-explained factors — 3 marks; each factor: 0.5 listing + 0.25 explanation = 0.75 marks × 4)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of value-density. How does it affect the choice between a centralized and decentralized distribution network?**

**Model Answer:**
Value-density is defined as the ratio of a product's selling price to its physical weight or volume:

$$ VD = \frac{P}{W} \quad \left[\frac{\$}{\text{kg}}\right] $$

**Effect on Network Design:**
- Products with **high value-density** (e.g., jewellery, electronics, pharmaceuticals) have a high cost-to-weight ratio, making **transportation cost negligible** compared to inventory holding cost. This justifies a **centralized network** with fewer facilities, pooled inventory, and longer shipping distances.

- Products with **low value-density** (e.g., cement, beverages, sand) incur a high transportation cost per unit value. It is therefore economical to **decentralize** the network by opening many small warehouses near consumption zones, minimizing shipping distance.

Thus, value-density is a critical product-related factor that reverses the optimal network configuration depending on its magnitude.

*(Concept 1 mark + formula 1 mark + application 1 mark = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions offer **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below with full solutions.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **customer-related factors** that influence the design of a distribution network. Illustrate your answer with the example of an e-commerce grocery company operating in Kerala. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **cost-related factors** in distribution network design. Using the **Square-Root Rule**, derive and explain how the optimal number of facilities changes with a change in the relative magnitude of inventory and transportation costs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Customer-related factors represent the *demand side* of the distribution network design problem. They directly determine the *number, location, and capacity* of facilities needed to satisfy customer expectations economically.

**Detailed Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Demand Level (Volume):**
   - High aggregate demand favours centralization (one large DC serves many customers using economies of scale).
   - Low demand per customer zone favours many small local facilities.
   - *Example:* An e-commerce grocery firm in Kerala (e.g., based in Kochi) with high overall order volume may concentrate inventory in a single large fulfillment center near the city, despite serving customers across the state.

2. **Demand Variability:**
   - Seasonal spikes (Onam, Vishu) require safety stock buffers; the firm may need to lease temporary warehouses during festive seasons.
   - High variability increases the *safety stock* at each DC, raising holding cost and forcing a trade-off with facility count.

3. **Customer Density and Geographic Dispersion:**
   - Customers concentrated in Kochi, Trivandrum, and Calicut → firm can place regional DCs near these clusters.
   - Customers dispersed in Wayanad, Idukki, and Pathanamthitta → firm must use **micro-fulfillment centers** or partner with local kirana stores for last-mile delivery.

4. **Service-Level Requirements:**
   - Promises such as **"30-minute grocery delivery"** in urban areas force the firm to locate small warehouses within every 5 km radius in cities.
   - In rural areas where 24-hour delivery is acceptable, a single regional DC may suffice.

5. **Channel Preferences:**
   - Customers preferring online ordering with home delivery → need warehouse near residential clusters.
   - Customers preferring click-and-collect → need pickup points distributed across malls and metro stations.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
For a Kerala e-commerce grocery company, the optimal network is a **hybrid**: a central fulfillment warehouse in Kochi for bulk storage and inventory pooling, supplemented by **micro-fulfillment centers** in major cities to meet rapid-delivery promises.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Step 1: Introduction to Cost Factors (2 marks):**
The three primary cost components in a distribution network are:
- **Facility (fixed) cost** $F$
- **Transportation cost** $T$
- **Inventory holding cost** $I$

These costs **trade-off against each other**: improving one worsens the others. The optimal network balances all three.

**Step 2: Derivation of the Square-Root Rule (3 marks):**

Let $N_0$ = baseline number of DCs; $C_{\text{inv}}$ = annual inventory holding cost; $C_{\text{trans}}$ = annual transportation cost.

Assume each DC holds the same safety stock $SS_0$, and the firm wishes to find a new count $N$ that minimizes total cost. Total inventory carrying cost decreases with consolidation due to **risk pooling**:

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{\text{system}}(N) &= SS_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{N_0}}
\end{aligned}$$

Total transportation cost increases approximately inversely with the number of DCs (fewer DCs = longer average hauls):

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{trans}}(N) &\approx C_{\text{trans}}(N_0) \cdot \dfrac{N_0}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

Setting total cost derivative to zero yields the optimum:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{optimal}} \;\approx\; N_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{\text{inv}}}{C_{\text{trans}}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation (2 marks):**
- If **inventory cost rises** (e.g., due to higher capital cost or increased demand variability requiring more safety stock), the ratio $\sqrt{C_{\text{inv}}/C_{\text{trans}}}$ rises → the firm should **reduce the number of DCs** (consolidate inventory).
- If **transportation cost rises** (e.g., due to higher diesel prices or fuel surcharges), the ratio falls → the firm should **increase the number of DCs** (decentralize) to shorten delivery distances.
- This rule is a precise formalization of the **inventory-vs-transportation trade-off**, which is the central economic driver of DND.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Cost-related factors are not independent. They enter the design problem as trade-off parameters. The Square-Root Rule quantifies the trade-off and serves as a quick heuristic for top-management decisions on facility count.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** "Product-related factors are often the dominant drivers of distribution network design decisions." Critically examine this statement with reference to **value-density, perishability, and SKU variety**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of **government, regulatory, and macro-economic factors** in distribution network design. How do SEZs, GST, and environmental regulations influence facility location choices in India? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Product characteristics — particularly **value-density**, **perishability**, and **SKU variety** — often dictate the very architecture of a distribution network. The statement is largely correct, though other factors (customer demand, cost) also play significant roles.

**Analysis of Each Factor (2 marks each = 6 marks):**

1. **Value-Density $VD = P/W$:**
   - *High VD products* (electronics, pharmaceuticals, jewellery): A single shipment of small weight carries enormous monetary value. Shipping cost is negligible. Firms like Apple India maintain only **one or two central warehouses** for the entire country, using air freight for replenishment.
   - *Low VD products* (cement, packaged water, soft drinks): Weight dominates. Firms like ACC Cement or Coca-Cola operate **dense, regionally distributed plants and warehouses** to limit transportation cost per ton.
   - *Conclusion:* Value-density is a *dominant* driver in industries where it varies significantly across product lines.

2. **Perishability:**
   - Products with short shelf life (dairy, seafood, fresh produce) require extremely short, often *less than 24-hour*, delivery cycles.
   - Firms like **Milma (Kerala Co-operative Milk Marketing Federation)** operate a dense network of chilling centers and local distribution depots across every district.
   - A central warehouse strategy would *fail* for perishables, regardless of cost savings. Hence perishability is a *binding constraint*, not merely an influencing factor.

3. **SKU Variety:**
   - A high SKU count (e.g., 100,000+ SKUs for a hypermarket chain) demands large warehouses with extensive racking, slotting, and pick-pack systems.
   - This favours **centralized full-line DCs** rather than small local stores, because the cost of replicating the assortment across many locations is prohibitive.
   - Conversely, firms with narrow assortments (e.g., Bata shoes) can use **specialty regional DCs**.

**Critical Examination (1 mark):**
While product factors often *dominate*, they are not the *only* drivers. For non-perishable, mid-value-density products with stable demand, **cost-related factors** (labor, transport infrastructure) often dominate the design. The statement is therefore *true in context* but not universally true.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Government policies, tax structures, and macro-economic conditions impose binding constraints on facility location. They interact with cost factors but are often exogenous and uncontrollable.

**Detailed Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Special Economic Zones (SEZs) and Tax Incentives:**
   - Firms locate inside SEZs to benefit from **income-tax holidays**, **duty-free imports of capital goods**, and **export-profit exemptions**.
   - Example: Many electronics and pharma firms have located warehouses in **Sri City (Andhra Pradesh)**, **Cochin SEZ**, and **Noida SEZs**.
   - The SEZ decision affects *facility location* directly and often overrides pure cost-minimization.

2. **GST (Goods and Services Tax) Implications:**
   - GST has reduced multiple cascading taxes but introduced the concept of **Input Tax Credit**, which depends on proper documentation at every stock-keeping point.
   - Warehouses holding inventory for more than a defined period may attract **tax implications** in some states.
   - This affects **facility count and inventory strategy**: firms prefer centralized warehouses to avoid multi-state compliance overhead.

3. **Environmental Regulations:**
   - The **Environment Protection Act, 1986**, and state Pollution Control Board rules restrict the location of warehouses for hazardous chemicals, e-cigarettes, and pharma waste.
   - Cold-chain warehouses for pharma require specific energy-efficiency standards (BEE ratings) influencing the **type** and **size** of facility.

4. **Other Macro Factors:**
   - Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) policies affect whether a multinational can own warehouses in India or must use 3PL partners.
   - Geopolitical tensions and trade tariffs (e.g., post-Galwan import restrictions) drive firms to maintain **buffer inventory** and **dual sourcing**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Government, regulatory, and macro factors are *exogenous constraints* that can make otherwise cost-optimal locations infeasible. Effective DND must explicitly model these constraints alongside cost and demand parameters. In India, the SEZ-GST-Environmental triangle is the most influential regulatory cluster for distribution network design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Listing without explaining:** Writing "Customer factors" and stopping. You *must* explain *how* it affects the decision (location, number, size, or allocation). Marks are deducted for definitions without application.
> 2. **Confusing "factors" with "decisions":** Students often write about facility location itself instead of factors affecting facility location. The question asks for *factors*, not the decisions themselves.
> 3. **Missing the trade-off:** When cost factors are discussed, examiners expect the **inventory–transportation–facility triangle** to be explicitly mentioned. Omitting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the formula:** In numerical questions (Square-Root Rule type), *quoting the formula correctly* earns at least 1 mark even if subsequent calculation has a small error.
> 5. **No concluding sentence:** Always close with a one-line summary statement connecting back to the question's premise.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Definition:** Distribution Network Design is a strategic location-allocation problem that determines the *number*, *location*, *capacity*, and *assignment* of facilities and the *transportation mode* to satisfy demand at minimum cost.
- [ ] **Six Factor Categories:** Customer, Product, Cost, Infrastructure, Competitive, Regulatory/Macro — remember the mnemonic **C-P-C-I-C-R** (Customer, Product, Cost, Infrastructure, Competitive, Regulatory).
- [ ] **Central Economic Trade-off:** Inventory ↔ Transportation ↔ Facility. Any DND factor ultimately alters one of these three costs.
- [ ] **Value-Density Formula:** $VD = P/W$ — High VD ⇒ Centralize; Low VD ⇒ Decentralize.
- [ ] **Safety Stock Formula:** $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ — service-level factor $z$ comes from CSL via standard normal table.
- [ ] **Reorder Point:** $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$.
- [ ] **Square-Root Rule:** $N \approx N_0 \cdot \sqrt{C_{\text{inv}}/C_{\text{trans}}}$ — used for quick estimation of optimal facility count.
- [ ] **Perishability** forces decentralization, regardless of cost savings.
- [ ] **High demand variability** raises safety stock → influences facility *capacity* and *number*.
- [ ] **Tight service-level promises** force facilities *closer* to customers → more, smaller DCs.
- [ ] **High outbound transportation cost** → decentralize; **High inventory cost** → centralize.
- [ ] **Risk Pooling** consolidates safety stock as $\sqrt{N/N_0}$.
- [ ] **Government factors:** SEZs, GST, environmental rules are *binding constraints*, not just cost parameters.
- [ ] **Remember to draw or describe at least one diagram** in long-answer questions — it earns easy marks.
- [ ] **Always link factor → decision → cost impact** in your answers. The "factor-decision-cost" chain is what examiners look for.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Sentence Memory Aid:**
> *Distribution network design is shaped by factors that act through the cost triangle (Inventory–Transport–Facility), and the art of design lies in balancing this triangle under the binding constraints of customer service and product characteristics.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Network Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of Network Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: ROLE OF NETWORK DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supply Chain Network Design (SCND)** is the strategic process of configuring the entire chain of facilities, suppliers, warehouses, distribution centers, and retail outlets — along with the associated inventory levels, transportation lanes, and information flows — to minimize total landed cost while achieving the desired customer service level.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PEMET418), network design is treated as the **highest-leverage strategic decision** in supply chain management because it determines the physical, financial, and operational footprint of a firm for the next **5 to 10 years**. A poor network design locks the company into high costs, slow response, and poor resilience; a strong network design creates a durable competitive advantage.

The network design decision typically answers four questions:

1. **WHERE** should each facility be located? (plants, warehouses, DCs)
2. **WHAT** role should each facility perform? (source, make, store, cross-dock, return)
3. **WHICH** markets should each facility serve? (allocation of customers)
4. **HOW** much inventory and capacity should each facility hold?

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric Box**
> - **Service Level Target:** typically **95%** to **98%** (Type-1 / Type-2 fill rate)
> - **Planning Horizon:** **3 to 10 years** (strategic horizon)
> - **Cost of a wrong decision:** **10% to 40%** of total supply chain operating cost (Chopra & Meindl benchmark)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body as a Supply Chain

Think of your **body** as a company:
- Your **heart, lungs, and liver** are *facilities* (each performs a specific role).
- Your **arteries and veins** are *transportation lanes* carrying oxygen and nutrients.
- Your **blood cells and glucose reserves** are *inventory* held in different organs.
- Your **nervous system** is the *information flow*.

If your **heart is in the wrong place** (poor facility location) or your **arteries are too narrow** (poor transportation design), even a healthy person gets tired quickly. The body still "works", but at a high metabolic cost. Similarly, a company can have excellent products and great employees, but if the **supply chain network is mis-designed**, every shipment will be expensive and slow. **Network design = the anatomy of the company.**

### 1.3 Real-World Examples (KTU Board Favourite)

| Company | Network Design Choice | Strategic Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Amazon** | 175+ Fulfillment Centers + sortation hubs + last-mile stations | 1–2 day delivery, dominant e-commerce share |
| **Apple** | Single mega-supplier (Foxconn, China) + global air hubs | High margin, but high geopolitical risk |
| **Walmart** | 150+ regional DCs serving stores within 250 miles | Lowest cost retail in the world |
| **Zara (Inditex)** | Centralized DCs in Spain + air freight to stores | Fast fashion leader (15-day cycle) |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Why is network design called a strategic decision?"* — The answer is the **long-term cost commitment**, the **difficulty of reversal**, and the **cascading effect on inventory, transportation, and information decisions**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Layers of Supply Chain Decisions

Network design sits at the top of the **decision hierarchy**. Every decision below it must conform to the network chosen at the top.

| Decision Layer | Time Horizon | Cost of Reversal | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic (Network Design)** | **3–10 years** | **Very High** | Plant/DC location, capacity, sourcing country |
| **Tactical | 3–12 months | Medium | Inventory policy, lot sizes, supplier contracts |
| **Operational | Daily/Weekly | Low | Order fulfillment, truck loading, scheduling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Because network design fixes the *physical skeleton* of the supply chain, all subsequent tactical and operational decisions (EOQ, reorder points, routing) are **constrained** by the network. Hence network design is the **highest-leverage** decision in the entire course.

## 2.2 The Four Key Roles of Network Design

1. **Cost Minimization Role** — Facility fixed costs, transportation costs, and inventory carrying costs add up to the *Total Landed Cost*. Network design seeks to minimize:

$$TC = F_{fixed} + C_{transport} + C_{inventory} + C_{operating}$$

2. **Service Level Enablement Role** — A network must be physically close enough to customers to deliver within the promised time. Network design determines the **maximum achievable fill rate** and **response time**.

3. **Strategic Capability Role** — The network defines the firm's ability to enter new markets, hedge geopolitical risk, support new product launches, and absorb demand volatility.

4. **Risk and Resilience Role** — Modern networks must balance **efficiency** (single global plant) with **resilience** (multiple regional plants). The post-COVID shift toward **regionalization** is a network design response.

## 2.3 Key Factors Influencing Network Design

- **Strategic factors:** competitive strategy, product lifecycle stage, customer service requirements.
- **Technological factors:** production technology scale, perishability, automation maturity.
- **Macroeconomic factors:** tariffs, exchange rates, tax incentives, free trade zones.
- **Logistics factors:** transportation cost per ton-mile, warehouse automation cost, port/airport capacity.
- **Demand factors:** demand variability, customer density, growth rate, seasonality.
- **Competitive factors:** competitor location and pricing.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU PEMET418 examinations.

**Table 2.1 — Core Network Design Equations**

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Relationship | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $TC$ | Total Network Cost | $TC \;=\; F \;+\; \alpha \cdot T \cdot d$ | INR / year |
| $F$ | Fixed facility cost | $F \;=\; \sum_{i \in \text{open}} f_i$ | INR / year |
| $T$ | Transportation cost rate | $T$ (per ton-km) | INR / ton-km |
| $d$ | Weighted distance | $d \;=\; \sum_{j} w_j \cdot \text{dist}(i,j)$ | km |
| $\alpha$ | Tonnage shipped | $\alpha$ | tons / year |
| $C_{inv}$ | Annual inventory carrying cost | $C_{inv} \;=\; h \cdot \bar{I}$ | INR / year |
| $h$ | Holding cost per unit per year | $h \;=\; i \cdot C_u$ | INR / unit-yr |
| $C_u$ | Unit cost | input | INR |
| $i$ | Holding rate | $0.20$ to $0.30$ typical | dimensionless |
| $\bar{I}$ | Average inventory | $\bar{I} \;\approx\; SS \;+\; Q/2$ | units |
| $z$ | Safety stock z-score | $z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | units |
| $SS$ | Safety stock | $SS \;=\; z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | units |
| $R$ | Service level (Type-2) | $R \;\geq\; 1 - \dfrac{Q \cdot P(X>0)}{D}$ | ratio |
| $NDC$ | # of DCs to evaluate | $\sqrt{\dfrac{A}{B}}$ heuristic | count |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When you write a markdown table, the absolute-value symbol like $\vert x \vert$ is fine in math, but never write a *plain* `|x|` in a table row — it will break the column delimiter. Use **\vert** or **\mid** inside LaTeX instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

In actual industry, these equations are encoded inside **Supply Chain Design Software** such as **SAP IBP**, **LLamasoft (now Coupa)**, **o9 Solutions**, and **Blue Yonder**. The manager's role is to: (a) input the data, (b) interpret the output, (c) make the strategic call. The KTU syllabus expects you to **understand the math**, not run the software.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Network Total Cost Function

We derive the **canonical network design cost function** used in every facility location problem (CFLP / UFLP family). This derivation is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Identify the cost components

A supply chain network has exactly **four** cost buckets:

1. **Fixed facility cost** $f_i$ — paid once per year for every facility $i$ that is opened.
2. **Variable operating cost** $g_i$ — depends on throughput of facility $i$.
3. **Transportation cost** $c_{ij}$ — cost of moving one unit from facility $i$ to customer $j$.
4. **Inventory carrying cost** $h_j$ — cost of holding one unit of safety stock at customer $j$ for one year.

### Step 2 — Define the decision variables

We define two binary/integer variables:

$$
y_i = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if facility } i \text{ is opened} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
x_{ij} = \text{quantity shipped from facility } i \text{ to customer } j
$$

### Step 3 — Write the cost components in LaTeX form

**Fixed cost** (sum over all opened facilities):

$$
F \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} f_i \cdot y_i
$$

**Variable operating cost** (assume linear in throughput):

$$
V \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} g_i \cdot \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \right)
$$

**Transportation cost**:

$$
C_{tr} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}
$$

**Inventory cost** (each customer holds safety stock proportional to demand served by nearest facility):

$$
C_{inv} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} h_j \cdot z \cdot \sigma_{d_j} \cdot \sqrt{L_j}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine into the full objective function

$$
\boxed{\; TC \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} f_i \, y_i \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} g_i \!\! \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij} \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} h_j \, z \, \sigma_{d_j} \sqrt{L_j} \;}
$$

### Step 5 — Subject to constraints (KTU expects you to mention these)

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} \;\geq\; D_j \quad \forall j \; \text{(demand satisfaction)}
$$

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \;\leq\; K_i \cdot y_i \quad \forall i \; \text{(capacity limit)}
$$

$$
x_{ij} \;\geq\; 0, \quad y_i \;\in\; \{0,1\}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Complete, No Skipped Steps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question (Module 1, KTU-style):** A Kerala-based spice company is choosing **one** warehouse location between **Kochi (i = 1)** and **Kozhikode (i = 2)** to serve three customer markets: **Trivandrum (j = 1)**, **Cochin city (j = 2)**, and **Mysore (j = 3)**.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Fixed cost Kochi $f_1$ | **8,00,000 INR/year** |
| Fixed cost Kozhikode $f_2$ | **6,00,000 INR/year** |
| Kochi capacity $K_1$ | **6,000 tons/year** |
| Kozhikode capacity $K_2$ | **5,000 tons/year** |
| Transport rate $T$ | **5 INR per ton-km** |

**Distance matrix (km):**

| From \ To | Trivandrum | Cochin city | Mysore |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kochi | 220 | 30 | 350 |
| Kozhikode | 450 | 200 | 130 |

**Annual demand:** Trivandrum = 1,500 t, Cochin city = 2,000 t, Mysore = 1,500 t.

### Step 1 — Compute total demand

$$
D_{total} \;=\; 1500 + 2000 + 1500 \;=\; 5000 \text{ tons/year}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute "all-customer" weighted distance from each candidate

**Kochi to all customers (all demand served by Kochi):**

$$
d_1 \;=\; 1500(220) + 2000(30) + 1500(350)
$$

$$
d_1 \;=\; 330000 + 60000 + 525000 \;=\; 915000 \text{ ton-km}
$$

**Kozhikode to all customers (all demand served by Kozhikode):**

$$
d_2 \;=\; 1500(450) + 2000(200) + 1500(130)
$$

$$
d_2 \;=\; 675000 + 400000 + 195000 \;=\; 1270000 \text{ ton-km}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute annual transportation cost for each option

$$
C_{tr,1} \;=\; T \cdot d_1 \;=\; 5 \times 915000 \;=\; 45,75,000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
C_{tr,2} \;=\; T \cdot d_2 \;=\; 5 \times 1270000 \;=\; 63,50,000 \text{ INR}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute total network cost (fixed + transport)

$$
TC_{Kochi} \;=\; F_1 + C_{tr,1} \;=\; 8,00,000 + 45,75,000 \;=\; 53,75,000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
TC_{Kozhikode} \;=\; F_2 + C_{tr,2} \;=\; 6,00,000 + 63,50,000 \;=\; 69,50,000 \text{ INR}
$$

### Step 5 — Feasibility check (capacity)

- Kochi capacity = 6,000 t ≥ 5,000 t demand → **Feasible** ✓
- Kozhikode capacity = 5,000 t ≥ 5,000 t demand → **Feasible** (just at limit) ✓

### Step 6 — Decision

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Choose KOCHI. Savings} = 69,50,000 - 53,75,000 = 15,75,000 \text{ INR/year} \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **single-facility Capacitated Facility Location Problem (CFLP)**. In KTU exams, you will also see the **2-facility allocation variant** where you must split demand between two DCs to minimize cost — the method is the same, but you enumerate customer assignment.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Supply Chain Network — Block Architecture

This diagram shows the **physical flow** of products, information, and funds through a typical supply chain network. Each block is a *facility* whose **location and role** is decided during network design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1["SUPPLIERS\n(RAW MATERIAL SOURCE)"] --> n2["MANUFACTURING PLANT\n(MAKE)"]
    n2 --> n3["CENTRAL WAREHOUSE\n(STORE BULK)"]
    n3 --> n4["REGIONAL DC 1\n(STORE / CROSS DOCK)"]
    n3 --> n5["REGIONAL DC 2\n(STORE / CROSS DOCK)"]
    n4 --> n6["RETAIL OUTLET A\n(DELIVER)"]
    n4 --> n7["RETAIL OUTLET B\n(DELIVER)"]
    n5 --> n8["E COMMERSE CUSTOMER\n(LAST MILE)"]
    n5 --> n9["INSTITUTIONAL BUYER\n(B2B SHIPMENT)"]
    n6 -.->|"INFORMATION FLOW"| n10["DEMAND SIGNAL"]
    n7 -.->|"INFORMATION FLOW"| n10
    n8 -.->|"INFORMATION FLOW"| n10
    n9 -.->|"INFORMATION FLOW"| n10
    n10 -->|"FORECAST AND REORDER"| n1
    n2 -.->|"FUNDS FLOW"| n11["FINANCE AND AUDIT"]
    n6 -.->|"FUNDS FLOW"| n11
    n7 -.->|"FUNDS FLOW"| n11
```

## 4.2 Network Design Decision Framework

This diagram shows the **decision logic** a manager follows when designing a supply chain network. It is the same framework referenced in the KTU textbook (Chopra & Meindl, *Supply Chain Management*).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["STEP 1: DEFINE COMPETITIVE STRATEGY\n(Cost leader / Differentiator / Responder)"] --> s2
    s2["STEP 2: IDENTIFY CUSTOMER VALUE CHAIN\n(Service, Price, Variety, Speed)"] --> s3
    s3["STEP 3: COLLECT NETWORK DATA\n(Demand, Costs, Capacity, Tariffs)"] --> s4
    s4["STEP 4: FORMULATE OPTIMIZATION MODEL\n(Min TC subject to service and capacity)"] --> s5
    s5["STEP 5: GENERATE CANDIDATE NETWORKS\n(Scenarios: Centralized / Regional / Hybrid)"] --> s6
    s6["STEP 6: SOLVE AND COMPARE SCENARIOS\n(Cost, Service, Risk)"] --> s7
    s7["STEP 7: SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS\n(What if demand grows 20 percent?)"] --> s8
    s8{"IS NETWORK\nROBUST?"}
    s8 -->|YES| s9["STEP 8: SELECT AND IMPLEMENT"]
    s8 -->|NO| s3
```

## 4.3 Cost-Trade-off Schematic

Network design always involves a **trade-off**. The most famous is the **inventory–transportation trade-off**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["FEWER FACILITIES\n(CENTRALIZED)"] --> a2["LOWER FIXED COST\nLOWER INVENTORY COST\nHIGHER TRANSPORT COST\nHIGHER RISK"]
    a3["MORE FACILITIES\n(DECENTRALIZED)"] --> a4["HIGHER FIXED COST\nHIGHER INVENTORY COST\nLOWER TRANSPORT COST\nLOWER RISK"]
    a5["OPTIMUM ZONE\n(BASED ON CUSTOMER DENSITY\nAND SERVICE TARGET)"] -.-> a2
    a5 -.-> a4
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why does an additional warehouse not always reduce cost?"* Use the diagram above in your answer — it is a 3-marker gift.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q1. Define supply chain network design. Why is it considered a strategic decision?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Supply chain network design is the process of configuring the locations and roles of facilities — suppliers, plants, warehouses, distribution centers, and retail outlets — and the flows of products, information, and funds among them, in order to minimize total cost while achieving the target service level.

It is considered **strategic** because:

1. The decision involves **large capital investment** and a long payback period (3–10 years).
2. It is **difficult and expensive to reverse** once facilities are built.
3. It **constrains all subsequent decisions** on inventory, transportation, and information systems.

> *(Word count ~ 80. Crisp 3-marker style.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. List any four factors that influence supply chain network design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Competitive strategy** of the firm (cost leader vs differentiator).
2. **Customer service requirements** (delivery time, fill rate).
3. **Transportation and facility cost structure** in target geographies.
4. **Government policies** — taxes, tariffs, free-trade zones, FDI rules.
5. **Demand characteristics** — volume, variability, and customer density.
6. **Risk factors** — geopolitical, natural disaster, supplier concentration.

*(Any 4 well-explained points = full 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **strategic role of network design** in a supply chain. Discuss how it impacts the **four key cost components** of a supply chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **network design decisions influence customer service level** and **supply chain responsiveness**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The strategic role of network design is to create the **physical, financial, and operational skeleton** on which the entire supply chain operates. It impacts four cost components:

| Cost Component | Effect of Network Design | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Facility Cost** | Determined by number, size, and location of plants and warehouses. | Amazon's 175+ Fulfillment Centers = huge fixed cost but enables same-day delivery. |
| **Transportation Cost** | Determined by shipment distance, mode, and consolidation. | A southern India plant serving North India = high freight; relocating closer reduces it. |
| **Inventory Cost** | Determined by safety stock at each stocking point. | More DCs = more safety stock duplicated = higher holding cost. |
| **Operating Cost** | Determined by labor, energy, and automation level. | Plant in Kerala = high labor cost; plant in Tamil Nadu = lower. |

**Conclusion:** Network design is the **highest-leverage decision** because it sets the cost structure for years.

---
