**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Network design directly affects two customer-facing outcomes:

1. **Customer Service Level (Fill Rate):** A DC close to the customer can refill a stockout within hours. Example: **BigBasket** uses micro-fulfillment centers in apartment complexes to deliver groceries in **15–30 minutes**. Without this network, fill rate would collapse.

2. **Responsiveness (Lead Time):** A decentralized network enables **2–4 day** delivery; a centralized one enables **7–14 day** delivery. Example: **Flipkart** uses regional sortation centers to deliver Tier-1 in 24 h and Tier-2 in 2–3 days.

3. **Product Variety:** Localized DCs can stock more SKUs for local tastes. Example: **Amul** maintains regional chilling centers to deliver fresh variants of curd, paneer, and buttermilk.

4. **Reliability:** A well-designed network has redundancy. Example: **Hospitals** stock critical drugs at multiple pharmacies so a single point of failure does not stop treatment.

**Conclusion:** The right network design **translates the supply chain strategy into customer experience**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **framework for supply chain network design decisions** highlighting the steps a manager must follow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is evaluating **two warehouse locations** for serving three customers.

- Fixed cost: Location A = **Rs. 5,00,000/year**, Location B = **Rs. 3,00,000/year**
- Transport cost = **Rs. 4 per ton-km**
- Demand: $D_1 = 1000$ t, $D_2 = 1500$ t, $D_3 = 2500$ t
- Distances: A→1 = 100 km, A→2 = 200 km, A→3 = 300 km; B→1 = 300 km, B→2 = 100 km, B→3 = 200 km

**Calculate the total cost for each option and recommend the best location.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The standard framework (Chopra & Meindl) is:

1. **Define the supply chain strategy** (cost, response, innovation focus).
2. **Identify the customer value chain** — what customers value.
3. **Gather network data** — demand, costs, capacity, tariffs, lead times.
4. **Formulate the optimization model** — minimize TC subject to service constraint.
5. **Generate scenarios** — centralized, decentralized, hybrid, regional.
6. **Solve the model** — using heuristics, MILP, simulation, or software.
7. **Run sensitivity analysis** — test ±20% demand, ±10% cost scenarios.
8. **Select and implement** the robust design.

A **7-mark** answer should also include a small sketch of the **decision flow** and mention the **inventory–transportation trade-off** and **efficiency–resilience trade-off**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks (Complete Worked Solution)**

**Step 1 — Total demand:**

$$
D \;=\; 1000 + 1500 + 2500 \;=\; 5000 \text{ tons/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Weighted distance for Location A:**

$$
d_A \;=\; 1000(100) + 1500(200) + 2500(300)
$$

$$
d_A \;=\; 100000 + 300000 + 750000 \;=\; 1,150,000 \text{ ton-km}
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted distance for Location B:**

$$
d_B \;=\; 1000(300) + 1500(100) + 2500(200)
$$

$$
d_B \;=\; 300000 + 150000 + 500000 \;=\; 950,000 \text{ ton-km}
$$

**Step 4 — Transportation cost:**

$$
C_{tr,A} \;=\; 4 \times 1150000 \;=\; 46,00,000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
C_{tr,B} \;=\; 4 \times 950000 \;=\; 38,00,000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cost:**

$$
TC_A \;=\; 5,00,000 + 46,00,000 \;=\; 51,00,000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
TC_B \;=\; 3,00,000 + 38,00,000 \;=\; 41,00,000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 6 — Recommendation:**

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Choose LOCATION B. Savings} = 51,00,000 - 41,00,000 = 10,00,000 \text{ INR/year} \;}
$$

**Verification (sensitivity):** Even if Location B's fixed cost rises by Rs. 2,00,000, it still remains cheaper. **B is robust.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - ❌ Forgetting to convert **distance × tons** before multiplying by rate. Many students write $5 \times 100$ instead of $5 \times 1,150,000$ — this is the **#1 source of full-mark loss**.
> - ❌ Not stating the **decision variables** $y_i$ and $x_{ij}$ explicitly when writing the cost function. KTU wants to see the **mathematical structure**, not just numbers.
> - ❌ Mixing up **service level (Type-1 vs Type-2)** in the objective function. Type-2 is preferred in network design.
> - ❌ Missing the **capacity constraint** $\sum_j x_{ij} \leq K_i y_i$ — a complete 7-mark derivation must include all constraints.
> - ❌ Writing the absolute value symbol as `|x|` inside a markdown table — this breaks the table. Use **\vert x \vert** in LaTeX.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Things You Must Remember for KTU PEMET418 — Module 1**

- **Definition:** Network design = configuring facilities, flows, and information to minimize total cost and meet service level.
- **Strategic horizon:** 3–10 years; **very high** cost of reversal.
- **Four key decisions:** **W**here, **W**hat role, **W**hich market, **H**ow much capacity.
- **Three cost buckets:** fixed facility, transportation, inventory.
- **The objective function:**
  $$TC = \sum f_i y_i + \sum \sum c_{ij} x_{ij} + \sum h_j z \sigma_{d_j}\sqrt{L_j}$$
- **Constraints:** demand satisfaction, capacity, non-negativity, binary.
- **Key trade-off:** inventory–transportation (fewer DCs = more transport, less inventory).
- **Service level** is enabled, not guaranteed, by network design.
- **Efficiency–Resilience trade-off** — post-COVID has shifted balance toward resilience.
- **Decision framework** = Strategy → Data → Model → Scenarios → Sensitivity → Implement.
- **Real-world examples** to memorize: Amazon, Walmart, Zara, Apple, Flipkart, BigBasket.
- **In Kerala context:** Spices, coir, tea, ayurveda, seafood — these are the **KTU-favoured industry examples**.
- **Always show units** in numerical answers (INR/year, ton-km, units/year).
- **Always check capacity** feasibility before recommending a location.
- **Sensitivity analysis** is what separates a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Role of Network Design**
> *(Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10, NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models for Designing Regional Network Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Models for Designing Regional Network Configuration ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Regional Network Configuration (RNC)** refers to the strategic decision-making process of determining the optimal number, location, capacity, and mission of facilities (plants, warehouses, distribution centers, cross-docks) that will serve a given geographic market region to minimize total supply chain costs while meeting customer service requirements. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for PEMET418, the formal model framework involves positioning facilities such that the sum of facility costs (fixed + operating) and distribution costs (transportation, handling, inventory) is minimized, subject to demand satisfaction constraints. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Regional Network Configuration models are part of **Module 1 – Supply Chain Network Design**. The official syllabus mandates coverage of: *Factor Rating, Center of Gravity, Load-Distance technique, Break-Even Analysis, Transportation Method, Set-Covering/Maximal-Covering Location, and Network Optimization* formulations. Mathematically, a generic location model can be expressed as: $$\min Z = \sum_{i \in I} f_i \cdot y_i + \sum_{i \in I}\sum_{j \in J} c_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$$ Subject to: - Demand satisfaction: $\sum_{i} x_{ij} = D_j$ for all $j \in J$ - Capacity constraint: $\sum_{j} x_{ij} \leq s_i \cdot y_i$ for all $i \in I$ - Binary assignment: $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$, $x_{ij} \geq 0$ Where: - $f_i$ = fixed cost of opening facility $i$ - $c_{ij}$ = per-unit transportation cost between $i$ and $j$ - $d_{ij}$ = distance between $i$ and $j$ - $D_j$ = demand at market $j$ - $s_i$ = capacity of facility $i$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a Kerala-based FMCG company (like a snack manufacturer) trying to decide **where to put your next warehouse** to serve retailers across South India. You could: - Put it in **Kochi** (close to your factory, but far from Bangalore retailers) - Put it in **Bangalore** (close to the biggest market, but shipping raw materials is costly) - Put it in **Coimbatore** (central, but neither close to source nor market) A **Regional Network Configuration Model** is like a **"GPS for facility placement"** — it mathematically calculates the *best compromise location* balancing all costs and service levels. Think of it as the corporate equivalent of deciding the best spot to place a single pizza delivery outlet to serve an entire city, but with thousands of customers and millions in rupees at stake. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter for KTU exams?** This topic carries a **high weightage (14-mark Part B questions expected)** in Module 1 because facility location decisions are *irreversible strategic decisions* — once a ₹50 crore warehouse is built, it cannot be moved easily. Thus, managers need rigorous quantitative models to justify their choices. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Network Design - **Euclidean Distance:** $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ - **Manhattan Distance:** $d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ - **Cost per unit-distance:** measured in **INR (₹) per ton-km** - **Service Level:** percentage of demand delivered within a target time (e.g., **24-hour delivery**) - **Capacity Utilization:** $\frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Designed Capacity}} \times 100\%$ --- > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always start your answer with a clear *one-line definition* of the model being asked. KTU examiners reward definitional precision (often awarding 1–2 marks just for a correct opening line). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Network Configuration Models Network design models in supply chain management are broadly classified into: 1. **Single-Facility Continuous Location Models** (e.g., Center of Gravity) 2. **Multi-Facility Discrete Location Models** (e.g., Set Covering, P-Median) 3. **Capacity-Constrained Models** (e.g., Capacitated Plant Location) 4. **Coverage-Based Models** (e.g., Maximal Covering Location Problem – MCLP) 5. **Optimization-Based Models** (e.g., Transportation Method for Location) 6. **Qualitative Scoring Models** (e.g., Factor Rating Method) ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Major Model #### **A. Center of Gravity Method (COG)** Used when an existing facility is to be relocated to minimize total transportation cost. Assumes cost is proportional to distance and demand. - **Why?** Because it's the *analytical* analog of finding the "center of mass" in physics — placing a single facility at the demand-weighted centroid minimizes weighted distance. - **How?** Iteratively calculates the weighted average of (longitude, latitude) coordinates of all demand points. #### **B. Load-Distance Method** Selects among a finite set of candidate locations the one that minimizes the sum of (load × distance) for all demand-assignment pairs. - **Why?** Because in real life, you cannot place a facility *anywhere* — you have 3-5 shortlisted sites. Load-Distance picks the best from these. - **How?** Compute $\sum_{j} d_{ij} \cdot D_j$ for each candidate $i$, and choose the one with the minimum. #### **C. Factor Rating Method** A qualitative-quantitative hybrid that scores candidate locations on weighted critical success factors. - **Why?** Because some factors (labor quality, government incentives, political stability) are **not easily quantifiable** but are critical. - **How?** Assign weights to factors → score each location on each factor → compute weighted total → pick the highest scorer. #### **D. Break-Even Analysis for Location** Determines the range of demand/volume over which a particular location is the most cost-effective, by comparing total cost curves of two or more alternatives. - **Why?** Because a location that is cheaper at low volumes may become expensive at high volumes — Break-Even identifies the *switching point*. - **How?** Solve the equation: $TC_A = TC_B$ to find the break-even volume $Q^*$. #### **E. Transportation Method Applied to Location** Treats candidate locations as sources and markets as destinations; solves the resulting transportation problem to find the minimum-cost assignment. - **Why?** Because it leverages the well-known **MODI/Stepping-Stone** algorithms that students already study in Operations Research. - **How?** Set up the cost matrix → check balance → find initial basic feasible solution (North-West Corner / Vogel's / Least Cost) → optimize using MODI. #### **F. Set-Covering and Maximal-Covering Location Models** - **Set-Covering:** Minimize the number of facilities needed to ensure **every demand point** lies within a maximum service distance/time. - **Maximal-Covering:** Given a **fixed number of facilities**, maximize the total demand covered within the service threshold. - **Why?** Because emergency services (ambulances, fire stations) and **24-hour delivery promises** require coverage guarantees, not just cost minimization. - **How?** Binary integer programming: $y_i = 1$ if facility $i$ is opened, $x_{ij} = 1$ if demand $j$ is covered. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Model** | **Objective Function** | **Decision Variables** | **Key Formula** | **Units** | |-----------|------------------------|------------------------|-----------------|-----------| | Center of Gravity | Minimize $\sum c_i d_i$ | $(x^*, y^*)$ = location | $x^* = \frac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$, $y^* = \frac{\sum w_i y_i}{\sum w_i}$ | km, m | | Load-Distance | Minimize $\sum_j d_{ij} D_j$ | Candidate index $i$ | $L_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} d_{ij} \cdot D_j$ | ton-km | | Factor Rating | Maximize $\sum w_k s_{ik}$ | Candidate index $i$ | $Score_i = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot s_{ik}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | | Break-Even Analysis | $TC_A = TC_B$ | Volume $Q$ | $Q^* = \frac{F_B - F_A}{V_A - V_B}$ | units/year | | Transportation | Minimize $\sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$ | Flow $x_{ij}$ | Standard transportation constraints | ₹ | | Set-Covering | Minimize $\sum y_i$ | $y_i \in \{0,1\}$ | $\sum_{i \in N_j} y_i \geq 1$ | count | | Maximal-Covering | Max $\sum a_j x_j$ | $y_i, x_j \in \{0,1\}$ | $x_j \leq \sum_{i \in N_j} y_i$ | demand covered | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULA — Center of Gravity (Board Exam Favorite):** > $$x^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}, \quad y^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$ > Where $w_i$ is typically the **load shipped** to point $i$, and $(x_i, y_i)$ is the coordinate of demand point $i$. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Management - **FMCG Companies** (Hindustan Unilever, ITC): Use Center of Gravity to place regional depots. - **E-commerce** (Amazon, Flipkart): Use Set-Covering/Maximal-Covering to ensure 24-hour delivery coverage. - **Manufacturing Plants** (Maruti, Tata Motors): Use Factor Rating + Break-Even to choose between competing industrial states. - **Logistics Service Providers** (Delhivery, BlueDart): Use Load-Distance and Transportation models for hub selection. --- > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Insight for KTU 2024:** The **Break-Even Analysis** formula $Q^* = \frac{F_B - F_A}{V_A - V_B}$ is *almost guaranteed* to appear in some form. Memorize the sign convention carefully: $F$ = fixed cost, $V$ = variable cost per unit. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Center of Gravity Formula The Center of Gravity method finds the single point $(x^*, y^*)$ that minimizes the total weighted distance to a set of demand points. The weighted distance function is: $$Z(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}$$ **Step 1:** Take the partial derivative of $Z$ with respect to $x$: $$\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{x - x_i}{\sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}}$$ **Step 2:** For a minimum, set the partial derivative to zero: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{x - x_i}{\sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}} = 0$$ **Step 3:** Under the assumption that the new facility is at a **central** location such that all $(x - x_i) < 0$ and $(y - y_i) < 0$ contribute symmetrically, and treating the distance term as a *linearizing constant*, this reduces to: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot (x - x_i) = 0$$ **Step 4:** Expand and solve for $x$: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x - \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i = 0 \implies x \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i$$ **Step 5:** Final result for the x-coordinate: $$x^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$ **Step 6:** By identical logic, for the y-coordinate: $$y^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on Assumptions:** The derivation assumes cost is proportional to distance and that all demand points are pulled with equal "force." In practice, the COG method yields a *good starting point* but often requires iterative refinement. ### 3.2 Derivative Step: Center of Gravity — Worked Numerical Example Suppose a company has 3 retail markets with the following data (from a typical KTU 2024 question): | Market $i$ | $x_i$ (km) | $y_i$ (km) | $w_i$ (tons) | |------------|------------|------------|--------------| | 1 | 30 | 40 | 500 | | 2 | 60 | 20 | 400 | | 3 | 20 | 60 | 300 | **Step 1: Compute weighted sums.** $$\sum w_i = 500 + 400 + 300 = 1200 \text{ tons}$$ $$\sum w_i x_i = (500)(30) + (400)(60) + (300)(20) = 15000 + 24000 + 6000 = 45000$$ $$\sum w_i y_i = (500)(40) + (400)(20) + (300)(60) = 20000 + 8000 + 18000 = 46000$$ **Step 2: Apply formulas.** $$x^* = \frac{45000}{1200} = 37.5 \text{ km}$$ $$y^* = \frac{46000}{1200} \approx 38.33 \text{ km}$$ **Step 3: Interpret.** The optimal facility location is at coordinate **(37.5 km, 38.33 km)**. This point minimizes the total weighted distance to all three markets. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix for Model Selection | **Decision Context** | **Best Model to Use** | **Reason** | |----------------------|----------------------|------------| | Single new facility, no constraints | Center of Gravity | Minimizes weighted distance analytically | | Multiple short-listed sites | Load-Distance | Direct comparison of finite candidates | | Non-quantitative factors dominate | Factor Rating | Incorporates qualitative weights | | Comparing 2-3 location options with different cost structures | Break-Even Analysis | Identifies the volume range for best choice | | Many sources, many destinations | Transportation Method | Leverages existing OR algorithm | | Emergency / coverage-based service | Set-Covering / Maximal-Covering | Optimizes for service distance | | Large-scale strategic network | Capacitated Plant Location MIP | Handles capacity & fixed costs jointly | ### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation — Break-Even Volume Let: - $F_A$ = Fixed cost at Location A - $V_A$ = Variable cost per unit at Location A - $F_B$ = Fixed cost at Location B - $V_B$ = Variable cost per unit at Location B **Total costs:** $$TC_A = F_A + V_A \cdot Q$$ $$TC_B = F_B + V_B \cdot Q$$ **At the break-even point:** $$TC_A = TC_B$$ $$F_A + V_A \cdot Q^* = F_B + V_B \cdot Q^*$$ **Rearrange:** $$F_A - F_B = (V_B - V_A) \cdot Q^*$$ **Solve for $Q^*$:** $$Q^* = \frac{F_A - F_B}{V_B - V_A}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention Tip:** The KTU examiner will *always* test whether you can identify which location is preferred *below* the break-even volume and which is preferred *above*. Always conclude with: *"For $Q < Q^*$, choose the location with the lower fixed cost; for $Q > Q^*$, choose the location with the lower variable cost."* --- > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Pattern (KTU 2024):** Step-by-step derivations carry **7 marks** in Part B questions. Always label your steps, show units, and write a one-line interpretation at the end. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Decision Logic for Selecting a Network Configuration Model ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: New Facility Location Decision"] --> B{"Is the facility
location a single point
with no constraints?"} B -- "Yes" --> C["Use Center of Gravity Method"] B -- "No" --> D{"Are there multiple
short-listed candidate
sites?"} D -- "Yes" --> E["Use Load-Distance Method"] D -- "No" --> F{"Are qualitative factors
e.g., labor, government
policy critical?"} F -- "Yes" --> G["Use Factor Rating Method"] F -- "No" --> H{"Do you need to compare
2-3 locations with different
fixed and variable costs?"} H -- "Yes" --> I["Use Break-Even Analysis"] H -- "No" --> J{"Is the priority coverage
of demand within a
service distance?"} J -- "Yes" --> K["Use Set-Covering or
Maximal-Covering Model"] J -- "No" --> L["Use Transportation Method
or Capacitated Plant
Location MIP"] C --> M["End: Optimal Location Selected"] E --> M G --> M I --> M K --> M L --> M ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Regional Network Configuration Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Sources["Supply Side"] S1["Plant 1"] S2["Plant 2"] S3["Plant 3"] end subgraph Network["Distribution Network (Regional Hubs)"] H1["Regional Hub A"] H2["Regional Hub B"] H3["Regional Hub C"] end subgraph Markets["Demand Side (Markets)"] M1["Market 1"] M2["Market 2"] M3["Market 3"] M4["Market 4"] end S1 --> H1 S1 --> H2 S2 --> H2 S2 --> H3 S3 --> H1 S3 --> H3 H1 --> M1 H1 --> M2 H2 --> M2 H2 --> M3 H3 --> M3 H3 --> M4 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Center of Gravity Concept ```mermaid graph TD P1["Market A
x=30, y=40
w=500"] -. Weight 500 .-> COG P2["Market B
x=60, y=20
w=400"] -. Weight 400 .-> COG P3["Market C
x=20, y=60
w=300"] -. Weight 300 .-> COG COG["Optimal Facility Location
COG x=37.5, y=38.33
computed by weighted average"] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Cost-Curve Schematic: Break-Even Analysis ```mermaid graph LR Q["Volume Q
units per year"] --> A A["Total Cost Line A
Slope V_A, intercept F_A"] Q --> B B["Total Cost Line B
Slope V_B, intercept F_B"] A --- X["Break-Even Point Q*
where TC_A = TC_B"] B --- X X --> Z["Decision Rule
Q less than Q star choose A
Q greater than Q star choose B"] ``` --- > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** All diagrams are rendered as Mermaid flowcharts for clarity. In KTU examination answer sheets, hand-draw a similar decision tree or cost curve graph using a **pencil and scale** for full marks (typically 2 marks reserved for diagrams in Part B). ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions** > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]** > **Q1.** Define the **Center of Gravity method** for facility location. State its key assumption. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Center of Gravity method is a quantitative technique used to determine the optimal location of a single facility by computing the *weighted average* of the coordinates of all demand points, where the weights are typically the volumes of goods shipped to each demand point. $$x^* = \frac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}, \quad y^* = \frac{\sum w_i y_i}{\sum w_i}$$ **Key Assumption:** Transportation cost is directly proportional to both distance and volume (load), and the new facility can be located at *any* continuous point in the plane without geographic restrictions. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for assumption)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q2]** > **Q2.** Distinguish between the **Set-Covering** and **Maximal-Covering** location problems. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Set-Covering Problem | Maximal-Covering Problem | |--------|---------------------|--------------------------| | Objective | Minimize the *number* of facilities needed to cover **all** demand points | Maximize the *demand covered* given a **fixed** number of facilities | | Constraint | Every demand point must be within service distance | Only $P$ facilities can be opened | | Use case | When 100% coverage is mandatory (e.g., emergency services) | When budget limits the number of facilities (e.g., express delivery hubs) | --- ### **PART B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice** --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]** > **A(a) [7 marks]** A company is considering the location of a new regional warehouse to serve 4 markets. The market coordinates and monthly demand are given below: > > | Market | $x_i$ (km) | $y_i$ (km) | Demand $w_i$ (tons) | > |--------|------------|------------|---------------------| > | M1 | 10 | 30 | 200 | > | M2 | 40 | 20 | 350 | > | M3 | 30 | 50 | 250 | > | M4 | 50 | 40 | 400 | > > Using the **Center of Gravity method**, find the optimal warehouse location. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute total demand (denominator).** $$\sum w_i = 200 + 350 + 250 + 400 = 1200 \text{ tons}$$ **Step 2: Compute weighted x-coordinate sum.** $$\sum w_i x_i = (200)(10) + (350)(40) + (250)(30) + (400)(50)$$ $$= 2000 + 14000 + 7500 + 20000 = 43500$$ **Step 3: Compute weighted y-coordinate sum.** $$\sum w_i y_i = (200)(30) + (350)(20) + (250)(50) + (400)(40)$$ $$= 6000 + 7000 + 12500 + 16000 = 41500$$ **Step 4: Apply Center of Gravity formulas.** $$x^* = \frac{43500}{1200} = 36.25 \text{ km}$$ $$y^* = \frac{41500}{1200} \approx 34.58 \text{ km}$$ **Step 5: Interpret the result.** The new warehouse should be located at **(36.25 km, 34.58 km)** to minimize the total weighted transportation distance to all 4 markets. *(7 marks: 1 mark for setup, 2 marks each for x and y computation, 1 mark for sum, 1 mark for final answer with interpretation)* --- > **A(b) [7 marks]** Two potential sites for a plant have the following cost structures: > > | Location | Fixed Cost (₹/year) | Variable Cost (₹/unit) | > |----------|----------------------|------------------------| > | Site X | 5,00,000 | 120 | > | Site Y | 8,00,000 | 90 | > > Using **Break-Even Analysis**, determine the range of annual production volume for which each site is preferable. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Write the total cost equations.** $$TC_X = 500000 + 120Q$$ $$TC_Y = 800000 + 90Q$$ **Step 2: Set them equal at the break-even point.** $$500000 + 120Q^* = 800000 + 90Q^*$$ **Step 3: Solve for $Q^*$.** $$120Q^* - 90Q^* = 800000 - 500000$$ $$30Q^* = 300000$$ $$Q^* = 10000 \text{ units}$$ **Step 4: Decision rule.** - For $Q < 10000$ units/year → **Site X** is more economical (lower fixed cost dominates). - For $Q > 10000$ units/year → **Site Y** is more economical (lower variable cost dominates). - At $Q = 10000$ units/year → Both sites yield identical total cost = ₹17,00,000. *(7 marks: 1 mark for equations, 3 marks for solving, 1 mark for break-even value, 2 marks for decision rule and interpretation)* --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]** > **B(a) [7 marks]** A logistics manager must choose between three candidate warehouse locations P, Q, and R. The distance (in km) from each location to four markets and their daily demand (in tons) are tabulated: > > | From / To | M1 (Demand=100) | M2 (Demand=150) | M3 (Demand=200) | M4 (Demand=50) | > |-----------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------| > | P | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | > | Q | 25 | 15 | 35 | 45 | > | R | 30 | 25 | 20 | 40 | > > Using the **Load-Distance Method**, identify the best location. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute Load-Distance for each candidate.** $$L_P = (20)(100) + (30)(150) + (40)(200) + (50)(50)$$ $$= 2000 + 4500 + 8000 + 2500 = 17000 \text{ ton-km}$$ $$L_Q = (25)(100) + (15)(150) + (35)(200) + (45)(50)$$ $$= 2500 + 2250 + 7000 + 2250 = 14000 \text{ ton-km}$$ $$L_R = (30)(100) + (25)(150) + (20)(200) + (40)(50)$$ $$= 3000 + 3750 + 4000 + 2000 = 12750 \text{ ton-km}$$ **Step 2: Compare and select minimum.** Since $L_R < L_Q < L_P$ (i.e., $12750 < 14000 < 17000$), **Location R is optimal**. **Step 3: Interpret.** Location R yields the lowest total weighted distance and should be selected. *(7 marks: 2 marks per location calculation + 1 mark for comparison)* --- > **B(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Factor Rating Method** for facility location with a suitable example. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Concept.** The Factor Rating Method is a *qualitative-quantitative* hybrid technique that scores candidate locations against a list of weighted critical success factors. *(2 marks)* **Step 2: Procedure.** 1. Identify the critical success factors (e.g., proximity to market, labor cost, infrastructure, tax incentives, political stability). 2. Assign weights to each factor such that $\sum w_k = 1$ or $100\%$. 3. Score each location on each factor (typically 1–5 or 1–10 scale). 4. Compute the weighted total score for each location. 5. Select the location with the highest total weighted score. **Step 3: Illustrative Example.** | Factor | Weight | Site A Score | Site B Score | Site C Score | |--------|--------|--------------|--------------|--------------| | Proximity to market | 0.30 | 8 | 7 | 9 | | Labor cost | 0.20 | 6 | 9 | 7 | | Infrastructure | 0.25 | 7 | 8 | 8 | | Tax incentives | 0.15 | 9 | 6 | 7 | | Political stability | 0.10 | 7 | 8 | 9 | | **Weighted Total** | **1.00** | **7.35** | **7.60** | **7.95** | **Site C is selected** as the best location with a weighted score of 7.95. *(7 marks: 2 marks for procedure, 3 marks for worked example, 2 marks for interpretation)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting units:** Many students write $x^* = 37.5$ without specifying "km" — *lose 0.5 mark*. > 2. **Sign error in Break-Even:** Mixing up which location has the *higher* fixed cost leads to an inverted decision rule — *lose 2 marks*. > 3. **Confusing Set-Covering with Maximal-Covering:** Set-Covering *minimizes the number of facilities*; Maximal-Covering *maximizes coverage with a fixed number* — *lose 1 mark for incorrect objective*. > 4. **No interpretation:** Always end with a one-line *interpretation* of your numerical answer — examiners expect business relevance, not just numbers. > 5. **Skipping the COG assumption:** The Center of Gravity method assumes *linear cost-distance relationship* — students often miss this and lose 1 mark. --- ### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER** - ✅ **Center of Gravity** = weighted average of demand-point coordinates; assumes cost ∝ distance × load. - ✅ **Load-Distance** = pick the candidate site that minimizes $\sum d_{ij} D_j$ — used when sites are pre-shortlisted. - ✅ **Factor Rating** = weighted-score qualitative method — use when non-financial factors (labor, policy) matter. - ✅ **Break-Even Analysis** = find $Q^*$ where $TC_A = TC_B$; gives the *volume range* for each location's dominance. - ✅ **Transportation Method** = treat candidate sites as sources, markets as destinations; solve via MODI/Stepping-Stone. - ✅ **Set-Covering** = minimize facilities; **Maximal-Covering** = maximize demand covered with $P$ facilities. - ✅ **Network Optimization (MIP)** = combines fixed costs, variable costs, capacity, and demand constraints. - ✅ **Euclidean distance** is the default in most network design models; **Manhattan distance** is used for grid-like city layouts. - ✅ **Iterative refinement** of the COG solution is often needed because real distances are non-linear. - ✅ **Always state assumptions** (continuum of locations, cost proportionality, demand certainty) — examiners love this. --- > [!TIP] > **Final 60-Second Revision Strategy:** Memorize **four** model names, **four** objective functions, and **four** decision rules. In a 14-mark question, you can score **10+ marks** just by *naming the right model and writing its formula correctly*, even if the numerical computation has a minor error. The KTU 2024 scheme rewards **methodology and structure** as much as the final number.

topics.title: Impact of Globalisation on Supply Chain Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Impact of Globalisation on Supply Chain Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Globalisation** in the context of supply chain management refers to the strategic integration and interdependence of procurement, production, logistics, and distribution activities across international borders to create a seamless, value-adding network that transcends national boundaries. It represents the transition from a domestic, locally-optimised supply chain to a **geographically distributed, multi-tier, multi-stakeholder network** that exploits comparative advantages of different regions.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET418) syllabus framing, globalisation in supply chains is defined as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Globalisation of Supply Chain Networks:** *"The process of configuring and coordinating the flow of materials, information, and finances across geographically dispersed locations—spanning raw material sourcing, manufacturing, warehousing, and end-customer delivery—to achieve competitive advantage through cost optimisation, market expansion, risk diversification, and access to specialised capabilities."*

### 1.2 Key Constituents of a Globalised Supply Chain

A fully globalised supply chain network exhibits the following characteristic features:

- **Geographically Dispersed Nodes:** Production, sourcing, and distribution facilities located in different countries/continents.
- **Cross-Border Flows:** Movement of raw materials, semi-finished goods, finished products, capital, and information across national boundaries.
- **Multi-Tier Supplier Networks:** Tier-1, Tier-2, and Tier-3 suppliers spanning multiple economies.
- **Strategic Alliances & Contracts:** Long-term partnerships with international vendors, 3PLs, and 4PLs.
- **Standardised yet Localised Operations:** Global standards with local market adaptation (glocalisation).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Global Kitchen**
> Imagine you are running a global restaurant chain. You don't grow tomatoes in your own backyard. Instead, you source tomatoes from Italy (best climate), olive oil from Greece (best quality), cheese from Switzerland (specialty), spices from India (variety), and cook the dishes in a centralised kitchen in Singapore (low labour cost). Finally, you serve the pizza hot in New York, Tokyo, and Dubai within hours.
>
> **What just happened?** You built a **Global Supply Chain Network.**
> - Italy is your **upstream supplier**.
> - Singapore is your **global manufacturing hub**.
> - New York, Tokyo, Dubai are your **distributed downstream markets**.
> - The entire orchestration is **globalisation of your supply chain.**

This is exactly how Apple, Toyota, Nike, Zara, and McDonald's operate—designing in one country, manufacturing in another, and selling across the globe.

### 1.4 Core Terminology Reference

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Global Sourcing** | Procuring raw materials/components from international suppliers to leverage cost/quality advantages. |
| **Offshoring** | Relocating business processes to a foreign country. |
| **Reshoring** | Bringing previously offshored operations back to the home country. |
| **Nearshoring** | Relocating operations to a neighbouring or geographically closer country. |
| **Friendshoring** | Sourcing from politically allied nations to reduce geopolitical risk. |
| **Gloc alisation** | Global strategy + Local responsiveness (Think global, act local). |
| **Trade Blocs** | Regional economic alliances (EU, ASEAN, NAFTA/USMCA) that shape supply chain decisions. |

### 1.5 The Three Pillars of Globalisation Impact on Supply Chains

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **three primary dimensions** through which globalisation reshapes supply chain networks:
> 1. **Market Dimension** – Access to global customers and new revenue streams.
> 2. **Cost Dimension** – Exploitation of cost arbitrage (labour, raw materials, land).
> 3. **Capability Dimension** – Access to specialised skills, technology, and innovation ecosystems.

These three pillars form the analytical lens through which all subsequent sections evaluate the globalised supply chain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Drivers of Globalisation in Supply Chains

The globalisation of supply chains is not accidental; it is propelled by **five fundamental drivers**, each of which directly alters the network design decisions (location, capacity, inventory, transportation, information).

#### Driver 1: Cost Arbitrage (The Economic Driver)

Firms seek to minimise **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** by locating activities in low-cost regions. The total landed cost is the sum of:

$$\text{TLC} = P + T + I + W + C_c + C_d + C_i$$

Where:
- $P$ = Purchase price of the product
- $T$ = Transportation cost
- $I$ = Inventory carrying cost
- $W$ = Warehousing cost
- $C_c$ = Customs duties / tariffs
- $C_d$ = Documentation & compliance cost
- $C_i$ = Insurance cost

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** A firm might source from a country with a higher unit price ($P$) if the lower logistics costs ($T$) and duty-free access ($C_c$) make the **Total Landed Cost** significantly lower. This is the bedrock of global sourcing decisions.

#### Driver 2: Market Access & Growth (The Demand Driver)

Companies establish production/distribution facilities closer to emerging consumer markets (BRICS nations, ASEAN, Africa) to:
- Reduce **lead time** to customers.
- Avoid import tariffs by producing locally.
- Respond faster to local tastes (glocalisation).

#### Driver 3: Capability & Technology Access (The Strategic Driver)

Certain regions possess specialised competencies:
- **Silicon Valley** → Semiconductor design & AI
- **Germany** → Precision engineering
- **Japan** → Robotics & automation
- **China** → Mass manufacturing scale
- **India** → Software & R&D services

Globalisation allows firms to **tap into these innovation clusters**.

#### Driver 4: Risk Diversification (The Resilience Driver)

By spreading suppliers and facilities across geographies, firms reduce dependency on a single source. However, globalisation also *introduces* new risks (discussed in Section 2.3).

#### Driver 5: Trade Liberalisation & Policy Environment (The Institutional Driver)

Agreements like **WTO frameworks**, **USMCA**, **RCEP**, **EU Single Market**, and **ASEAN FTA** progressively lower trade barriers, making cross-border supply chains economically viable.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Framework | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** | $\text{TLC} = P + T + I + W + C_c + C_d + C_i$ | Global sourcing decisions |
| **Effective Tariff Rate** | $\text{ETR} = \dfrac{\text{Tariff Paid}}{\text{CIF Value}} \times 100$ | Trade cost analysis |
| **Inventory Carrying Cost** | $ICC = \dfrac{A \times C \times I}{100}$ (A = avg. inventory, C = unit cost, I = carrying cost %) | Global warehouse decisions |
| **Lead Time Variance** | $\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{supplier}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{transit}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{customs}}^2}$ | Global supply chain risk modelling |
| **Bullwhip Effect Amplification** | $\text{Var}(Q) \approx \dfrac{L}{1+\rho} \cdot \text{Var}(D)$ (Chen et al. model) | Demand distortion in global chains |
| **Network Optimisation (p-median)** | $\min \sum_{i} \sum_{j} h_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ | Facility location across countries |
| **Cobb-Douglas Globalisation Index** | $G = \alpha L^\beta K^\gamma$ (L = labour, K = capital) | Economic openness measure |
| **Kogut's Location Advantage** | $LA_i = f(GDP_i, \text{Labour}_i, \text{Infra}_i, \text{Tariff}_i)$ | Country attractiveness scoring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Questions often ask students to *qualitatively* discuss impact, but expect the use of these formulas in Part B (14-mark) numerical problems involving cost comparison across countries or facility location decisions. Memorising the **TLC** formula and **Lead Time Variance** is critical.

### 2.3 Structural Impact of Globalisation on Supply Chain Networks

#### Impact 1: Network Configuration Transformation

| Aspect | Pre-Globalisation (Domestic SC) | Post-Globalisation (Global SC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Tiers** | 2–3 tiers | 5–10+ tiers |
| **Geographic Spread** | Single country | Multi-continent |
| **Supplier Base** | 10–50 local suppliers | 500–5,000 global suppliers |
| **Lead Time** | Days | Weeks to months |
| **Information Systems** | Local ERP | Cloud-based, multi-currency, multi-language |
| **Inventory Strategy** | Push (forecast-based) | Hybrid (Postponement, VMI, CPFR) |

#### Impact 2: Strategic Shifts in Supply Chain Design

Globalisation forces firms to adopt advanced strategies:

- **Postponement:** Final product assembly/configuration delayed until customer demand is known (e.g., Dell, HP printers).
- **Risk Pooling:** Centralised inventory serving multiple regional markets.
- **Multi-Modal Transportation:** Combining sea, air, rail, and road for cost-effective global flows.
- **Hub-and-Spoke Distribution:** Central global hub feeding regional spokes.

#### Impact 3: Challenges Introduced by Globalisation

> [!WARNING]
> **Globalisation is a double-edged sword.** While it creates value, it introduces significant challenges. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can articulate *both sides*.

| Challenge | Description |
|---|---|
| **Longer Lead Times** | Sea freight from China to USA: 25–35 days |
| **Higher Inventory Buffers** | Safety stock must absorb demand + supply + transit uncertainty |
| **Currency Fluctuation Risk** | USD-INR, EUR-USD volatility affects margins |
| **Geopolitical Risk** | Trade wars (US-China), sanctions, Brexit |
| **Compliance Complexity** | Different labour laws, environmental norms, product standards |
| **Sustainability Concerns** | High carbon footprint from long-haul logistics |
| **Loss of Control** | Tier-2 and Tier-3 suppliers often invisible to brand owners |
| **Disruption Propagation** | COVID-19, Suez Canal blockage, Taiwan Strait tensions |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

The globalisation framework is actively used by:
- **Automotive industry** (Toyota Production System + global JIT)
- **Electronics** (Apple's Foxconn-led global assembly)
- **Pharmaceuticals** (Pfizer's globally distributed vaccine cold chain)
- **Apparel** (Zara's Spain-hub + Asia-sourcing model)
- **E-Commerce** (Amazon's cross-border FBA network)

For KTU 2024 scheme students, the practical utility lies in **decision-making**: when a firm contemplates "Make vs. Buy," "Domestic vs. Global," or "Single vs. Multi-source," the globalisation framework provides the analytical structure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Impact Dimensions of Globalisation

The following matrix provides a structured, exhaustive analytical framework for evaluating the impact of globalisation across **four critical supply chain dimensions**: cost, speed, risk, and sustainability.

| Dimension | Pre-Globalisation State | Post-Globalisation Impact | Quantitative Indicator | Net Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost Structure** | High domestic labour/material cost | Lower unit cost via offshoring | $\Delta \text{TLC} = \text{TLC}_{\text{domestic}} - \text{TLC}_{\text{global}}$ | **Positive** (typically 15–30% saving) |
| **Lead Time** | Short domestic lead time | Longer transit + customs | $\text{LT}_{\text{global}} = \text{LT}_{\text{supplier}} + \text{LT}_{\text{transit}} + \text{LT}_{\text{customs}}$ | **Negative** (3–10x longer) |
| **Risk Exposure** | Concentrated, manageable | Dispersed but amplified | $\sigma_{\text{global}} > \sigma_{\text{domestic}}$ | **Negative** (higher variance) |
| **Market Reach** | Limited to home country | Multi-continent presence | $\#\text{countries served}$ | **Positive** (exponential) |
| **Innovation Access** | Local talent pool only | Global innovation clusters | $\#\text{R\&D centres abroad}$ | **Positive** |
| **Carbon Footprint** | Low transport emissions | High long-haul emissions | $CO_2 \propto \text{distance} \times \text{weight}$ | **Negative** |
| **Supply Base Resilience** | Few alternatives | Many alternatives | Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) | **Mixed** |
| **Compliance Burden** | Single regulatory regime | Multiple jurisdictions | $\#\text{regulatory bodies}$ | **Negative** |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation: Total Landed Cost Comparison

Let us derive the comparative cost advantage of a global sourcing decision, which is a frequently asked **14-mark KTU numerical problem**.

**Problem Setup:**
A KTU-context firm, "Kerala Electronics Pvt. Ltd.," manufactures a circuit board. Two sourcing options:
- **Option A (Domestic):** Buy from Kochi supplier at ₹500/unit, 3-day lead time, 0% duty.
- **Option B (Global):** Buy from Shenzhen supplier at ₹350/unit, 28-day transit, 8% import duty, ₹50/unit freight, ₹30/unit insurance.

**Step 1: Compute TLC for Option A (Domestic)**

$$\text{TLC}_A = P_A + T_A + I_A + W_A + C_{c,A} + C_{d,A} + C_{i,A}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{TLC}_A = 500 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = ₹500$$

> **Note:** Since this is domestic, transport/duty/insurance costs are minimal or included in the unit price.

**Step 2: Compute TLC for Option B (Global)**

$$\text{TLC}_B = P_B + T_B + I_B + W_B + C_{c,B} + C_{d,B} + C_{i,B}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{TLC}_B = 350 + 50 + 0 + 0 + (0.08 \times 350) + 0 + 30$$

$$\text{TLC}_B = 350 + 50 + 28 + 30 = ₹458$$

**Step 3: Compute Cost Advantage**

$$\Delta \text{TLC} = \text{TLC}_A - \text{TLC}_B = 500 - 458 = ₹42 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 4: Express as Percentage Savings**

$$\% \text{Savings} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{TLC}}{\text{TLC}_A} \times 100 = \dfrac{42}{500} \times 100 = 8.4\%$$

**Step 5: Adjust for Lead Time Risk (Inventory Carrying Cost)**

The 28-day transit means ₹458 worth of capital is locked for ~28 days instead of 3 days. Assuming carrying cost $I = 12\%$ annually:

$$\text{ICC}_A = \dfrac{500 \times 12\% \times 3}{365} = ₹0.49/\text{unit}$$

$$\text{ICC}_B = \dfrac{458 \times 12\% \times 28}{365} = ₹4.21/\text{unit}$$

$$\Delta \text{ICC} = 4.21 - 0.49 = ₹3.72 \text{ additional cost per unit}$$

**Step 6: Net Adjusted Advantage**

$$\text{Net Savings} = 42 - 3.72 = ₹38.28/\text{unit (} \approx 7.66\% \text{)}$$

**Conclusion:** Even after adjusting for longer lead time, the global sourcing option provides a **net 7.66% cost advantage**, validating the globalisation decision on purely economic grounds. However, this analysis *excludes* currency, geopolitical, and quality risks—which in real KTU exam problems should be acknowledged.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation: Geopolitical Risk Quantification

For a global supply chain, the **Lead Time Variance** is the sum of independent variances (square root of sum of squares):

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{supplier}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{transit}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{customs}}^2}$$

**Numerical Illustration:**

- Supplier variability: $\sigma_{\text{supplier}} = 2$ days
- Transit variability (weather, port congestion): $\sigma_{\text{transit}} = 4$ days
- Customs clearance variability: $\sigma_{\text{customs}} = 3$ days

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{2^2 + 4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{4 + 16 + 9} = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.39 \text{ days}$$

**Safety Stock Calculation (for 95% service level, $z = 1.65$):**

$$\text{Safety Stock} = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot D_{\text{daily}}$$

For a daily demand of $D = 100$ units:

$$\text{Safety Stock} = 1.65 \times 5.39 \times 100 = 889.35 \approx 890 \text{ units}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This safety stock of 890 units represents *capital locked up* purely due to globalisation-induced uncertainty. Compare this with a domestic supply chain where $\sigma_{LT}$ would be closer to 1–2 days, yielding safety stock of only ~250 units. This is a **3.5x increase in working capital tied up**—a hidden cost of globalisation that KTU examiners love to test.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Bullwhip Effect in Global vs. Domestic Chains

The **Bullwhip Effect** (demand variance amplification upstream) is **significantly amplified** in global supply chains due to:

$$\text{Var}(Q) \approx \dfrac{L}{1+\rho} \cdot \text{Var}(D)$$

Where:
- $L$ = Lead time (longer in global chains)
- $\rho$ = Correlation of demands (lower in global chains due to market diversity)

Since $L_{\text{global}} \gg L_{\text{domestic}}$, the upstream demand variance is **multiplied**, causing the bullwhip to be more severe. This is why global firms invest heavily in **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment)** and **demand visibility tools**.

### 3.5 Summary Matrix: Globalisation Impact on 7 SCOR Model Processes

| SCOR Process | Domestic Impact | Global Impact | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Simple demand-supply matching | Multi-region, multi-currency planning | S&OP, IBP |
| **Source** | Local vendor management | Global supplier qualification, compliance | Supplier audits, digital twins |
| **Make** | Centralised production | Distributed manufacturing | Make-or-buy, postponement |
| **Deliver** | Local trucking | Multi-modal, multi-country logistics | 3PL/4PL partnerships |
| **Return** | Easy reverse logistics | Complex cross-border returns | Regional return centres |
| **Enable** | Single regulatory regime | Multi-jurisdictional compliance | Trade compliance teams |
| **Finance** | Single currency | Multi-currency, hedging | Forward contracts, FX hedging |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Global Supply Chain Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Raw Material Suppliers\nAsia Africa Latin America] --> nodeB[Tier 2 Suppliers\nSpecialised Components]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Tier 1 Suppliers\nSub-assemblies]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Global Manufacturing Hubs\nChina Mexico India Vietnam]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Global Distribution Centres\nSingapore Rotterdam Dubai]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Regional Warehouses\nNorth America Europe Asia Pacific]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Retailers and End Customers\nGlobal Markets]
    nodeH[Information Flow\nCloud ERP Blockchain] -.-> nodeA
    nodeH -.-> nodeB
    nodeH -.-> nodeC
    nodeH -.-> nodeD
    nodeH -.-> nodeE
    nodeH -.-> nodeF
    nodeI[Financial Flows\nMulti currency FX Hedging] -.-> nodeA
    nodeI -.-> nodeD
    nodeI -.-> nodeE
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Drivers and Impact Flow of Globalisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Globalisation Drivers] --> nodeB[Cost Arbitrage]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Market Access]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Technology Access]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Risk Diversification]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Trade Liberalisation]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Supply Chain Network Impacts]
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Network Reconfiguration]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Longer Lead Times]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Higher Inventory Buffers]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Increased Compliance Burden]
    nodeG --> nodeL[Enhanced Innovation Access]
    nodeH --> nodeM[Strategic Outcomes]
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[Competitive Advantage]
    nodeM --> nodeO[Resilience or Vulnerability]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Globalisation Impact Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Domestic vs Global Sourcing Decision] --> nodeQ1{Is cost the primary driver?}
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ2{Is lead time tolerance greater than 30 days?}
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeQ3{Is geopolitical risk acceptable?}
    nodeQ3 -- Yes --> nodeR1[Adopt Global Sourcing]
    nodeQ3 -- No --> nodeR2[Adopt Nearshoring or Friendshoring]
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeR3[Retain Domestic Sourcing]
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeQ4{Is market access the primary driver?}
    nodeQ4 -- Yes --> nodeR4[Establish Local Production in Target Market]
    nodeQ4 -- No --> nodeQ5{Is innovation access the driver?}
    nodeQ5 -- Yes --> nodeR5[Acquire or Partner with Foreign Tech Firm]
    nodeQ5 -- No --> nodeR6[Maintain Status quo]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Global Supply Chain Risk Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1[Strategic Layer]
        nodeA1[Network Design Decisions]
        nodeA2[Country Selection]
        nodeA3[Supplier Portfolio Strategy]
    end
    subgraph Layer2[Tactical Layer]
        nodeB1[Inventory Positioning]
        nodeB2[Transportation Mode Choice]
        nodeB3[Contract Structures]
    end
    subgraph Layer3[Operational Layer]
        nodeC1[Order Management]
        nodeC2[Customs Clearance]
        nodeC3[Warehouse Operations]
    end
    subgraph Layer4[Risk Layer]
        nodeD1[Currency Risk]
        nodeD2[Geopolitical Risk]
        nodeD3[Disruption Risk]
        nodeD4[Compliance Risk]
    end
    Layer1 --> Layer2
    Layer2 --> Layer3
    Layer1 -.-> Layer4
    Layer2 -.-> Layer4
    Layer3 -.-> Layer4
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 4: Globalisation Continuum — From Domestic to Fully Global

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Stage 1 Domestic SC] --> node2[Stage 2 International SC]
    node2 --> node3[Stage 3 Multinational SC]
    node3 --> node4[Stage 4 Global SC]
    node4 --> node5[Stage 5 Digital Global SC]
    node1 -- Export to nearby countries --> node2
    node2 -- Establish overseas subsidiaries --> node3
    node3 -- Integrate global operations --> node4
    node4 -- AI Blockchain IoT enabled --> node5
```

### 4.6 Diagram Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use these diagrams in KTU exams:**
> - **Diagram 1** answers: "Draw a global supply chain network" (a 7-mark sub-question).
> - **Diagram 2** answers: "Explain the impact of globalisation with a flowchart" (a common 7-mark question).
> - **Diagram 3** answers: "Discuss the decision framework for global sourcing" (analytical question).
> - **Diagram 4** answers: "Describe the levels of supply chain globalisation" (a 14-mark question structure).
> - **Block Architecture** answers: "Explain the functional layers impacted by globalisation" (a Part A question).

Always **label diagrams clearly**, **use arrows for direction of flow**, and **add a 2-line caption** beneath each diagram stating its purpose.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define globalisation in the context of supply chain management. Mention any three drivers.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEMET418, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Globalisation in supply chain management refers to the strategic integration of procurement, production, distribution, and information flows across international borders to achieve competitive advantage. (1 mark)

**Three key drivers are:**
1. **Cost Arbitrage:** Exploiting lower labour and material costs in foreign countries to reduce total landed cost. (1 mark)
2. **Market Access:** Establishing operations in foreign markets to serve local customers with reduced lead time and lower tariffs. (0.5 marks)
3. **Risk Diversification:** Spreading supply and demand across geographies to reduce concentration risk. (0.5 marks)

---

#### Question 2: What is the "Bullwhip Effect," and how does globalisation amplify it?
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEMET418, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Bullwhip Effect is the phenomenon where small fluctuations in end-customer demand get amplified as they move upstream through the supply chain, resulting in excessive inventory and erratic orders at supplier level. (1.5 marks)

Globalisation amplifies the Bullwhip Effect because:
- **Longer lead times** ($L$ in the demand variance formula) magnify variance proportionally. (0.75 marks)
- **Reduced demand correlation** across geographies leads to less accurate forecasting, causing order batching and panic ordering. (0.75 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEMET418, 2024 Scheme]**

**"Globalisation has fundamentally restructured modern supply chain networks. Discuss the key drivers of supply chain globalisation and analyse its impact on network design, lead time, and inventory management with suitable examples."**

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Drivers and Network Design Impact

**Model Answer:**

**1. Drivers of Supply Chain Globalisation (4 marks):**

- **Cost Arbitrage:** Firms relocate production to low-cost regions to reduce Total Landed Cost. Example: Apple manufacturing iPhones in China via Foxconn reduces unit cost by ~30% compared to US assembly.
- **Market Expansion:** Emerging markets (BRICS, ASEAN) offer 2–3 billion new consumers. Example: Toyota establishing manufacturing plants in India to serve the South Asian market.
- **Capability Access:** Specialised clusters—Germany for automotive engineering, Taiwan for semiconductors—provide access to world-class competencies. Example: Intel designing chips in Israel and Oregon, manufacturing in Arizona.
- **Risk Diversification:** Firms spread suppliers across multiple countries to reduce dependency. Example: Apple diversifying iPhone production from China to India and Vietnam post-2020.

**2. Impact on Network Design (3 marks):**

- **Multi-tier, multi-country networks:** Networks now span 5–10+ tiers, replacing the simple 2–3 tier domestic models.
- **Hub-and-spoke and Postponement strategies:** Centralised assembly with regional configuration (e.g., Zara's Spain hub + Asia sourcing).
- **Dispersed distribution centres:** Amazon's global FBA network spanning 20+ countries.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Impact on Lead Time and Inventory Management

**Model Answer:**

**1. Lead Time Impact (3.5 marks):**

- **Extended transit times:** Sea freight from China to US takes 25–35 days vs. 2–3 days for domestic trucking.
- **Customs and compliance delays:** Border inspections add 2–7 days of variability.
- **Mathematical representation:**
  $$\text{LT}_{\text{global}} = \text{LT}_{\text{supplier}} + \text{LT}_{\text{transit}} + \text{LT}_{\text{customs}}$$
- **Example:** Toyota's global JIT system uses consolidated shipping from Thailand to reduce transit variability.

**2. Inventory Management Impact (3.5 marks):**

- **Increased safety stock:** To buffer against long and variable lead times, firms hold 2–4x more safety stock. Example: A smartphone manufacturer requires 890 units of safety stock globally vs. 250 units domestically (as derived in Section 3.3).
- **Pipeline inventory:** Goods in transit for 30+ days represent locked-up working capital.
- **Mathematical representation:**
  $$\text{Safety Stock} = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot D_{\text{daily}}$$
- **Mitigation strategies:** VMI, CPFR, postponement, nearshoring.

**Conclusion:** Globalisation increases both lead time and inventory requirements, necessitating sophisticated coordination mechanisms and digital visibility tools.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEMET418, 2024 Scheme]**

**"With the help of a suitable numerical example, explain the Total Landed Cost (TLC) framework for evaluating global sourcing decisions. Discuss the challenges introduced by globalised supply chain networks."**

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks: TLC Numerical Framework

**Model Answer:**

**1. Total Landed Cost Formula (2 marks):**

$$\text{TLC} = P + T + I + W + C_c + C_d + C_i$$

Where $P$ = purchase price, $T$ = transport, $I$ = inventory carrying, $W$ = warehousing, $C_c$ = customs duty, $C_d$ = documentation, $C_i$ = insurance.

**2. Worked Numerical Example (5 marks):**

A firm compares domestic vs. global sourcing:

| Cost Component | Domestic (Kochi) | Global (Shenzhen) |
|---|---|---|
| Purchase Price ($P$) | ₹500 | ₹350 |
| Transport ($T$) | ₹0 (incl.) | ₹50 |
| Customs Duty ($C_c$) | ₹0 | ₹28 (8%) |
| Insurance ($C_i$) | ₹0 | ₹30 |
| **TLC** | **₹500** | **₹458** |

$$\Delta \text{TLC} = 500 - 458 = ₹42 \text{ per unit savings } (8.4\%)$$

After adjusting for inventory carrying cost over 28-day transit:
$$\text{Adjusted Net Savings} = ₹38.28/\text{unit } (7.66\%)$$

**Decision:** Despite higher lead time, global sourcing remains economically superior. However, qualitative factors (currency risk, geopolitics) must be considered.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Challenges of Globalised Supply Chains

**Model Answer:**

1. **Longer and more variable lead times** due to multi-modal transportation and customs. (1.5 marks)
2. **Higher safety stock requirements** increasing working capital tied up. (1.5 marks)
3. **Currency fluctuation risk** affecting margins unpredictably. (1 mark)
4. **Geopolitical disruptions** (US-China trade war, Russia-Ukraine conflict, Suez Canal blockage). (1.5 marks)
5. **Compliance complexity** across multiple regulatory regimes (labour, environment, product standards). (1 mark)
6. **Sustainability concerns** from high carbon emissions in long-haul logistics. (0.5 marks)

**Conclusion:** Globalisation creates value but requires robust risk management frameworks, including dual sourcing, nearshoring options, and digital supply chain twins.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Defining globalisation vaguely** — Always tie it to *supply chain networks* specifically, not just economic globalisation.
> 2. **Listing drivers without explaining impact** — Examiners award marks for *cause-effect linkage*, not bullet lists.
> 3. **Ignoring the dual nature of globalisation** — Always mention both opportunities (cost, market, innovation) AND challenges (lead time, risk, sustainability).
> 4. **Numerical errors in TLC** — Students often forget to include customs duty or inventory carrying cost. Each missing component = **1–2 mark loss**.
> 5. **No diagrams** — A 14-mark answer without a single flowchart/block diagram loses up to **2 marks** for poor presentation.
> 6. **Not using real-world examples** — Apple, Toyota, Zara, Amazon examples significantly boost examiner confidence.
> 7. **Conflating "offshoring" and "globalisation"** — Offshoring is one *consequence*; globalisation is the broader phenomenon.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Impact of Globalisation on Supply Chain Networks**
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** Globalisation = cross-border integration of supply chain activities for competitive advantage.
>
> ✅ **Three Pillars:** Market access, cost arbitrage, capability access.
>
> ✅ **Five Drivers:** Cost arbitrage, market access, technology/capability, risk diversification, trade liberalisation.
>
> ✅ **Key Formula — TLC:** $\text{TLC} = P + T + I + W + C_c + C_d + C_i$
>
> ✅ **Lead Time Equation:** $\text{LT}_{\text{global}} = \text{LT}_{\text{supplier}} + \text{LT}_{\text{transit}} + \text{LT}_{\text{customs}}$
>
> ✅ **Lead Time Variance:** $\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{\sigma_s^2 + \sigma_t^2 + \sigma_c^2}$
>
> ✅ **Safety Stock:** $\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot D$
>
> ✅ **Network Shifts:** Domestic → International → Multinational → Global → Digital Global (5-stage continuum).
>
> ✅ **Impacts to Remember:** Longer lead times, higher inventory, currency risk, geopolitical risk, compliance complexity, sustainability concerns.
>
> ✅ **Strategic Responses:** Postponement, nearshoring, friendshoring, dual sourcing, digital twins, VMI/CPFR.
>
> ✅ **Real-World Examples to Quote:** Apple (Foxconn), Toyota (global JIT), Zara (hub-and-spoke), Amazon (FBA), Pfizer (cold chain).
>
> ✅ **Diagrams to Master:** Global network architecture, drivers-impact flowchart, decision framework, risk layer architecture.
>
> ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always present both sides — *benefits* and *challenges* — in 14-mark answers to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain # MODULE 2 — DEMAND FORECASTING IN SUPPLY CHAIN ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)** > **Demand Forecasting** is the process of estimating future customer demand for a product or service over a specified time horizon, using historical data, statistical techniques, causal models, and managerial judgment, to enable effective supply chain planning, inventory control, capacity planning, and procurement decisions. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, demand forecasting is treated as the **strategic backbone** of any supply chain system. It is the critical input that drives every downstream decision — from a supplier's raw material order to a retailer's shelf-stocking schedule. > [!NOTE] > **Forecasting Horizon Classification (Mandatory KTU Concept)** > - **Short-term forecast:** Up to 3 months — used for production scheduling, inventory replenishment, and order processing. > - **Medium-term forecast:** 3 months to 2 years — used for workforce planning, machine capacity allocation, and intermediate procurement. > - **Long-term forecast:** 2 years and above — used for strategic decisions like plant location, new product launch, capital investment, and supply chain network design. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you run a small tea stall near a college campus. You have noticed that every Monday morning, students buy roughly 50 cups of tea; on rainy days, demand jumps to 80 cups; and during exam week, it drops to 30. You cannot "control" this demand, but you can **prepare** for it. * **Forecasting** is exactly this: instead of *guessing* how much tea to boil, you use **past patterns** (historical data), **external clues** (weather, holidays, exams), and **structured mathematics** to predict tomorrow's demand with reasonable accuracy. This way, you never run out of tea (stockout) nor boil too much (wastage). * In supply chain language, demand forecasting replaces **guesswork with structured anticipation**, allowing every link in the chain — manufacturer, warehouse, transporter, retailer — to plan in sync. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for Students** > A forecast is *never 100% accurate*. The KTU question pattern often tests whether a student understands that the **goal of forecasting is not perfection but the minimization of forecast error**. ### Core Components of Demand Forecasting * **Demand Patterns:** Trend, Seasonality, Cyclicality, Randomness, Autocorrelation. * **Forecasting Methods:** Qualitative (Delphi, Market Research), Quantitative (Time Series, Causal), and Hybrid models. * **Forecast Error Metrics:** MAD, MSE, MAPE, MFE, Tracking Signal. * **Forecasting Process:** A four-stage cycle (Specify, Select, Validate, Operate). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Characteristics of Demand (Demand Patterns) Every demand pattern observed in a time series consists of four basic components. Identifying which components exist in the data is the **first step** before choosing a forecasting method. * **Level (L):** The baseline or average demand around which the series fluctuates. * **Trend (T):** A long-term sustained increase or decrease in the average level. Mathematically, this is a non-zero slope in the series. * **Seasonality (S):** A regular, predictable, calendar-based pattern that repeats every season, month, week, or day. Examples: ice-cream sales in summer, air-conditioner sales in April-July. * **Random/Noise (ε):** The unpredictable residual variation that cannot be explained by L, T, or S. There are two mathematical ways to combine these components: * **Additive Model:** $D_t = L + T + S + \epsilon_t$ — Used when seasonal variation is roughly constant over time. * **Multiplicative Model:** $D_t = L \times T \times S \times \epsilon_t$ — Used when seasonal variation grows proportionally with the level of the series. ### 2.2 Types of Forecasting Methods (The KTU Classification Tree) Forecasting techniques are broadly divided into two families, with a third hybrid family emerging in modern supply chain practice. * **Qualitative Methods** — Used when **no historical data is available** (e.g., new product launch). These rely on expert opinion. * *Delphi Method:* Iterative anonymous expert survey. * *Market Research:* Direct customer surveys, focus groups. * *Sales Force Composite:* Aggregation of salesperson field estimates. * **Quantitative Methods** — Used when **reliable historical data exists**. * *Time-Series Methods:* Assume demand is a function of time only. (Naive, MA, WMA, Exponential Smoothing, Holt's, Winter's). * *Causal / Associative Methods:* Demand is a function of one or more independent variables (e.g., price, advertising, GDP). Uses **Linear Regression**. * **Hybrid (AI/ML) Methods** — ARIMA, Neural Networks, Ensemble methods — combine statistical and computational intelligence. ### 2.3 Time Series Forecasting Methods (The KTU Formula Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)** > All formulas below are repeated in board exams. Memorize the logic behind each one. | Method | Core Formula (t-th period) | Key Parameter | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Naive Forecast** | $F_{t+1} = D_t$ | None | Stationary random data; baseline benchmark. | | **Simple Moving Average (n-MA)** | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} D_i$ | Window size **n** | Stable demand with no trend/seasonality. | | **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i D_{t-i+1}$, where $\sum w_i = 1$ | Weights $w_i$ | Recent data more reliable than older data. | | **Single Exponential Smoothing (SES)** | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | Smoothing constant $\alpha \in (0,1)$ | No trend, no seasonality. | | **Holt's Two-Parameter (Trend)** | $L_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1}+T_{t-1})$
$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$
$F_{t+p} = L_t + p \cdot T_t$ | $\alpha$, $\beta$ | Linear trend, no seasonality. | | **Winter's Three-Parameter (Holt-Winters)** | Additive form incorporates seasonal index $S_t$ | $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ | Trend + Seasonality both present. | | **Linear Regression (Causal)** | $\hat{D} = a + bX$ | $b = \dfrac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$
$a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}$ | When demand is driven by an external variable X. | | **Seasonal Index** | $S_i = \dfrac{\text{Average demand in season } i}{\text{Overall average demand}}$ | — | To deseasonalize or seasonally adjust. | > [!WARNING] > **Constraint:** When working with Weighted Moving Average, the **sum of all weights must exactly equal 1**. This is a frequent mark-losing point in KTU numericals. ### 2.4 Forecast Accuracy / Error Metrics (Mandatory KTU Topic) A forecast that is not evaluated is **useless** for decision-making. The following metrics quantify how far the forecast deviates from actual demand. > [!NOTE] > **The Four Standard Error Measures (Always appear in KTU Part-B questions)** * **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD):** Average of absolute errors. Easy to interpret; same unit as demand. $$MAD = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert D_t - F_t \vert$$ * **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** Average of squared errors. Penalizes large errors more heavily; useful in optimization. $$MSE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} (D_t - F_t)^2$$ * **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE):** Average of percentage errors. Scale-independent; widely used in industry for comparing products. $$MAPE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{D_t - F_t}{D_t} \right\vert \times 100\%$$ * **Mean Forecast Error (MFE) / Bias:** Average of signed errors. Detects whether the forecast systematically overshoots or undershoots. $$MFE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} (D_t - F_t)$$ * **Tracking Signal (TS):** Used to detect systematic bias. If TS lies outside the range $\pm 4$ MAD, the forecast needs correction. $$TS = \dfrac{\text{Running Sum of Forecast Errors (RSFE)}}{MAD}$$ ### 2.5 The Forecasting Process (KTU 4-Step Cycle) 1. **Determine the Use of the Forecast** — Define the decision (inventory, capacity, finance) the forecast will drive. 2. **Select the Forecasting Method** — Match method to data availability, pattern, and horizon. 3. **Validate the Model** — Compute MAD/MSE/MAPE on a holdout sample to verify accuracy. 4. **Make the Forecast and Operate** — Deploy, monitor, and continually refine. ### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Supply Chain * **Inventory Management:** Safety stock = $z \times \sigma_{LT} \times \sqrt{L}$, where forecast error variance $\sigma$ directly impacts safety stock levels. * **Capacity Planning:** Long-term forecasts determine plant size, equipment, and labor. * **Supplier Negotiation:** Forecasts drive blanket purchase orders and long-term contracts. * **Transportation Planning:** Forecasts of SKU volumes determine truck/container loads. * **Bullwhip Effect:** Inaccurate forecasting is the **root cause** of demand variability amplification upstream. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section presents the **complete worked derivations** for the most frequently asked KTU numerical problems. Every algebraic transition is written explicitly so that the model answer can be reproduced verbatim in the answer sheet. ### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Regression Coefficients (Least Squares Method) **Given:** A demand series $D_i$ influenced by an independent variable $X_i$. We assume a linear relationship: $$\hat{D}_i = a + bX_i$$ **Objective:** Find values of $a$ and $b$ that **minimize the sum of squared errors**: $$SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - \hat{D}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - a - bX_i)^2$$ **Step 1: Partial Derivative with respect to $a$ (set to zero for minimum).** We differentiate $SSE$ with respect to $a$, treating $b$ and $X_i$ as constants: $$\dfrac{\partial(SSE)}{\partial a} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - a - bX_i) = 0$$ **Step 2: Simplify the sum.** Dividing both sides by 2 and removing the minus sign: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} a - b \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i = 0$$ Since $a$ is a constant, $\sum a = n \cdot a$: $$\sum D_i = n \cdot a + b \sum X_i$$ Dividing throughout by $n$: $$\bar{D} = a + b\bar{X} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$ **Step 3: Partial Derivative with respect to $b$ (set to zero for minimum).** $$\dfrac{\partial(SSE)}{\partial b} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i (D_i - a - bX_i) = 0$$ Expanding: $$\sum X_i D_i = a \sum X_i + b \sum X_i^2 \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$ **Step 4: Solve the system of normal equations.** Substituting $a = \bar{D} - b\bar{X}$ from Equation 1 into Equation 2: $$\sum X_i D_i = (\bar{D} - b\bar{X})\sum X_i + b\sum X_i^2$$ $$\sum X_i D_i = \bar{D}\sum X_i - b\bar{X}\sum X_i + b\sum X_i^2$$ $$b\left(\sum X_i^2 - \bar{X}\sum X_i\right) = \sum X_i D_i - \bar{D}\sum X_i$$ Using the identity $\bar{X}\sum X_i = n\bar{X}^2$, and $\bar{D}\sum X_i = n\bar{X}\bar{D}$: $$b = \dfrac{\sum X_i D_i - n\bar{X}\bar{D}}{\sum X_i^2 - n\bar{X}^2}$$ **Final closed-form expressions (the KTU answer-book formulas):** $$b = \dfrac{n\sum X_i D_i - \sum X_i \sum D_i}{n\sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}, \quad a = \bar{D} - b\bar{X}$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical — 3-Month Moving Average **Problem:** A company has recorded the following monthly demand for a product: Jan = 100, Feb = 120, Mar = 110, Apr = 130, May = 125. Compute the 3-month Moving Average forecast for June and evaluate accuracy using MAD and MAPE. **Step 1: Compute the 3-MA forecasts.** The 3-MA for month $t$ uses months $t-3$, $t-2$, $t-1$. So forecast for April uses Jan, Feb, Mar. | Month | Actual $D_t$ | 3-MA Forecast $F_t$ | Error $D_t - F_t$ | $\vert \text{Error} \vert$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Jan | 100 | — | — | — | | Feb | 120 | — | — | — | | Mar | 110 | — | — | — | | Apr | 130 | $(100+120+110)/3 = 110.00$ | $20.00$ | $20.00$ | | May | 125 | $(120+110+130)/3 = 120.00$ | $5.00$ | $5.00$ | | Jun | ? | $(110+130+125)/3 = \mathbf{121.67}$ | — | — | **Step 2: Compute MAD.** $$MAD = \dfrac{1}{2}(20.00 + 5.00) = 12.50 \text{ units}$$ **Step 3: Compute MAPE.** $$MAPE = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\left\vert \dfrac{20}{130} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{5}{125} \right\vert\right) \times 100\%$$ $$MAPE = \dfrac{1}{2}(15.38\% + 4.00\%) \times 100\% = 9.69\%$$ **Inference:** A MAPE of under 10% is considered a **good** forecast in supply chain practice. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Single Exponential Smoothing (SES) **Problem:** Given $F_1 = 50$ and the demand series 50, 56, 58, 60, 55, compute the SES forecast for period 6 using $\alpha = 0.3$. The recurrence is $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$. **Iteration 1:** $F_2 = 0.3(50) + 0.7(50) = 50.00$ **Iteration 2:** $F_3 = 0.3(56) + 0.7(50.00) = 16.80 + 35.00 = 51.80$ **Iteration 3:** $F_4 = 0.3(58) + 0.7(51.80) = 17.40 + 36.26 = 53.66$ **Iteration 4:** $F_5 = 0.3(60) + 0.7(53.66) = 18.00 + 37.56 = 55.56$ **Iteration 5:** $F_6 = 0.3(55) + 0.7(55.56) = 16.50 + 38.89 = \mathbf{55.39}$ **Verification logic:** Notice how SES *lags behind* actual demand. A higher $\alpha$ makes the forecast more responsive to recent changes; a lower $\alpha$ smooths more aggressively. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Tracking Signal (Forecast Bias Detection) **Given:** RSFE (Running Sum of Forecast Errors) over 4 periods = 24, MAD = 6. $$TS = \dfrac{RSFE}{MAD} = \dfrac{24}{6} = 4.0$$ **Decision rule:** The acceptable range is $\pm 4$ MAD. A TS of +4 indicates the forecast is **persistently under-forecasting** (actual > forecast) and must be recalibrated. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Forecasting Methods | Criterion | Naive | MA | WMA | SES | Holt | Winter's | Regression | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Handles Trend? | No | No | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | | Handles Seasonality? | No | No | No | No | No | **Yes** | No (directly) | | Data Required | 1 pt | n pts | n pts | $\alpha$ + initial | $\alpha, \beta$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ | All historical X, D | | Computational Cost | Minimal | Low | Low | Low | Moderate | High | Moderate | | Responsiveness to Change | Highest | Low | Medium | Tunable | High | High | Medium | | Common KTU Exam Use | Rare | Frequent | Frequent | **Very Frequent** | Frequent | Occasional | Frequent | ## 4.
