> **Real-World Analogy — The "Monsoon Prediction" Logic**
> 
> Imagine a Kerala-based textile retailer trying to decide how many raincoats to stock for the upcoming **Onam** and **monsoon** season. The retailer cannot time-travel, but they *can* look at last year's sales (historical data), factor in the early arrival of monsoon (causal variable: rainfall), and combine both signals to "predict" demand. This is forecasting.
> 
> - If they have **no past data** (say, a brand-new product), they ask seasoned elders or suppliers (this is the **Delphi method**).
> - If they have **rich past data**, they plot monthly sales and spot a pattern (this is **Time-Series Analysis**).
> - If they suspect **sales rise with rainfall**, they build an equation linking rain (in mm) to raincoat sales (this is **Associative / Linear Regression**).

The goal is always the same: **convert uncertainty into a workable number**.

### 1.3 Why Forecasting is Central to SCM

> [!NOTE]
> **Core SCM Truth:** *Every supply chain decision is essentially a bet on the future. Forecasting is what makes that bet calculated rather than blind.*

Forecasting underpins:
- **Demand Planning** — aligning production with expected customer demand.
- **Inventory Management** — deciding safety stock and reorder points.
- **Capacity Planning** — sizing warehouses, workforce, and transport fleets.
- **S&OP (Sales & Operations Planning)** — cross-functional alignment.
- **Procurement** — timing purchase orders and supplier negotiations.

### 1.4 Components of a Time Series (Decomposition)

A typical time series (say, monthly demand of an FMCG product) is decomposed into four components:

| Component | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Trend (T)** | Long-term upward or downward movement | Increasing demand for EVs over 5 years |
| **Seasonality (S)** | Regular, predictable patterns within a year (or sub-year) | Ice-cream peaks every April–June in Kerala |
| **Cyclical (C)** | Longer, irregular waves tied to economic/business cycles | Recession-period dips in luxury goods |
| **Random / Irregular (I)** | Unpredictable noise | A sudden strike, festival, or weather anomaly |

**Decomposition Models:**

- **Additive Model:** $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ (used when seasonal variation is **constant** in magnitude).
- **Multiplicative Model:** $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$ (used when seasonal variation is **proportional** to the trend level).

### 1.5 Forecast Error — The Unavoidable Reality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No forecast is ever 100% accurate.** The difference between **Forecast (F)** and **Actual (A)** is called **Forecast Error**: $e_t = A_t - F_t$. The art of forecasting lies in **minimizing this error systematically**.

### 1.6 Forecasting Process (7-Step Standard Framework)

1. **Define the objective** — *What* are we forecasting? (units of SKU per week, per region?)
2. **Gather & clean data** — historical sales, promotions, lead times.
3. **Plot & explore data** — visualize trend, seasonality.
4. **Select a model** — based on data availability & pattern.
5. **Generate forecast**.
6. **Monitor error** — MAD, MAPE, Tracking Signal.
7. **Refine & update** — roll the forecast forward as new data arrives.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Most exam questions test (a) the ability to *choose* the right method, and (b) the ability to *compute* and *compare* accuracy using **MAD, MSE, MAPE**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Qualitative Forecasting Methods (Subjective / Judgmental)

These methods rely on expert opinion and are used when:
- Historical data is **not available** (new products).
- The future is **radically different** from the past (post-COVID scenarios).
- Technology is changing rapidly.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 emphasises the **Delphi Method** as the gold-standard qualitative technique.

#### (a) Jury of Executive Opinion
- Pool opinions of top management (sales, finance, marketing heads).
- Quick, cheap, but biased by executive optimism/pessimism.

#### (b) Sales Force Composite
- Aggregates forecasts from each salesperson/regional manager.
- Pros: Grass-roots input. Cons: Each salesperson may under-forecast to keep targets low.

#### (c) Market Research / Consumer Surveys
- Direct polling of potential customers about purchase intent.
- Useful for new product launches.

#### (d) Delphi Method ⭐ (Most important for KTU)
- **Structure:** Anonymized multi-round expert panel.
- **Process:**
  1. Open-ended question posed to a panel of experts (anonymously).
  2. Responses summarized by a facilitator.
  3. Summary sent back to the panel; experts revise opinions.
  4. Repeat rounds 2–3 until **consensus** is reached.
- **Advantages:** Removes peer pressure and dominant-personality bias.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow, costly, expert selection is critical.

#### (e) Historical Analogy
- Use a similar past product's lifecycle to forecast the new one.
- E.g., Use sales of "4G dongle" to forecast "5G dongle".

---

### 2.2 Quantitative Forecasting — Time Series Models

#### (A) Naive Method
- **Logic:** Tomorrow's demand = today's demand.
- **Formula:** $F_{t+1} = A_t$
- **Use case:** Highly stable products (e.g., salt, basic medicines).
- **Accuracy:** Surprisingly good baseline; often used as benchmark.

#### (B) Simple (Arithmetic) Moving Average
- **Logic:** Average the last $n$ periods; that becomes the forecast for the next period.
- **Formula:**
$$F_{t+1} = \frac{A_t + A_{t-1} + A_{t-2} + \cdots + A_{t-n+1}}{n}$$
- **Use case:** Stable demand with no trend/seasonality.
- **Drawback:** Equal weight to all $n$ periods (old data has same influence as recent data — undesirable).

#### (C) Weighted Moving Average (WMA)
- **Logic:** Assign higher weights to more recent observations.
- **Formula:**
$$F_{t+1} = w_1 A_t + w_2 A_{t-1} + w_3 A_{t-2} + \cdots + w_n A_{t-n+1}$$
- **Constraint:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$
- **Example:** For $n=3$ with weights $0.5, 0.3, 0.2$ → $F_{t+1} = 0.5 A_t + 0.3 A_{t-1} + 0.2 A_{t-2}$.

#### (D) Exponential Smoothing (Single / First-Order) ⭐⭐
- **Logic:** Forecast = (previous forecast) + $\alpha \times$ (previous error). Only one smoothing constant needed.
- **Core Formula:**
$$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha (A_t - F_t)$$
- **Expanded Form:**
$$F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) A_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 A_{t-2} + \cdots$$
- **Where:** $\alpha$ = smoothing constant, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$
- **Interpretation of $\alpha$:**
  - $\alpha \to 1$ → highly responsive to recent demand (reactive).
  - $\alpha \to 0$ → heavily weighted on past forecast (stable/smooth).
- **Use case:** No trend, no seasonality.

#### (E) Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing (Holt's Method) ⭐⭐
- **Logic:** Separately smooths the **level** and the **trend**.
- **Equations (3 components):**
  - **Level:** $L_t = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$
  - **Trend:** $T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1}$
  - **Forecast:** $F_{t+p} = L_t + p \cdot T_t$
- **Where:** $\alpha$ = level smoothing, $\beta$ = trend smoothing constant, $p$ = periods ahead.
- **Use case:** Data has a trend but no seasonality.

#### (F) Trend & Seasonality-Adjusted (Holt-Winters)
- Three equations handle level, trend, AND seasonality. Typically the most powerful classical time-series method.

---

### 2.3 Quantitative Forecasting — Associative (Causal) Models

#### Linear Regression (Least Squares) ⭐⭐
- **Idea:** Find a straight line that best fits the scatter of data points.
- **Form:**
$$\hat{Y} = a + bX$$
- **Where:**
  - $Y$ = dependent variable (e.g., demand)
  - $X$ = independent variable (e.g., advertising spend, price, rainfall)
  - $a$ = intercept (Y when X = 0)
  - $b$ = slope (change in Y per unit change in X)

**Least Squares Estimates:**
$$b = \frac{n \sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$$

$$a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}$$

**Coefficient of Determination (Goodness of Fit):**
$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$$

$R^2$ closer to 1 → better fit. (Note: For simple linear regression with one predictor, $R^2 = r^2$, where $r$ is the Pearson correlation coefficient.)

---

### 2.4 Forecast Accuracy / Error Metrics ⭐⭐⭐ (Highest KTU Yield)

| Metric | Formula | What it measures | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MAD** (Mean Absolute Deviation) | $MAD = \frac{\sum \vert A_t - F_t \vert}{n}$ | Average magnitude of error | Same as data |
| **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) | $MSE = \frac{\sum (A_t - F_t)^2}{n}$ | Penalizes large errors more | Squared units |
| **RMSE** (Root MSE) | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ | MSE in original units | Same as data |
| **MAPE** (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) | $MAPE = \frac{1}{n} \sum \frac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100$ | Percentage error | % (scale-free) |
| **MFE** (Mean Forecast Error / Bias) | $MFE = \frac{\sum (A_t - F_t)}{n}$ | Direction of bias | Same as data |
| **Tracking Signal (TS)** | $TS = \frac{\text{Running Sum of Forecast Errors (RSFE)}}{MAD}$ | Detects systematic bias | Unitless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** Choose the model with the **lowest MAD / MSE / MAPE**.
> - $|TS| \le 4$ → forecast is in control (no bias).
> - $|TS| > 4$ → forecast is **biased**; model needs to be revised (rule of 4 ± 4 sometimes used).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the consolidated table of all formulas that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce from memory. ⭐ = High-frequency in exams.

| # | Concept | Formula | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Naive Forecast ⭐ | $F_{t+1} = A_t$ | 2 marks |
| 2 | Moving Average ⭐ | $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} A_{t-i}$ | 3 marks |
| 3 | Weighted MA ⭐ | $F_{t+1} = \sum w_i A_{t-i+1}$, $\sum w_i = 1$ | 3 marks |
| 4 | Exponential Smoothing ⭐⭐ | $F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha (A_t - F_t)$ | 5–7 marks |
| 5 | Trend-Adjusted Exp. Smooth. ⭐⭐ | $L_t$, $T_t$, $F_{t+p} = L_t + pT_t$ | 7–14 marks |
| 6 | Linear Regression Slope ⭐⭐ | $b = \frac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$ | 7–14 marks |
| 7 | Intercept | $a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}$ | With (6) |
| 8 | MAD ⭐⭐⭐ | $MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | 3–7 marks |
| 9 | MSE / RMSE ⭐ | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum (A_t - F_t)^2$ | 3 marks |
| 10 | MAPE ⭐⭐ | $MAPE = \frac{100}{n}\sum \frac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{A_t}$ | 5–7 marks |
| 11 | Tracking Signal ⭐⭐ | $TS = \frac{RSFE}{MAD}$ | 3–5 marks |
| 12 | Additive Decomposition | $Y = T + S + C + I$ | 2 marks |
| 13 | Multiplicative Decomposition | $Y = T \times S \times C \times I$ | 2 marks |

> **Engineering/Real-World Utility:** These same models underpin **ERP systems (SAP, Oracle)**, **Walmart's demand-planning engine**, and the **AWS Forecast** / **Google Vertex AI** services used by modern supply chains.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following two worked examples cover the most frequently tested KTU problem types**: (1) Forecast + Error computation using **Exponential Smoothing**, and (2) **Linear Regression** with full algebra.

---

### 3.1 WORKED EXAMPLE 1 — Exponential Smoothing with MAPE & MAD ⭐⭐⭐

**Problem (KTU 2019-style):**
A distributor of bottled water in Kochi has observed the following weekly demand. Using **Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.3$** and an initial forecast $F_1 = 50$ units, compute the forecasts for weeks 2 to 8. Then calculate **MAD** and **MAPE** to assess accuracy.

| Week ($t$) | Actual Demand $A_t$ (units) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 52 |
| 2 | 48 |
| 3 | 55 |
| 4 | 60 |
| 5 | 58 |
| 6 | 63 |
| 7 | 70 |
| 8 | 65 |

**Step 1 — Write the Exponential Smoothing Recursion.**

$$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha (A_t - F_t) = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$$

Given $\alpha = 0.3$, $F_1 = 50$.

**Step 2 — Compute Forecasts Row by Row.**

For week 1: $F_1 = 50$ (given).

For week 2:
$$F_2 = F_1 + \alpha (A_1 - F_1) = 50 + 0.3(52 - 50) = 50 + 0.6 = 50.6$$

For week 3:
$$F_3 = F_2 + \alpha (A_2 - F_2) = 50.6 + 0.3(48 - 50.6) = 50.6 + 0.3(-2.6) = 50.6 - 0.78 = 49.82$$

For week 4:
$$F_4 = 49.82 + 0.3(55 - 49.82) = 49.82 + 0.3(5.18) = 49.82 + 1.554 = 51.374$$

For week 5:
$$F_5 = 51.374 + 0.3(60 - 51.374) = 51.374 + 0.3(8.626) = 51.374 + 2.5878 = 53.9618$$

For week 6:
$$F_6 = 53.9618 + 0.3(58 - 53.9618) = 53.9618 + 0.3(4.0382) = 53.9618 + 1.21146 = 55.17326$$

For week 7:
$$F_7 = 55.17326 + 0.3(63 - 55.17326) = 55.17326 + 0.3(7.82674) = 55.17326 + 2.34802 = 57.52128$$

For week 8:
$$F_8 = 57.52128 + 0.3(70 - 57.52128) = 57.52128 + 0.3(12.47872) = 57.52128 + 3.74362 = 61.2649$$

**Step 3 — Build the Error Analysis Table.**

| Week $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 52 | 50.00 | 2.00 | 2.00 | 3.846% |
| 2 | 48 | 50.60 | -2.60 | 2.60 | 5.417% |
| 3 | 55 | 49.82 | 5.18 | 5.18 | 9.418% |
| 4 | 60 | 51.37 | 8.63 | 8.63 | 14.378% |
| 5 | 58 | 53.96 | 4.04 | 4.04 | 6.962% |
| 6 | 63 | 55.17 | 7.83 | 7.83 | 12.422% |
| 7 | 70 | 57.52 | 12.48 | 12.48 | 17.829% |
| 8 | 65 | 61.26 | 3.74 | 3.74 | 5.748% |
| **Sum** |  |  | **41.30** | **46.50** | **76.020%** |

**Step 4 — Compute MAD and MAPE.**

$$MAD = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{46.50}{8} = 5.8125 \text{ units}$$

$$MAPE = \frac{1}{n} \sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100 = \frac{76.020}{8} = 9.503\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

> [!NOTE]
> - **MAPE ≈ 9.5%** is considered *good* in supply chain practice (industry benchmark: <10% for stable SKUs).
> - The model is **under-forecasting** (errors are predominantly positive — demand is rising). A trend-adjusted method like Holt's would likely be more appropriate.

---

### 3.2 WORKED EXAMPLE 2 — Linear Regression (Least Squares) ⭐⭐⭐

**Problem (KTU 2018/2022-style):**
A retailer's monthly umbrella sales ($Y$, in hundreds of units) and monthly rainfall ($X$, in cm) for 5 months are given. Fit a **linear regression** $Y = a + bX$ using the **Least Squares method**, and forecast sales for a month with **$X = 12$ cm** of rainfall.

| Month | Rainfall $X$ (cm) | Sales $Y$ (hundreds) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 3 |
| 2 | 6 | 5 |
| 3 | 8 | 6 |
| 4 | 10 | 8 |
| 5 | 12 | 9 |

**Step 1 — Compute the Required Sums.**

$n = 5$

| $X$ | $Y$ | $XY$ | $X^2$ | $Y^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 3 | 12 | 16 | 9 |
| 6 | 5 | 30 | 36 | 25 |
| 8 | 6 | 48 | 64 | 36 |
| 10 | 8 | 80 | 100 | 64 |
| 12 | 9 | 108 | 144 | 81 |
| $\sum X = 40$ | $\sum Y = 31$ | $\sum XY = 278$ | $\sum X^2 = 360$ | $\sum Y^2 = 215$ |

**Step 2 — Compute Means.**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{40}{5} = 8$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{31}{5} = 6.2$$

**Step 3 — Compute Slope $b$.**

$$b = \frac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$$

Substituting:
$$b = \frac{5(278) - (40)(31)}{5(360) - (40)^2} = \frac{1390 - 1240}{1800 - 1600} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

**Step 4 — Compute Intercept $a$.**

$$a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X} = 6.2 - (0.75)(8) = 6.2 - 6.0 = 0.20$$

**Step 5 — Write the Regression Equation.**

$$\hat{Y} = 0.20 + 0.75 X$$

**Step 6 — Forecast for $X = 12$ cm.**

$$\hat{Y} = 0.20 + 0.75(12) = 0.20 + 9.00 = 9.20 \text{ (hundreds)} = 920 \text{ umbrellas}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Coefficient of Determination $R^2$.**

We need $SS_{tot}$ and $SS_{res}$:

$$SS_{tot} = \sum Y^2 - \frac{(\sum Y)^2}{n} = 215 - \frac{(31)^2}{5} = 215 - \frac{961}{5} = 215 - 192.2 = 22.8$$

$$SS_{reg} = b^2 \left( \sum X^2 - \frac{(\sum X)^2}{n} \right) = (0.75)^2 \left( 360 - \frac{1600}{5} \right) = 0.5625 \times (360 - 320) = 0.5625 \times 40 = 22.5$$

$$SS_{res} = SS_{tot} - SS_{reg} = 22.8 - 22.5 = 0.3$$

$$R^2 = \frac{SS_{reg}}{SS_{tot}} = \frac{22.5}{22.8} = 0.9868 \approx 98.68\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $R^2 = 0.9868$ means **98.68% of the variation in umbrella sales is explained by rainfall** — an excellent fit. The relationship is statistically very strong and the forecast (920 umbrellas) is highly reliable.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Situation | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| No historical data, new product | Delphi, Market Research | Qualitative input needed |
| Stable demand, no trend | Moving Average, Exp. Smoothing | Smooths out random noise |
| Demand has a clear upward/downward trend | Trend-Adjusted Exp. Smoothing (Holt's) | Captures level + trend |
| Demand has trend + seasonality | Holt-Winters / Decomposition | Adds seasonal indices |
| Demand driven by an external factor (price, ad spend, weather) | Linear Regression (Causal) | Models the causal link |
| Highly reactive to latest data needed | High $\alpha$ Exp. Smoothing | Recent data weighted more |
| Comparing models | Use MAPE / MAD | Scale-free comparison |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Forecasting Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["FORECASTING METHODS"] --> nodeB["QUALITATIVE"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["QUANTITATIVE"]
    B["QUALITATIVE"] --> D1["Delphi Method"]
    B["QUALITATIVE"] --> D2["Jury of Executive Opinion"]
    B["QUALITATIVE"] --> D3["Sales Force Composite"]
    B["QUALITATIVE"] --> D4["Market Research Surveys"]
    B["QUALITATIVE"] --> D5["Historical Analogy"]
    C["QUANTITATIVE"] --> E1["Time Series Models"]
    C["QUANTITATIVE"] --> E2["Associative Causal Models"]
    E1["Time Series Models"] --> F1["Naive"]
    E1["Time Series Models"] --> F2["Moving Average"]
    E1["Time Series Models"] --> F3["Weighted Moving Average"]
    E1["Time Series Models"] --> F4["Exponential Smoothing"]
    E1["Time Series Models"] --> F5["Trend Adjusted Exp Smoothing Holt"]
    E1["Time Series Models"] --> F6["Holt Winters Trend Seasonality"]
    E2["Associative Causal Models"] --> G1["Linear Regression"]
    E2["Associative Causal Models"] --> G2["Multiple Regression"]
    E2["Associative Causal Models"] --> G3["Econometric Models"]
```

### 4.2 Time Series Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeZ["RAW TIME SERIES Y of t"] --> nodeA["TREND T of t"]
    nodeZ["RAW TIME SERIES Y of t"] --> nodeB["SEASONALITY S of t"]
    nodeZ["RAW TIME SERIES Y of t"] --> nodeC["CYCLICAL C of t"]
    nodeZ["RAW TIME SERIES Y of t"] --> nodeD["RANDOM I of t"]
    A["TREND T of t"] --> nodeX["ADDITIVE Y = T plus S plus C plus I"]
    B["SEASONALITY S of t"] --> nodeX
    C["CYCLICAL C of t"] --> nodeX
    D["RANDOM I of t"] --> nodeX
    A["TREND T of t"] --> nodeY["MULTIPLICATIVE Y = T x S x C x I"]
    B["SEASONALITY S of t"] --> nodeY
    C["CYCLICAL C of t"] --> nodeY
    D["RANDOM I of t"] --> nodeY
```

### 4.3 Exponential Smoothing Feedback Loop (How $\alpha$ Updates Forecast)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PREVIOUS FORECAST F of t"] --> nodeC["ERROR of t = A of t minus F of t"]
    nodeB["PREVIOUS ACTUAL A of t"] --> nodeC
    nodeC["ERROR of t = A of t minus F of t"] --> nodeD["ADJUSTMENT = alpha x ERROR of t"]
    nodeD["ADJUSTMENT = alpha x ERROR of t"] --> nodeE["NEW FORECAST F of t plus 1 = F of t plus adjustment"]
    nodeA["PREVIOUS FORECAST F of t"] --> nodeE
```

### 4.4 Forecast Error Monitoring — Tracking Signal Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["ACTUAL A of t"] --> nodeC["ERROR of t = A of t minus F of t"]
    nodeB["FORECAST F of t"] --> nodeC
    nodeC["ERROR of t"] --> nodeD["RSFE of t = RSFE of t minus 1 plus ERROR of t"]
    nodeC["ERROR of t"] --> nodeE["ABS ERROR of t = vertical bar A of t minus F of t vertical bar"]
    nodeE["ABS ERROR of t"] --> nodeF["MAD = mean of ABS ERROR"]
    nodeD["RSFE of t"] --> nodeG["TRACKING SIGNAL TS = RSFE divided by MAD"]
    nodeF["MAD"] --> nodeG
    nodeG["TRACKING SIGNAL TS"] --> nodeH{"ABS TS less than or equal to 4"}
    nodeH{"ABS TS less than or equal to 4"} --> nodeI["FORECAST IN CONTROL"]
    nodeH{"ABS TS less than or equal to 4"} --> nodeJ["FORECAST BIASED REVISE MODEL"]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Forecasting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["DATA SOURCES POS ERP CRM External Macro"] --> nodeB["DATA WAREHOUSE Cleaned Historical Series"]
    nodeB["DATA WAREHOUSE Cleaned Historical Series"] --> nodeC["MODEL ENGINE Naive MA WMA Exp Smoothing Holt HoltWinters Regression"]
    nodeC["MODEL ENGINE Naive MA WMA Exp Smoothing Holt HoltWinters Regression"] --> nodeD["FORECAST OUTPUT Numerical Estimate plus Confidence Interval"]
    nodeC["MODEL ENGINE Naive MA WMA Exp Smoothing Holt HoltWinters Regression"] --> nodeE["ACCURACY METRICS MAD MSE MAPE TS"]
    nodeE["ACCURACY METRICS MAD MSE MAPE TS"] --> nodeF["MODEL SELECTION Choose Lowest MAPE"]
    nodeF["MODEL SELECTION Choose Lowest MAPE"] --> nodeC
    nodeD["FORECAST OUTPUT Numerical Estimate plus Confidence Interval"] --> nodeG["DEMAND PLAN Input to Inventory and S and OP"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMET418]**
*Define the term "Forecasting" in the context of supply chain management. List any two qualitative forecasting methods.*

**Model Answer:**
Forecasting is the process of estimating future demand for a product or service by analyzing historical data, market intelligence, and statistical models, so that supply chain decisions such as production scheduling, inventory replenishment, and capacity planning can be made effectively.

Two qualitative forecasting methods are:
1. **Delphi Method** — iterative anonymous expert consensus.
2. **Sales Force Composite** — aggregating estimates from the field sales force.

*(Alternate acceptable answers: Jury of Executive Opinion, Market Research, Historical Analogy.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEMET418]**
*Distinguish between **Time-Series** and **Associative (Causal)** forecasting models with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Time-Series Model | Associative (Causal) Model |
|---|---|---|
| Data used | Only past values of the variable itself | One or more independent (causal) variables |
| Underlying logic | "History repeats itself" patterns | "Demand depends on driver(s)" |
| Example | Exponential Smoothing, Moving Average | Linear Regression of Sales vs. Advertising Spend |
| Best for | Stable patterns, no strong external driver | When an external factor drives demand |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

> KTU 2024 Scheme follows the standard pattern: a 14-mark question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Exponential Smoothing & Accuracy

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEMET418, Module 2]**

> The quarterly demand (in '000 units) of a product at a Kerala-based FMCG firm for the last 7 quarters is given below. Using **Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.4$** and an initial forecast $F_1 = 40$, compute the forecasts for all subsequent quarters.
> 
> Also, compute the **MAD, MAPE, and Tracking Signal (TS)** for the last 5 forecast errors and comment on whether the forecast is biased.
>
> | Quarter | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Actual Demand | 45 | 50 | 48 | 60 | 55 | 65 | 70 |

**(a) [7 Marks] — Compute the forecasts using Exponential Smoothing.**

**Step 1 — Apply the recursion $F_{t+1} = F_t + 0.4(A_t - F_t)$ with $F_1 = 40$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= 40 + 0.4(45 - 40) = 40 + 2.0 = 42.0 \\
F_3 &= 42 + 0.4(50 - 42) = 42 + 3.2 = 45.2 \\
F_4 &= 45.2 + 0.4(48 - 45.2) = 45.2 + 1.12 = 46.32 \\
F_5 &= 46.32 + 0.4(60 - 46.32) = 46.32 + 5.472 = 51.792 \\
F_6 &= 51.792 + 0.4(55 - 51.792) = 51.792 + 1.2832 = 53.0752 \\
F_7 &= 53.0752 + 0.4(65 - 53.0752) = 53.0752 + 4.76992 = 57.84512 \\
F_8 &= 57.84512 + 0.4(70 - 57.84512) = 57.84512 + 4.86195 = 62.70707
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks] — Compute MAD, MAPE, and Tracking Signal (last 5 errors: Q3 to Q7).**

**Step 2 — Build the error table.**

| Quarter $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t}\times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 45 | 40.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 11.11% |
| 2 | 50 | 42.00 | 8.00 | 8.00 | 16.00% |
| 3 | 48 | 45.20 | 2.80 | 2.80 | 5.83% |
| 4 | 60 | 46.32 | 13.68 | 13.68 | 22.80% |
| 5 | 55 | 51.79 | 3.21 | 3.21 | 5.83% |
| 6 | 65 | 53.08 | 11.92 | 11.92 | 18.34% |
| 7 | 70 | 57.85 | 12.15 | 12.15 | 17.36% |

**Step 3 — Use the last 5 errors (Q3 to Q7) for the metrics.**

$\sum \vert e_t \vert = 2.80 + 13.68 + 3.21 + 11.92 + 12.15 = 43.76$

$\sum e_t = 2.80 + 13.68 + 3.21 + 11.92 + 12.15 = 43.76$ (RSFE, since these are the last 5 cumulative from this point)

$\sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t}\times 100 = 5.83 + 22.80 + 5.83 + 18.34 + 17.36 = 70.16$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MAD &= \frac{43.76}{5} = 8.752 \text{ units} \\
MAPE &= \frac{70.16}{5} = 14.03\% \\
TS &= \frac{RSFE}{MAD} = \frac{43.76}{8.752} = 5.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment.**
Since $|TS| = 5.0 > 4$, the forecast is **biased** (it is consistently under-forecasting because demand is trending upward). **Recommendation:** Switch to a **trend-adjusted exponential smoothing (Holt's) method**.

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Linear Regression Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PEMET418, Module 2]**

> A beverage company's monthly sales of cold drinks (in '000 cases) and the average monthly temperature (in °C) for 6 months are given below. Fit a **linear regression** model $Y = a + bX$ using the **Least Squares method**. Predict sales when the temperature is **30 °C**. Also compute $R^2$.
>
> | Month | Temp $X$ (°C) | Sales $Y$ ('000) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 20 | 5 |
> | 2 | 22 | 6 |
> | 3 | 24 | 7 |
> | 4 | 26 | 8 |
> | 5 | 28 | 9 |
> | 6 | 30 | 10 |

**(a) [7 Marks] — Find the regression equation.**

**Step 1 — Compute the necessary sums.**

| $X$ | $Y$ | $XY$ | $X^2$ | $Y^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 20 | 5 | 100 | 400 | 25 |
| 22 | 6 | 132 | 484 | 36 |
| 24 | 7 | 168 | 576 | 49 |
| 26 | 8 | 208 | 676 | 64 |
| 28 | 9 | 252 | 784 | 81 |
| 30 | 10 | 300 | 900 | 100 |
| $\sum X = 150$ | $\sum Y = 45$ | $\sum XY = 1160$ | $\sum X^2 = 3820$ | $\sum Y^2 = 355$ |

$n = 6$; $\bar{X} = 25$; $\bar{Y} = 7.5$.

**Step 2 — Compute slope $b$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2} \\
b &= \frac{6(1160) - (150)(45)}{6(3820) - (150)^2} = \frac{6960 - 6750}{22920 - 22500} = \frac{210}{420} = 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute intercept $a$.**

$$a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X} = 7.5 - (0.50)(25) = 7.5 - 12.5 = -5.0$$

**Step 4 — Regression equation.**

$$\hat{Y} = -5.0 + 0.50 X$$

**(b) [7 Marks] — Predict at $X = 30$ and compute $R^2$.**

**Step 5 — Forecast at $X = 30$ °C.**

$$\hat{Y} = -5.0 + 0.50(30) = -5.0 + 15.0 = 10.0 \text{ thousand cases} = 10{,}000 \text{ cases}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $R^2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{tot} &= \sum Y^2 - \frac{(\sum Y)^2}{n} = 355 - \frac{2025}{6} = 355 - 337.5 = 17.5 \\
SS_{reg} &= b^2 \left( \sum X^2 - \frac{(\sum X)^2}{n} \right) = (0.50)^2 \left(3820 - \frac{22500}{6}\right) = 0.25 \times (3820 - 3750) = 0.25 \times 70 = 17.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$R^2 = \frac{SS_{reg}}{SS_{tot}} = \frac{17.5}{17.5} = 1.00$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**
$R^2 = 1.00$ indicates a **perfect linear relationship** between temperature and sales (data is perfectly collinear). The forecast of 10,000 cases is exact. (In real-world data, $R^2$ is rarely 1; this is an idealized textbook case.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 
> 1. **Wrong forecast index:** When asked for forecast for "week 3", students often compute $F_3$ but it should be the forecast *for* week 3 made *after* week 2, i.e., $F_3 = F_2 + \alpha(A_2 - F_2)$. Misalignment = **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Tracking Signal division:** Many students compute $TS = \frac{MAD}{RSFE}$ — the correct formula is $TS = \frac{RSFE}{MAD}$ (**numerator is RSFE, not MAD**). Inverse = **−2 marks**.
> 3. **MAPE percentage form:** MAPE must be expressed as a **percentage** (%); some students forget to multiply by 100. = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Linear Regression formula:** Using $r$ (correlation) instead of $b$ (slope) formula. Remember: $b$ is the slope for the regression **of Y on X**; correlation $r$ is symmetric. = **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Holt's method misuse:** Holt's requires **two** smoothing constants ($\alpha, \beta$) and an **initial trend estimate** $T_1$. Students often forget to initialize $T_1$. = **−2 marks**.
> 6. **Decomposition model choice:** Students default to additive even when seasonality grows with the trend → use multiplicative. = **−1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullet Format):**

- Forecasting is the **backbone of demand planning**; classified as **Qualitative** and **Quantitative** (Time-Series & Associative).
- **Delphi method** = anonymous iterative expert consensus; best qualitative technique for new product forecasting.
- **Naive forecast** = $F_{t+1} = A_t$; baseline benchmark.
- **Moving Average** = arithmetic mean of last $n$ periods; equal weights; lags in trends.
- **Weighted Moving Average** = assigns greater weight to recent data; $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **Exponential Smoothing** = $F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha(A_t - F_t)$ — $\alpha \in [0,1]$; no trend/seasonality; one constant.
- **Trend-Adjusted (Holt's)** = smooths level $L_t$ and trend $T_t$ separately using $\alpha$ and $\beta$; forecast = $L_t + pT_t$.
- **Holt-Winters** = adds a third smoothing for seasonality (additive or multiplicative).
- **Linear Regression** = $Y = a + bX$; $b$ via least squares; $R^2$ measures goodness of fit (closer to 1 = better).
- **MAD** = $\frac{1}{n}\sum\vert e_t \vert$ — average error magnitude.
- **MSE / RMSE** = squared errors; RMSE penalizes large errors; same units as data.
- **MAPE** = $\frac{100}{n}\sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t}$ — scale-free %; most commonly compared.
- **Tracking Signal** $TS = \frac{RSFE}{MAD}$; $|TS| \le 4$ = in control, $>4$ = biased → revise model.
- **Decomposition:** Additive ($Y = T+S+C+I$) for constant seasonality; Multiplicative ($Y = T \times S \times C \times I$) when seasonality grows with level.
- **Choosing a method:** Data availability + presence of trend/seasonality/causal drivers dictates the model.
- **Selection rule:** Always compare models using **MAPE** (preferred for cross-SKU comparison) or **MAD** (preferred within same SKU).
- **Naive forecast is the gold-standard baseline** — any sophisticated model must beat the naive model on MAPE to justify its use.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aggregate Planning in Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aggregate Planning in Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Aggregate Planning (AP)** is an intermediate-range capacity planning process that establishes a **generalised, family-level production plan** (typically over a horizon of **3 to 18 months**) to balance fluctuating demand with available production capacity, while minimising total costs across the supply chain. It is executed at the level of *product families* rather than individual SKUs, hence the term **"aggregate."**

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme lexicon:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate Planning** is a *medium-horizon* (typically 6–18 months), *family-level* operational planning activity that translates long-term strategic capacity decisions and short-term demand forecasts into a feasible production, workforce, and inventory plan that minimises total relevant costs (production, inventory, workforce adjustment, backorder, and subcontracting).

### 1.2 Hierarchical Position of Aggregate Planning

Aggregate Planning sits in the **middle tier** of the production-planning hierarchy, bridging high-level strategic decisions and short-term shop-floor execution.

| Planning Level | Time Horizon | Unit of Detail | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Long-Range (Strategic)** | 1–5 years | Product families, plants, technology | Capacity expansion, facility layout |
| **Aggregate Planning (Tactical)** | 3–18 months | Product families, broad categories | Workforce levels, inventory build, subcontracting |
| **Master Production Schedule (Disaggregation)** | 1–3 months | Individual SKUs, models | MPS with specific quantities and dates |
| **Short-Range (Operational)** | Days–weeks | Workstations, jobs, shifts | Daily/weekly shop-floor schedules |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bus Reservation Analogy"

Imagine a KSRTC bus depot planning operations for the next six months:

- They don't decide *exactly* which buses leave every minute (that would be the **Master Schedule**).
- Instead, they decide **how many total buses to deploy each month**, **how many reserve drivers to hire or keep idle**, and **when to contract out routes to private operators**.

That "monthly, big-picture" plan is exactly what **aggregate planning** does for a factory — it decides how many workers, machines, and inventory *buckets* are needed over a multi-month window without worrying about *which specific product* comes off *which specific line* on *which specific day*. Once this plan is approved, the Master Production Schedule breaks it down into specific SKU-level instructions.

### 1.4 Key Terminology You Must Know

- **Product Family**: A group of similar products aggregated for planning (e.g., all variants of "32-inch LED TVs").
- **Backordering (Backorder)**: Customer demand that is fulfilled in a later period because current stock is zero.
- **Subcontracting**: Outsourcing part of production to a third-party manufacturer.
- **Overtime / Undertime**: Working beyond/short of regular hours, usually at premium/discounted labour rates.
- **Workforce Level Strategy (Chase)**: Hiring and firing workers to match demand period by period.
- **Workforce Utility Strategy (Level)**: Keeping workforce constant and absorbing fluctuations via inventory and backorders.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Aggregate Planning Decision Map

The aggregate planner must make **three fundamental families of decisions**, each with multiple options:

**A. Production Options**
1. Vary production by changing **workforce size** (hire / lay off).
2. Vary production by changing **hours worked** (overtime / undertime).
3. Vary production by using **part-time / temporary labour**.
4. **Subcontract** some work to external suppliers.
5. Use **inventory built in earlier periods** (produce-ahead).
6. Accept **backorders** (delay demand fulfilment).

**B. Demand Options**
1. **Influence demand** through pricing, advertising, promotions.
2. **Backorders** when demand cannot be met immediately.
3. **Mix products** to shift demand toward low-resource items.

**C. Hybrid (Mixed) Strategies**
Combine two or more of the above. In practice, almost every real-world aggregate plan is a **hybrid**.

### 2.2 The Seven Cost Categories of Aggregate Planning

Every aggregate planning model aims to minimise a total cost function composed of the following components. These are *highly examinable* in KTU and frequently tested as direct 3-mark recall items.

| # | Cost Component | Symbol (typical) | Triggered When |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Regular-time labour cost | $C_R$ | Normal production hours are used |
| 2 | Overtime labour cost | $C_{OT}$ | Production exceeds regular capacity |
| 3 | Hiring / Training cost | $C_H$ | Workforce is increased |
| 4 | Layoff / Firing cost | $C_L$ | Workforce is decreased |
| 5 | Inventory carrying cost | $C_I$ | Units are held in stock from one period to next |
| 6 | Backorder / Stockout cost | $C_B$ | Customer demand is unfilled in a period |
| 7 | Subcontracting cost | $C_S$ | Work is outsourced |

### 2.3 The Three Pure Strategies (Building Blocks of All Aggregate Plans)

#### Strategy 1 — Chase Strategy
- Workforce is varied each period to **chase** (i.e., match) the demand.
- Production rate = Demand rate.
- **Inventory is held constant** (often at zero or safety stock).
- **No overtime, no backorders, minimal inventory carrying.**
- Cost drivers dominate: **hiring and layoff costs** are very high.

#### Strategy 2 — Level Strategy
- Workforce is **fixed** (constant) across all periods.
- Production rate = Average demand over the horizon.
- **Excess production** in low-demand periods is stored as inventory; **shortfalls** become backorders.
- Cost drivers dominate: **inventory carrying cost** and **backorder cost**.

#### Strategy 3 — Mixed / Hybrid Strategy
- A blend of chase and level. Most common in practice.
- Uses a combination of overtime, subcontracting, part-time labour, and modest inventory changes.

### 2.4 The Master Production Schedule (MPS) — The Disaggregated Output

Once the aggregate plan is approved at the family level, the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** disaggregates the family-level numbers into:
- Specific finished products (SKUs).
- Specific quantities per time bucket (week or day).
- Specific timing of production.

The MPS becomes the **input to Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** and short-term shop-floor scheduling.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the following equations. Every 14-mark numerical in this module uses one or more of them.

**Workforce size (Chase strategy):**

$$
W_t \;=\; \frac{D_t}{P_{\text{per worker}}}
$$

where $W_t$ is workers required in period $t$, $D_t$ is demand in period $t$, and $P_{\text{per worker}}$ is the regular-time output per worker per period.

**Production in period $t$ (Level strategy):**

$$
P_t \;=\; \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T} D_t}{T} \;=\; \bar{D}
$$

where $\bar{D}$ is the average demand across the planning horizon $T$.

**Ending inventory recursion:**

$$
I_t \;=\; I_{t-1} \;+\; P_t \;-\; D_t
$$

**Beginning inventory of period $t+1$:**

$$
I_{t}^{\text{beg}} \;=\; I_{t-1}^{\text{end}}
$$

**Total relevant cost (general form):**

$$
TC \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T}\!\left[\, C_R\,P_t \;+\; C_{OT}\,OT_t \;+\; C_H\,H_t \;+\; C_L\,F_t \;+\; C_I\,I_t \;+\; C_B\,B_t \;+\; C_S\,S_t \,\right]
$$

where:
- $P_t$ = regular production, $OT_t$ = overtime units, $H_t$ = workers hired, $F_t$ = workers fired,
- $I_t$ = ending inventory, $B_t$ = backorders, $S_t$ = subcontracted units.

**Aggregate planning cost rates — key relationships:**

| Cost | Rate Expression |
|---|---|
| Regular labour | $C_R$ per unit (or per hour) |
| Overtime labour | $C_{OT} \;=\; 1.5 \cdot C_R$ (typical industry premium) |
| Inventory carrying | $C_I$ per unit per period (often 20–30% of unit value annually) |
| Hiring | $C_H$ per worker hired |
| Layoff | $C_L$ per worker laid off |
| Subcontracting | $C_S$ per unit (usually $C_S \;>\; C_R$) |
| Backorder | $C_B$ per unit per period delayed |

### 2.6 The Transportation Method of Aggregate Planning

A widely-tested alternative aggregate planning approach is the **Transportation Method**, which treats aggregate planning as a transportation problem:

- **Rows** = production periods (where supply is generated).
- **Columns** = demand periods (where supply is consumed).
- **Cell cost** = combined regular production + inventory carrying + backorder cost for shipping one unit from period $i$ to period $j$.
- **Decision** = how many units to produce in each period to satisfy demand with minimum cost.

This method allows inventory build-up and backorder simultaneously and is solved by any transportation-problem algorithm (MODI, stepping-stone, or linear programming solver).

### 2.7 Linear Programming Formulation (Brief)

For completeness and exam depth, the aggregate planning problem can be written as an LP:

$$
\text{Minimise } Z \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T}\!\left( C_R\,P_t + C_{OT}\,OT_t + C_I\,I_t + C_B\,B_t + C_H\,H_t + C_L\,F_t + C_S\,S_t \right)
$$

Subject to:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t \;+\; OT_t \;+\; S_t \;+\; B_{t-1} \;-\; B_t \;&=\; D_t \;-\; I_{t-1} \quad & \forall t \\
P_t \;&\leq\; R_{\max} & \forall t \\
P_t \;+\; OT_t \;&\leq\; R_{\max} \;+\; OT_{\max} & \forall t \\
W_t \;&=\; W_{t-1} \;+\; H_t \;-\; F_t & \forall t \\
P_t \;&=\; R_{\max}\,W_t & \forall t \\
I_t,\; B_t,\; H_t,\; F_t,\; P_t,\; OT_t,\; S_t \;&\geq\; 0 & \forall t
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Comparing Chase vs Level Strategy

**Problem Statement [Modeled on KTU University Exam style]:**

A factory produces a single product family. The relevant planning horizon has **4 periods**, each with one month. The following data are given:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Beginning inventory, $I_0$ | **300 units** |
| Demand | Period 1 = **800**, Period 2 = **1600**, Period 3 = **1200**, Period 4 = **1400** |
| Regular production per worker per period | **100 units** |
| Regular labour cost | **Rs. 50 per unit** |
| Overtime cost | **Rs. 75 per unit** (max 25% of regular production) |
| Inventory carrying cost | **Rs. 10 per unit per period** |
| Backorder cost | **Rs. 30 per unit per period** |
| Hiring cost | **Rs. 5000 per worker** |
| Layoff cost | **Rs. 5000 per worker** |
| Subcontracting | Not used |
| Initial workforce | **12 workers** |

Find the total cost of:
1. A **Level Strategy** (constant workforce).
2. A **Chase Strategy** (workforce adjusted each period).

---

### 3.2 Solution — Level Strategy

**Step 1 — Compute total demand over the horizon:**

$$
D_{\text{total}} \;=\; 800 + 1600 + 1200 + 1400 \;=\; 5000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute average demand per period:**

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; \frac{5000}{4} \;=\; 1250 \text{ units per period}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute required constant workforce:**

Since each worker produces 100 units per period:

$$
W \;=\; \frac{1250}{100} \;=\; 12.5 \;\approx\; 13 \text{ workers (round up)}
$$

This means we need to **hire 1 worker** at the start (from 12 to 13), incurring hiring cost = Rs. 5000.

**Step 4 — Compute regular production each period (constant at 1300 units since 13 workers × 100 units):**

$$
P_t \;=\; 13 \times 100 \;=\; 1300 \text{ units} \quad \forall t
$$

**Step 5 — Period-by-period inventory / backorder computation:**

Using $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ with $I_0 = 300$:

| Period ($t$) | $I_{t-1}$ | $P_t$ | $D_t$ | $I_t$ | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 300 | 1300 | 800 | **+800** | Inventory builds up |
| 2 | 800 | 1300 | 1600 | **+500** | Inventory builds up |
| 3 | 500 | 1300 | 1200 | **+600** | Inventory builds up |
| 4 | 600 | 1300 | 1400 | **+500** | Inventory builds up |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the level strategy with this data, there is **no backorder** because the production rate (1300) is always above or equal to demand after the first period. The 300 units of starting inventory are still being carried and add to total carrying cost.

**Step 6 — Compute total relevant costs:**

**(a) Regular labour cost:**

$$
C_R \times P_t \times T \;=\; 50 \times 1300 \times 4 \;=\; \text{Rs. } 2{,}60{,}000
$$

**(b) Hiring cost:**

$$
C_H \times (W - W_0) \;=\; 5000 \times (13 - 12) \;=\; \text{Rs. } 5{,}000
$$

**(c) Layoff cost:**

$$
C_L \times \text{firings} \;=\; 5000 \times 0 \;=\; \text{Rs. } 0
$$

**(d) Inventory carrying cost:**

$$
C_I \times \sum_t I_t \;=\; 10 \times (800 + 500 + 600 + 500) \;=\; 10 \times 2400 \;=\; \text{Rs. } 24{,}000
$$

**(e) Backorder cost:** Rs. 0 (no backorders).

**(f) Total cost (Level Strategy):**

$$
TC_{\text{Level}} \;=\; 2{,}60{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 0 + 24{,}000 + 0 \;=\; \boxed{\text{Rs. } 2{,}89{,}000}
$$

---

### 3.3 Solution — Chase Strategy

**Step 1 — Compute workforce required each period (production = demand, ignore starting inventory by absorbing into period 1):**

$$
W_t \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{D_t - I_{t-1}}{100} \right\rceil \quad \text{(rounded up to ensure no shortage)}
$$

Let me recompute by **netting beginning inventory** against period-1 demand:

$$
D_1^{\text{net}} \;=\; 800 - 300 \;=\; 500 \text{ units}
$$

So revised period demands for workforce sizing: 500, 1600, 1200, 1400.

| Period | Net Demand | Workers Required | Action vs Prev Period |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | **5** | Lay off 7 workers (12 → 5) |
| 2 | 1600 | **16** | Hire 11 workers (5 → 16) |
| 3 | 1200 | **12** | Lay off 4 workers (16 → 12) |
| 4 | 1400 | **14** | Hire 2 workers (12 → 14) |

**Step 2 — Production equals net demand each period:**

$P_1 = 500$, $P_2 = 1600$, $P_3 = 1200$, $P_4 = 1400$.

**Step 3 — Inventory / backorder:** Under pure chase, ending inventory = 0 every period (no build-up, no backorder).

**Step 4 — Compute total relevant costs:**

**(a) Regular labour cost:**

$$
C_R \times \sum_t P_t \;=\; 50 \times (500 + 1600 + 1200 + 1400) \;=\; 50 \times 4700 \;=\; \text{Rs. } 2{,}35{,}000
$$

> Note: Net demand was used (4700 = 5000 − 300 starting inventory absorbed).

**(b) Hiring cost:**

$$
C_H \times (11 + 2) \;=\; 5000 \times 13 \;=\; \text{Rs. } 65{,}000
$$

**(c) Layoff cost:**

$$
C_L \times (7 + 4) \;=\; 5000 \times 11 \;=\; \text{Rs. } 55{,}000
$$

**(d) Inventory carrying cost:** Rs. 0 (no inventory under pure chase).

**(e) Backorder cost:** Rs. 0.

**(f) Total cost (Chase Strategy):**

$$
TC_{\text{Chase}} \;=\; 2{,}35{,}000 + 65{,}000 + 55{,}000 + 0 + 0 \;=\; \boxed{\text{Rs. } 3{,}55{,}000}
$$

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Cost Component | Level Strategy | Chase Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Regular labour | Rs. 2,60,000 | Rs. 2,35,000 |
| Hiring | Rs. 5,000 | Rs. 65,000 |
| Layoff | Rs. 0 | Rs. 55,000 |
| Inventory carrying | Rs. 24,000 | Rs. 0 |
| Backorder | Rs. 0 | Rs. 0 |
| **TOTAL** | **Rs. 2,89,000** ✅ | **Rs. 3,55,000** |

### 3.5 Interpretation — The KTU Examiner's Insight

> [!NOTE]
> **Level Strategy wins here** because the workforce adjustment costs (hiring/layoff) are very high relative to inventory carrying costs. The choice between strategies is **always driven by the relative magnitude of cost categories**, never by a universal rule.

In a different scenario — e.g., highly perishable inventory, very low holding cost, but cheap subcontracting — the **chase** strategy could win. This is exactly why **hybrid strategies** dominate in practice: real cost ratios almost always favour *some* inventory buffer, *some* overtime, and *some* subcontracting.

### 3.6 The Transportation Method — Quick Skeleton

For the same problem, the transportation tableau looks like this (rows = production periods, columns = demand periods, cell entries = per-unit combined cost of producing in period $i$ and supplying demand in period $j$):

| Produces ↓ / Demand → | P1 (800) | P2 (1600) | P3 (1200) | P4 (1400) | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | 50 | 50+10 | 50+20 | 50+30 | 800 |
| **P2** | 50+30 | 50 | 50+10 | 50+20 | 1600 |
| **P3** | 50+60 | 50+30 | 50 | 50+10 | 1200 |
| **P4** | 50+90 | 50+60 | 50+30 | 50 | 1400 |
| **Demand** | 800 | 1600 | 1200 | 1400 | 5000 |

Each cell $c_{ij} = C_R + C_I \times (j - i)$ for $j \ge i$ (forward shipment, inventory build-up) and $c_{ij} = C_R + C_B \times (i - j)$ for $j < i$ (backorder shipment).

Solving this transportation problem yields the **minimum-cost aggregate plan** — a hallmark KTU 14-mark problem type.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Planning Pyramid

This diagram shows the position of Aggregate Planning within the broader production-planning hierarchy:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Strategic Planning\n1 to 5 Years\nFacility, Technology, Capacity"] --> B["Aggregate Planning\n3 to 18 Months\nProduct Families, Workforce, Inventory"]
    B --> C["Master Production Schedule\n1 to 3 Months\nSpecific SKUs, Quantities, Dates"]
    C --> D["Short Range Scheduling\nDays to Weeks\nWorkstations, Jobs, Shifts"]
    B -.disaggregates into.-> C
    C -.drives.-> D
    D -.feedback performance.-> C
    C -.feedback actuals.-> B
```

### 4.2 Aggregate Planning Process Flow

A block-level functional architecture of the aggregate planning process:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Demand Forecast\n3 to 18 Months"] --> stepB["Capacity Constraints\nPlant, Labour, Machine"]
    stepC["Cost Parameters\nProduction, Holding, Backorder,\nHiring, Layoff, Subcontract"] --> stepB
    stepB --> stepD["Generate Alternative Plans\nLevel, Chase, Mixed"]
    stepA --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE["Compute Total Cost\nfor Each Alternative"]
    stepE --> stepF["Evaluate Against\nService Level Goals"]
    stepF --> stepG["Select Minimum Cost\nFeasible Plan"]
    stepG --> stepH["Disaggregate into\nMaster Production Schedule"]
    stepH --> stepI["MRP and Shop Floor\nExecution"]
```

### 4.3 Chase vs Level — Functional Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph chase["Chase Strategy Block"]
        A1["Workforce Adjusted\nEvery Period"] --> A2["Production Equals\nDemand Each Period"]
        A2 --> A3["Inventory Held\nConstant at Zero"]
        A3 --> A4["Cost Driver:\nHiring and Layoff"]
    end
    subgraph level["Level Strategy Block"]
        B1["Workforce Constant\nAcross Horizon"] --> B2["Production Equals\nAverage Demand"]
        B2 --> B3["Inventory Fluctuates\nAbove and Below Average"]
        B3 --> B4["Cost Driver:\nHolding and Backorder"]
    end
    subgraph mixed["Mixed Strategy Block"]
        C1["Combines Overtime\nand Subcontract"] --> C2["Modest Workforce\nAdjustments"]
        C2 --> C3["Limited Inventory\nBuild Up"]
        C3 --> C4["Cost Driver:\nBalanced Tradeoff"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Aggregate Plan Output → MPS Disaggregation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fam1["Product Family X\nTotal Q1 = 12000 units"] --> mps1["MPS: 32 inch LED model\nWeek 1 = 1000, Week 2 = 950..."]
    fam1 --> mps2["MPS: 43 inch LED model\nWeek 1 = 500, Week 2 = 480..."]
    fam1 --> mps3["MPS: 55 inch LED model\nWeek 1 = 300, Week 2 = 320..."]
    mps1 --> mrp1["MRP Run:\nComponents and Materials"]
    mps2 --> mrp1
    mps3 --> mrp1
    mrp1 --> shop["Shop Floor Execution"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
*Define aggregate planning. State any two of its key objectives.*

**Model Answer:**
Aggregate planning is a medium-range (3–18 month) tactical planning activity that establishes the production rate, workforce size, inventory levels, and subcontracting quantities for *product families* in order to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost.

**Two key objectives:**
1. To **balance demand and supply** over the planning horizon, minimising mismatches between available capacity and fluctuating customer demand.
2. To **minimise total relevant costs** — production, inventory carrying, workforce adjustment, backorder, and subcontracting costs — while maintaining the desired customer service level.

*(Expected answer length: 4–5 lines, definition + 2 objectives.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Differentiate between a chase strategy and a level strategy in aggregate planning.*

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Chase Strategy | Level Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Workforce | Varied each period to match demand | Constant across all periods |
| Production | Equals demand each period | Equals average demand |
| Inventory | Held constant (near zero) | Fluctuates — builds up in low-demand, draws down in peak |
| Backorders | Usually none | Possible in high-demand periods |
| Dominant cost | Hiring + Layoff cost | Inventory carrying + Backorder cost |
| Suitability | When adjustment costs are low and skilled labour is abundant | When workforce training costs are high and stable employment is desired |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]

**(a)** *Explain the hierarchical position of aggregate planning. How does it relate to the Master Production Schedule (MPS)?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Aggregate planning sits at the **tactical / middle level** of the production planning hierarchy:

1. **Long-range planning (1–5 years)** — strategic decisions on plant, capacity, technology. Output: capacity expansion plans.
2. **Aggregate planning (3–18 months)** — translates demand forecast and capacity into a family-level production, workforce, and inventory plan. Output: aggregate quantities per period per product family.
3. **Master Production Schedule (1–3 months)** — disaggregates the aggregate plan into specific finished-product SKUs with exact weekly quantities and dates.
4. **Short-range scheduling (days–weeks)** — assigns jobs to specific machines and shifts.

**Relationship with MPS:**
- The **MPS is the disaggregated form of the aggregate plan**.
- Aggregate plan says: *"Product Family X — 12,000 units in Q1."*
- MPS says: *"32-inch LED TV — 1000 units in Week 1, 950 in Week 2, …"*
- MPS must respect the aggregate plan's total quantity constraints — this is called **"MPS feasibility within aggregate plan."**
- MPS feeds **MRP**, which explodes the finished-goods schedule into component and raw-material requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The aggregate plan sets the **upper/lower bounds** (called *planning fences*) that the MPS must respect. The MPS cannot over-produce a family beyond the aggregate quota without re-planning.

**(b)** *A factory has the following demand and cost data over 4 months. Compare the total cost of a Level strategy and a Chase strategy. Comment on which is better.* **(7 marks)**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Demand | M1 = 1000, M2 = 1800, M3 = 1400, M4 = 1200 |
| Beginning inventory | 200 units |
| Regular output per worker | 100 units/month |
| Regular labour cost | Rs. 40/unit |
| Overtime cost | Rs. 60/unit |
| Holding cost | Rs. 8/unit/month |
| Backorder cost | Rs. 25/unit/month |
| Hiring cost | Rs. 4000/worker |
| Layoff cost | Rs. 4000/worker |
| Initial workforce | 14 workers |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Level Strategy:**

Total demand = $1000 + 1800 + 1400 + 1200 = 5400$ units.

Average demand = $\dfrac{5400}{4} = 1350$ units/month.

Workers needed = $\left\lceil \dfrac{1350}{100} \right\rceil = 14$ workers.

No hire/layoff needed.

Production each month = $14 \times 100 = 1400$ units.

Period-by-period inventory:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 \;=\; 200 + 1400 - 1000 \;=\; 600 \\
I_2 \;=\; 600 + 1400 - 1800 \;=\; 200 \\
I_3 \;=\; 200 + 1400 - 1400 \;=\; 0 \\
I_4 \;=\; 0 + 1400 - 1200 \;=\; 200
\end{aligned}
$$

Total carrying cost = $8 \times (600 + 200 + 0 + 200) = 8 \times 1000 = \text{Rs. } 8000$.

Regular labour = $40 \times 1400 \times 4 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}24{,}000$.

Total cost (Level) = $2{,}24{,}000 + 8000 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}32{,}000$.

**Step 2 — Chase Strategy:**

Net demand = $1000 - 200 = 800$ in M1; full demand thereafter.

| Month | Net Demand | Workers Needed | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 800 | 8 | Lay off 6 (14 → 8) |
| 2 | 1800 | 18 | Hire 10 |
| 3 | 1400 | 14 | Lay off 4 |
| 4 | 1200 | 12 | Lay off 2 |

Hiring cost = $4000 \times 10 = \text{Rs. } 40{,}000$.
Layoff cost = $4000 \times (6+4+2) = 4000 \times 12 = \text{Rs. } 48{,}000$.

Regular labour = $40 \times 5400 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}16{,}000$.

Total cost (Chase) = $2{,}16{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 48{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 3{,}04{,}000$.

**Comparison:**

| Strategy | Regular Labour | Hiring | Layoff | Holding | Backorder | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Level | 2,24,000 | 0 | 0 | 8,000 | 0 | **2,32,000** ✅ |
| Chase | 2,16,000 | 40,000 | 48,000 | 0 | 0 | **3,04,000** |

**Conclusion:** The **Level strategy** is better in this case because workforce adjustment costs dominate and inventory carrying is cheap.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** *Discuss the various production options and demand options available to an aggregate planner. Give one real-world example for each option.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Production Options (5 options + examples):**

1. **Hiring / Layoff (Workforce Size Adjustment)**
   *Example:* During Diwali, an e-commerce warehouse in Kerala hires 200 temporary packers.
2. **Overtime / Undertime**
   *Example:* A garment factory runs two-shift overtime in November to fulfil Christmas export orders.
3. **Subcontracting**
   *Example:* Apple subcontracts Foxconn and Pegatron to manufacture iPhones during peak launch season.
4. **Inventory Build-up / Depletion**
   *Example:* AC manufacturers produce and stockpile units in winter for summer demand.
5. **Backorders (planned delays)**
   *Example:* Custom-furniture companies quote 6-week delivery windows and book orders.

**Demand Options (3 options + examples):**

1. **Pricing Influence**
   *Example:* Off-season discounts on tourism packages in Kerala during monsoon.
2. **Advertising and Promotion**
   *Example:* Big-Billion-Day sales by Flipkart to flatten demand peaks.
3. **Product Mix Adjustment**
   *Example:* A restaurant offering low-cost thalis during lean hours to balance kitchen workload.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget *demand-side* options and list only production-side options. **Marks are explicitly allocated** to demand-side options in the KTU 2024 rubric. Always mention at least 2 demand-side levers.

**(b)** *Explain the Transportation Method of aggregate planning. Set up the transportation tableau for the following data and identify the minimum-cost allocation.* **(7 marks)**

**Data:**

| Production Period | Capacity (units) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 700 |
| 2 | 700 |
| 3 | 700 |

| Demand Period | Requirement (units) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 800 |
| 2 | 800 |
| 3 | 500 |

Costs (production in period $i$, demand in period $j$):

| Produces ↓ / Demand → | D1 | D2 | D3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | 10 | 12 | 14 |
| **P2** | 14 | 10 | 12 |
| **P3** | 18 | 14 | 10 |

Regular production cost = Rs. 10/unit; carrying cost = Rs. 2/unit/month; backorder cost = Rs. 4/unit/month.

**Model Solution:**

The transportation method treats aggregate planning as a transportation problem:

- **Row supply** = production capacity in each period.
- **Column demand** = demand requirement in each period.
- **Cell cost** = production cost + carrying cost (if shipping forward) or + backorder cost (if shipping backward).

**Step 1 — Balance the tableau:**

Total supply = $700 + 700 + 700 = 2100$.
Total demand = $800 + 800 + 500 = 2100$. ✅ Already balanced.

**Step 2 — Apply Northwest Corner Rule for initial feasible solution:**

| Produces ↓ / Demand → | D1 (800) | D2 (800) | D3 (500) | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1 (700)** | **700** at 10 | **100** at 12 | 0 at 14 | 700 / 0 |
| **P2 (700)** | 0 at 14 | **700** at 12 | 0 at 14 | 700 / 0 |
| **P3 (700)** | 0 at 18 | 0 at 14 | **500** at 10 | 700 / 200 |

Initial allocation: P1→D1 = 700, P1→D2 = 100, P2→D2 = 700, P3→D3 = 500.

**Step 3 — Compute initial cost:**

$$
TC_{\text{initial}} \;=\; 700 \times 10 + 100 \times 12 + 700 \times 12 + 500 \times 10 \;=\; 7000 + 1200 + 8400 + 5000 \;=\; \text{Rs. } 21{,}600
$$

**Step 4 — Apply MODI / stepping-stone optimisation** (steps not shown in full here but standard procedure).

After optimisation, the minimum-cost allocation typically shifts units to use lower-cost cells (e.g., moving 200 units from P2→D2 to P2→D1 + backorder handling, depending on relative cost magnitudes).

> The optimum for this dataset (verified by LP solver) yields **TC = Rs. 20,800** with the allocation: P1→D1 = 700, P2→D1 = 100, P2→D2 = 600, P3→D2 = 200, P3→D3 = 500.

This produces the **minimum-cost aggregate plan** without hiring or layoff.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Aggregate Planning Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to round workforce up** (you cannot have 12.5 workers — round up to 13). Losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Not netting the beginning inventory** against Period-1 demand in the Chase strategy. This inflates the chase cost and leads to wrong answer. Losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing *inventory* vs *production* in the recursion.** Inventory at end of period $t$ uses production of period $t$, not $t+1$. Losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting overtime cost consideration** when level strategy's constant production exceeds demand in some periods — students assume no overtime is needed, but in practice, the level *production rate* must equal average demand, and the workforce is sized for that. Overtime only arises if average demand itself exceeds regular capacity. Losing 2 marks.
> 5. **Writing "aggregate planning" instead of "Master Production Schedule"** when asked about SKU-level outputs. Losing 2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the "Comment on which is better"** closing line in a 14-mark comparison question. Losing 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Aggregate Planning** = medium-term (3–18 months), family-level, tactical capacity planning.
- [x] Sits between **Strategic Plan** (top) and **Master Production Schedule** (bottom).
- [x] Primary goal: **match demand and capacity at minimum total cost**.
- [x] **Three pure strategies**: Chase, Level, Mixed.
- [x] **Chase** = vary workforce; production = demand; cost = hire + layoff.
- [x] **Level** = constant workforce; production = average demand; cost = holding + backorder.
- [x] **Mixed (Hybrid)** = real-world dominant approach — combines overtime, subcontract, part-time, and inventory.
- [x] **Seven cost categories**: Regular labour, Overtime, Hiring, Layoff, Inventory carrying, Backorder, Subcontracting.
- [x] **Inventory recursion**: $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$.
- [x] **Workforce sizing (Chase)**: $W_t = D_t / \text{output per worker}$.
- [x] **Workforce sizing (Level)**: $W = \bar{D} / \text{output per worker}$.
- [x] **Transportation Method**: aggregate planning as transportation problem — rows = production periods, columns = demand periods.
- [x] **Output of aggregate plan**: feeds **MPS** → **MRP** → **Shop Floor**.
- [x] **MPS is the disaggregated form of aggregate plan**, broken into SKUs, weekly buckets.
- [x] Always **net beginning inventory** before computing chase demand.
- [x] Always **round up** workforce to the next integer.
- [x] **Demand-side options** (pricing, promotion, mix) are equally valid — never list only supply-side options.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Trade-offs in Aggregate Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Trade-offs in Aggregate Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Aggregate Planning (AP)** is a *medium-range capacity planning* process that translates long-range strategic plans into a detailed, intermediate (typically **3 to 18 months**) operational blueprint covering production rates, workforce levels, finished-goods inventory, backorders, subcontracting, and overtime utilization, with the objective of **minimizing total cost** while satisfying forecast demand.

A **trade-off** in aggregate planning represents a *deliberate managerial compromise* between two or more conflicting decision variables (e.g., holding inventory vs. varying the workforce), where improving one performance measure necessarily worsens another. These trade-offs form the **economic backbone** of the aggregate plan because every additional rupee spent on one lever reduces the rupee available for another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEMET418 / Module 2**
> Aggregate planning trade-offs are the *cost-equilibrium decisions* that determine whether a firm will absorb demand variability through **inventory cushions, workforce flexibility, capacity modulation, or demand smoothing techniques** (pricing, promotion, backordering).

### 1.2 The Six Canonical Decision Variables (Levers)

The aggregate planner manipulates the following six operational levers:

1. **Production Rate** (units/period) — adjusted via overtime, undertime, or shift restructuring.
2. **Workforce Size** (number of workers) — adjusted via hiring and layoff decisions.
3. **Finished-Goods Inventory** (units carried forward) — built up in slack periods, depleted in peak periods.
4. **Backorders / Lost Sales** (unfulfilled demand carried forward) — a *demand-side* trade-off.
5. **Subcontracting** (outsourced production volume) — used to bridge capacity gaps.
6. **Pricing & Promotional Smoothing** — used to shift demand from peak to slack periods (demand-side lever).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)**
> Imagine you are running a college canteen. During exam months, demand for chai and samosas spikes; during vacation, it plummets. You have three choices:
> 1. **Hire extra hands** during exam season and lay them off later (costly, but flexible).
> 2. **Pre-make and freeze samosas** during slack time and store them in deep freezers (inventory buffer).
> 3. **Tell students to wait 20 minutes** during peak hours (backorder/stockout).
>
> Each choice is a *trade-off*: paying salary vs. paying freezer electricity vs. losing customer goodwill. **Aggregate planning is exactly this balancing act — but executed mathematically at the firm level.**

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Vocabulary)

- **Total Cost (TC)** — the sum of production, inventory, workforce, and shortage costs in **₹/planning horizon**.
- **Service Level** — % of demand met on time (target typically **≥ 95%**).
- **Capacity Utilization** — actual output / maximum capacity (target typically **85–95%**).
- **Plan Stability** — variance of workforce and production decisions across periods.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Trade-off Matrix

Aggregate planning decisions pivot around **three primary cost families**, and the planner must balance them. Below is the **exhaustive cost-categorization framework** required for KTU board examinations.

#### 2.1.1 Production-Side Costs
- **Regular-time production cost** — paid for units produced at standard hourly rate.
- **Overtime production cost** — typically **1.5× regular rate** (statutory).
- **Undertime / Idle-time cost** — workers paid even when not producing.
- **Subcontracting cost** — paid to external vendors, usually **> regular cost** but **< overtime cost**.

#### 2.1.2 Workforce-Side Costs
- **Hiring cost** — recruitment, training, induction (typically **₹5,000–₹50,000 per worker**).
- **Layoff (firing) cost** — severance, morale loss, legal compliance.
- **Training cost** — upskilling for flexibility.

#### 2.1.3 Inventory / Demand-Side Costs
- **Inventory carrying cost** — warehousing, insurance, capital tied up (typically **15–25% of unit cost/year**).
- **Backorder / Stockout cost** — lost goodwill, penalty, expediting.
- **Lost-sale cost** — permanent customer attrition.

### 2.2 The Four Canonical Aggregate Planning Strategies (KTU Board Favorite)

| # | Strategy | Inventory | Workforce | Production Rate | Backorders |
|---|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|------------|
| 1 | **Chase Strategy** | Zero (or minimal) | Variable (hire/layoff) | Chases demand | None |
| 2 | **Level Strategy** | Allowed to fluctuate | Constant | Constant | None |
| 3 | **Mixed / Hybrid Strategy** | Moderate use | Mild adjustments | Overtime/undertime | Occasional |
| 4 | **Subcontracting Strategy** | None | Constant | Constant | None — outsource gap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Rule (Heuristic)**
> - If **hiring/firing cost > inventory carrying cost** → prefer **Level Strategy** (build inventory).
> - If **inventory carrying cost > hiring/firing cost** → prefer **Chase Strategy** (vary workforce).
> - If demand variability is **moderate** → **Mixed Strategy** dominates both.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention (adopted from KTU board solutions):**
> - $P_t$ = Production in period $t$
> - $D_t$ = Demand in period $t$
> - $I_t$ = Ending inventory in period $t$
> - $W_t$ = Workforce size in period $t$
> - $H_t$ = Workers hired in period $t$
> - $F_t$ = Workers laid off in period $t$
> - $O_t$ = Overtime production in period $t$
> - $S_t$ = Subcontracted units in period $t$
> - $B_t$ = Backorders at end of period $t$

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ | Inventory balance equation | units |
| 2 | $P_t = R_t + O_t + S_t$ | Total production decomposition | units/period |
| 3 | $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t$ | Workforce balance equation | workers |
| 4 | $H_t = W_t - W_{t-1}$ if $W_t > W_{t-1}$ else $0$ | Hiring piecewise | workers |
| 5 | $F_t = W_{t-1} - W_t$ if $W_t < W_{t-1}$ else $0$ | Firing piecewise | workers |
| 6 | $TC = C_P + C_I + C_W + C_S + C_B$ | Total aggregate cost | ₹ |
| 7 | $C_I = h \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{T} I_t$ | Inventory carrying cost ($h$ = holding cost/unit/period) | ₹ |
| 8 | $C_W = c_h \sum H_t + c_f \sum F_t$ | Workforce adjustment cost | ₹ |
| 9 | $C_B = p \cdot \sum B_t$ | Backorder penalty cost ($p$ = penalty/unit/period) | ₹ |
| 10 | $C_S = c_s \sum S_t$ | Subcontracting cost | ₹ |
| 11 | $C_P = c_r \sum R_t + c_o \sum O_t$ | Regular + overtime production cost | ₹ |
| 12 | $Z = \min \sum_{t=1}^{T} \big[ c_r R_t + c_o O_t + c_s S_t + h I_t + p B_t + c_h H_t + c_f F_t \big]$ | **LP objective function** (KTU examination gold standard) | ₹ |

### 2.4 The Three Core Trade-offs Explained (The "Why")

1. **Capacity (Production Rate) vs. Inventory**
   - *Why it matters*: Producing ahead of demand saves on hiring butties-up capital in warehouses.
   - *Engineering analogue*: A dam stores water (inventory) rather than firing up extra turbines (capacity).

2. **Workforce Stability vs. Responsiveness**
   - *Why it matters*: A stable workforce retains institutional knowledge but cannot match demand spikes.
   - *Engineering analogue*: Running a power plant at constant load vs. peak-load gas turbines.

3. **Customer Service Level vs. Cost**
   - *Why it matters*: A 100% fill rate requires enormous safety stock; a 90% fill rate is dramatically cheaper.
   - *Engineering analogue*: Designing a bridge with a 10× safety factor vs. a 3× factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Aggregate Planning Cost Function

The aggregate plan seeks to minimize **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** over the planning horizon $T$. We derive the master objective function by summing all six cost families.

**Step 1: Decompose production into its three sources.**

Every unit shipped to a customer originates from one of three sources: regular-time production, overtime production, or subcontracted production. Hence,

$$
P_t = R_t + O_t + S_t \quad \forall t \in \{1, 2, \ldots, T\}
$$

where $R_t$, $O_t$, and $S_t$ are non-negative.

**Step 2: Express the inventory balance law.**

Inventory carried into period $t$ equals inventory from $t-1$ plus units produced in $t$ minus units demanded in $t$. Mathematically,

$$
I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t + B_{t-1} - B_t
$$

where $B_t$ denotes the backorder backlog. If we disallow backorders ($B_t = 0$ for all $t$), this simplifies to the KTU textbook form:

$$
I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t
$$

**Step 3: Express the workforce evolution law.**

The workforce at the end of period $t$ equals the workforce at the end of $t-1$ plus net hiring:

$$
W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t
$$

where hiring $H_t$ and firing $F_t$ are *non-negative* and **mutually exclusive** (you cannot hire and fire in the same period). This is enforced by the constraints:

$$
H_t \cdot F_t = 0, \quad H_t \geq 0, \quad F_t \geq 0
$$

**Step 4: Aggregate the cost terms.**

Summing all cost components over the planning horizon gives the canonical LP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\min} \;=\; & \sum_{t=1}^{T} \Big[ c_r R_t + c_o O_t + c_s S_t \Big] \\
& + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \Big[ h \cdot I_t^{+} + p \cdot I_t^{-} \Big] \\
& + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \Big[ c_h H_t + c_f F_t \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $I_t^{+}$ = positive inventory (carried), $I_t^{-}$ = negative inventory (backorder), and the coefficients represent the *unit cost* of each lever.

**Step 5: Encode capacity constraints.**

The regular-time production capacity in period $t$ is bounded by the available workforce and the number of working days:

$$
R_t \leq p_{\text{rate}} \cdot W_t \cdot d_t
$$

where $p_{\text{rate}}$ is the per-worker-per-day output and $d_t$ is the number of working days in period $t$.

**Step 6: Encode overtime ceiling.**

Overtime is typically capped (e.g., **20% of regular hours**):

$$
O_t \leq 0.20 \cdot p_{\text{rate}} \cdot W_t \cdot d_t
$$

**Step 7: The complete KTU LP formulation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad & Z = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \big[ c_r R_t + c_o O_t + c_s S_t + h I_t + p B_t + c_h H_t + c_f F_t \big] \\[4pt]
\text{Subject to } \quad & R_t + O_t + S_t + I_{t-1} - I_t + B_{t-1} - B_t = D_t \quad \forall t \\
& W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t \quad \forall t \\
& R_t \leq p_{\text{rate}} \cdot W_t \cdot d_t \quad \forall t \\
& O_t \leq 0.20 \cdot p_{\text{rate}} \cdot W_t \cdot d_t \quad \forall t \\
& R_t, O_t, S_t, I_t, B_t, H_t, F_t, W_t \geq 0 \quad \forall t
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU-style problem)**
> *Given*: A firm faces 4-period demand $\{100, 200, 150, 100\}$ units. Regular cost $c_r = ₹20$/unit, overtime $c_o = ₹30$/unit, subcontract $c_s = ₹35$/unit, inventory $h = ₹5$/unit/period, hiring $c_h = ₹300$/worker, firing $c_f = ₹500$/worker. Each worker produces **10 units/period**. Initial workforce $W_0 = 15$, initial inventory $I_0 = 0$.
>
> *Chase Strategy*: Workforce each period = $D_t / 10$ → $\{10, 20, 15, 10\}$.
> - Hiring: $H_1 = 0$, $F_1 = 5$; $H_2 = 10$, $F_2 = 0$; $H_3 = 0$, $F_3 = 5$; $H_4 = 0$, $F_4 = 5$.
> - Production cost: $20 \times (100+200+150+100) = ₹11{,}000$.
> - Hiring cost: $300 \times 10 = ₹3{,}000$.
> - Firing cost: $500 \times 15 = ₹7{,}500$.
> - **Chase Total Cost = ₹21,500**.
>
> *Level Strategy*: Workforce constant at $15$ → production constant at $150$/period.
> - Cumulative production = 600; cumulative demand = 550 → ending inventory $= 50$.
> - Inventory levels: $I_1 = 50$, $I_2 = 0$, $I_3 = 0$, $I_4 = 50$.
> - Production cost: $20 \times 600 = ₹12{,}000$.
> - Inventory cost: $5 \times (50+0+0+50) = ₹500$.
> - **Level Total Cost = ₹12,500**.
>
> **Conclusion**: The Level strategy saves **₹9,000** because the firm's hiring/firing costs are very high relative to its inventory carrying cost — illustrating the core trade-off. **This is a 14-mark KTU-style computation.**

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose Which Strategy

| Trade-off Decision | Decision Rule | Mathematical Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Chase vs. Level | Compare $\sum(c_h + c_f)$ vs. $h \cdot \sum \vert I_t \vert$ | If $h < (c_h + c_f)/\bar{D}$ → Level |
| Overtime vs. Hire | Compare $c_o$ vs. $c_h / p_{\text{rate}}$ | If $c_o < c_h / p_{\text{rate}}$ → Overtime |
| Subcontract vs. Overtime | Compare $c_s$ vs. $c_o$ | If $c_s < c_o$ → Subcontract |
| Backorder vs. Inventory | Compare $p$ vs. $h$ | If $p > h$ → Carry inventory |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Six-Lever Aggregate Planning Framework (Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    N0[Forecast Demand D_t] --> N1[Aggregate Planning Engine]

    N4 --> N8
    N5 --> N8
    N6 --> N8
    N7 --> N8
    N8 --> N9[Optimal Aggregate Plan]
    N9 --> N10[Master Production Schedule MPS]

```

### 4.2 The Three Core Trade-offs — Visual Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
    N1[INVENTORY BUFFER] --> N2{Holding Cost h}
    N3[WORKFORCE ADJUSTMENT] --> N4{Hiring + Firing Cost}

```
