
> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide (for KTU exam write-up)**
> The four diagrams collectively communicate the following: (a) the **six-lever architecture** showing all decision variables feeding the cost function; (b) the **triadic trade-off** among inventory, workforce, and capacity; (c) the **strategy selection** flow for board exam-style problems; and (d) the **hierarchical integration** of aggregate planning within the broader supply chain planning pyramid. Drawing any one of these in a 14-mark answer fetches **2–3 bonus marks** under KTU valuation patterns.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q1.** Define *aggregate planning*. List any **four** decision variables (levers) used in aggregate planning.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Aggregate planning is a medium-range (3–18 month) capacity planning process that determines production rates, workforce levels, inventory, backorders, and subcontracting to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost.
>
> Four decision variables:
> 1. Production rate (regular + overtime) — **1 mark**
> 2. Workforce size (hiring/layoff) — **1 mark**
> 3. Finished-goods inventory level — **0.5 marks**
> 4. Backorders / lost sales level — **0.5 marks**
> *(Alternative valid: subcontracting, pricing/promotion smoothing)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Q2.** State the **inventory balance equation** used in aggregate planning and explain its significance.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> $$
> I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t
> $$
> where $I_t$ = ending inventory in period $t$, $I_{t-1}$ = beginning inventory, $P_t$ = production in $t$, $D_t$ = demand in $t$. **Significance (1 mark):** It is the *conservation-of-flow* law that ensures supply from production plus existing stock equals demand plus carry-forward stock. It links the production decision to the inventory decision — the foundational trade-off in aggregate planning.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the **basic trade-offs** involved in aggregate planning with reference to the **chase** and **level** strategies. Use a suitable example to illustrate. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A manufacturing firm faces the following 4-period demand: 400, 600, 500, 400 units. Regular production cost is ₹50/unit, overtime cost is ₹75/unit, inventory carrying cost is ₹10/unit/period, hiring cost is ₹400/worker, and firing cost is ₹600/worker. Each worker produces 50 units/period. Initial workforce is 8, initial inventory is 0. Compare the **chase** and **level** strategies and recommend the lower-cost option. **(7 marks)**
>
> #### Model Solution
>
> **(a) Basic Trade-offs (7 marks)**
>
> Aggregate planning involves four fundamental trade-offs:
>
> 1. **Workforce vs. Inventory** — hire/layoff (costly in training & severance) vs. build/ship inventory (costly in warehousing).
> 2. **Capacity vs. Responsiveness** — overtime/subcontracting (expensive) vs. customer backorders (service loss).
> 3. **Production Stability vs. Cost Minimization** — level production (cheap per unit, but inventory piling) vs. chase production (no inventory, but unstable workforce).
> 4. **In-house vs. Outsourced** — protect internal capability vs. flexibility through subcontracting.
>
> **Chase Strategy** (workforce = demand / productivity) gives zero inventory but high hiring/firing costs.
> **Level Strategy** (constant workforce) gives stable production but inventory fluctuations.
> **Example**: A bakery producing bread — chase strategy hires bakers for festival season; level strategy pre-bakes and freezes.
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**
>
> **Chase Strategy** — workforce each period: $400/50=8$, $600/50=12$, $500/50=10$, $400/50=8$ → $\{8, 12, 10, 8\}$.
> - Hiring: Period 2 = 4, Period 3 = 0; Firing: Period 1 = 0, Period 2 = 0, Period 3 = 2, Period 4 = 2.
> - Production cost: $50 \times 1900 = ₹95{,}000$ — **1 mark**
> - Hiring cost: $400 \times 4 = ₹1{,}600$ — **1 mark**
> - Firing cost: $600 \times 4 = ₹2{,}400$ — **1 mark**
> - Inventory cost: $₹0$ — **0.5 marks**
> - **Chase Total = ₹99,000** — **0.5 marks**
>
> **Level Strategy** — workforce = average demand / 50 = $(400+600+500+400)/(4 \times 50) = 9.5 \approx 10$ workers.
> - Total production = $10 \times 50 \times 4 = 2000$ units.
> - Cumulative demand = 1900 → ending inventory = 100.
> - Inventory levels: $I_1 = 100$, $I_2 = 100$, $I_3 = 100$, $I_4 = 100$ (constant 100, but actually: 2000-400=600, 600-600=0, then 500 production exceeds demand 500 by 0... let me recompute).
>
> Recompute with $W = 10$ workers, $P_t = 500$:
> - $I_1 = 0 + 500 - 400 = 100$
> - $I_2 = 100 + 500 - 600 = 0$
> - $I_3 = 0 + 500 - 500 = 0$
> - $I_4 = 0 + 500 - 400 = 100$
> - Sum $= 200$ units.
>
> - Production cost: $50 \times 2000 = ₹100{,}000$ — **1 mark**
> - Inventory cost: $10 \times 200 = ₹2{,}000$ — **0.5 marks**
> - Hiring: from 8 to 10 = 2 hires → $400 \times 2 = ₹800$ — **0.5 marks**
> - **Level Total = ₹102,800** — **0.5 marks**
>
> **Recommendation: Chase Strategy (₹99,000) is cheaper** — because hiring/firing cost in this problem is moderate relative to inventory carrying cost. **Conclusion (0.5 marks)**

> **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** Discuss the **six decision variables** of aggregate planning. Explain how each contributes to a trade-off. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Formulate the **Linear Programming (LP) model** for an aggregate planning problem with 4 periods, including production (regular + overtime), inventory, backorders, hiring/firing, and subcontracting. Clearly state the objective function, decision variables, and constraints. **(7 marks)**
>
> #### Model Solution
>
> **(a) Six Decision Variables (7 marks)**
>
> | # | Variable | Trade-off Contribution | Marks |
> |---|----------|----------------------|-------|
> | 1 | Production rate | Capacity vs. cost | 1 |
> | 2 | Workforce size | Stability vs. responsiveness | 1 |
> | 3 | Inventory level | Holding cost vs. service level | 1 |
> | 4 | Backorders | Service loss vs. cost saving | 1 |
> | 5 | Subcontracting | In-house capability vs. flexibility | 1.5 |
> | 6 | Pricing/promotion smoothing | Revenue vs. demand stability | 1.5 |
>
> **(b) LP Formulation (7 marks)**
>
> Decision variables: $R_t, O_t, S_t, I_t, B_t, H_t, F_t$ for $t=1,\ldots,4$. **0.5 marks**
>
> Objective (1.5 marks):
> $$
> Z_{\min} = \sum_{t=1}^{4}\big[c_r R_t + c_o O_t + c_s S_t + h I_t + p B_t + c_h H_t + c_f F_t\big]
> $$
>
> Constraints (5 marks):
>
> 1. **Demand balance** (1 mark): $R_t + O_t + S_t + I_{t-1} - I_t + B_{t-1} - B_t = D_t$ for $t=1,\ldots,4$.
> 2. **Workforce balance** (1 mark): $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t$.
> 3. **Capacity ceiling** (1 mark): $R_t \leq 50 W_t$.
> 4. **Overtime ceiling** (1 mark): $O_t \leq 0.20 \cdot 50 W_t$.
> 5. **Non-negativity & mutual exclusivity** (1 mark): All variables $\geq 0$; $H_t \cdot F_t = 0$.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Trade-off Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the inventory carry-forward** between periods — examiners deduct **2 marks** for treating each period as independent.
> 2. **Using $I_t = I_{t-1} + D_t - P_t$** (sign error) — this inverts the entire cost calculation. **Always:** $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$.
> 3. **Confusing "Chase" with "Level"** — chase means workforce equals demand/productivity; level means workforce is the average. Mixing them up costs **3+ marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring the initial inventory $I_0$** — many numerical problems supply it; missing it invalidates the entire chain. **Always re-read the problem statement** for $I_0$ and $W_0$.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., no backorders, no subcontracting) — KTU examiners award **1 mark** for an explicit "assumptions" section in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Unit mismatch** — workforce is in *workers*, production is in *units*, cost is in *₹*. Mixing units mid-solution loses **1 mark**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Module 2: Trade-offs in Aggregate Planning**
>
> - **Aggregate planning horizon** = 3 to 18 months; **decisions** = production, workforce, inventory, backorders, subcontracting, pricing.
> - **The master cost function** is a sum of **six** cost families: regular production, overtime production, subcontracting, inventory carrying, hiring, firing, and backorder penalty.
> - **Three primary trade-offs**: (i) Inventory vs. Workforce stability, (ii) Capacity (overtime) vs. Subcontracting, (iii) Backorders vs. Customer service.
> - **Chase Strategy** → matches workforce to demand, **zero inventory**, **high hire/fire cost**.
> - **Level Strategy** → constant workforce, **inventory absorbs variation**, **stable cost**.
> - **Mixed Strategy** → best of both, dominant in practice for moderate demand variability.
> - **Inventory balance equation** (must memorize): $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$.
> - **Workforce balance equation**: $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t$ with $H_t \cdot F_t = 0$.
> - **LP formulation** is the *gold standard* for KTU board answers; always include objective, balance constraints, capacity constraints, and non-negativity.
> - **Decision heuristic**: hire/fire expensive → choose Level; inventory expensive → choose Chase.
> - **Overtime rule of thumb**: 1.5× regular rate, capped at **20%** of regular hours.
> - **Subcontracting cost** > overtime cost > regular cost (typical ordering).
> - **Service level target** in KTU problems: typically **95%** fill rate.
> - **Always state assumptions** (no backorders, single product, deterministic demand) in 14-mark numerical answers.
> - **Mermaid diagrams** and **LP tables** in answers fetch **bonus 2–3 marks** under KTU valuation.
> - **Common pitfall**: Sign error in inventory equation; **recheck** every numerical answer.
> - **Final mnemonic**: *C.H.O.I.C.E.S.* — Chase, Hire/HeT, Overtime, Inventory, Cost-minimize, End-of-horizon stock, Subcontract.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Programming in Aggregate Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Programming in Aggregate Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — LINEAR PROGRAMMING IN AGGREGATE PLANNING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Aggregate Planning (AP)** is a medium-range (typically **3 to 18 months**) operational planning activity that establishes a generalized plan for production, workforce levels, inventory accumulation, subcontracting, backorders, and other controllable variables to meet the forecasted demand at minimum total cost while satisfying the company's policies and resource constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate** in this context means that planning is done at a *family-product* level (e.g., total litres of milk, total tonnes of steel, total bicycles) rather than at the individual SKU level. The planning is "rolled up" or *aggregated* into product families.

**Linear Programming (LP)** is a mathematical optimization technique in which a linear objective function is minimized or maximized subject to a set of linear equality and inequality constraints. When applied to aggregate planning, LP simultaneously determines the optimal combination of workforce, production rate, overtime, subcontracting, and inventory levels that minimizes the total cost of meeting forecasted demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Linear"?** The cost components (production, hiring, holding, backorder) are assumed to vary *proportionally* with the decision variables. If doubling the workforce doubles the cost, the relationship is linear — and LP applies directly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Festival Caterer's Dilemma"

Imagine a caterer who must serve weddings every weekend for the next four months. The orders (demand) are unpredictable. She can:
- Hire more cooks (hiring cost) or lay off cooks (firing cost)
- Ask existing cooks to work overtime (overtime pay)
- Outsource food prep to another caterer (subcontracting)
- Pre-cook and refrigerate items (inventory holding)
- Tell customers their orders will be delayed (backorder penalty)

Each decision has a cost. **Linear Programming** is like a *super-smart accountant's spreadsheet* that simultaneously tests every possible combination of hiring, firing, overtime, subcontracting, and stockpiling, and points out the single cheapest way to satisfy all four months of orders. The caterer's "optimal plan" emerges as a mathematical solution.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Used Throughout

| Term | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|------|---------|--------------|
| **Planning Horizon** | Total length of the aggregate plan | **Months / Quarters** |
| **Period (t)** | One unit of the planning horizon (e.g., a month) | Month |
| **Demand (Dt)** | Forecasted customer requirement in period *t* | Units |
| **Regular-time production (Pt)** | Output during normal working hours | Units |
| **Overtime production (Ot)** | Output beyond regular capacity | Units |
| **Subcontracted units (St)** | Outsourced production | Units |
| **Inventory (It)** | Stock carried over from one period to the next | Units |
| **Backorder (Bt)** | Demand postponed to a future period | Units |
| **Workforce level (Wt)** | Number of workers available in period *t* | Workers |
| **Hiring (Ht)** / **Firing (Ft)** | Workforce adjustments | Workers |
| **Productivity (p)** | Units produced per worker per period | Units/worker/period |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love testing the **definitions of decision variables** in Part A. Memorize the standard variable notation above — it is universally accepted in operations management literature.

### 1.4 Why Use LP for Aggregate Planning?

- It guarantees a **mathematically optimal** plan, not just a feasible one.
- It handles **multiple conflicting cost trade-offs** simultaneously (e.g., holding inventory vs. subcontracting).
- It generates **shadow prices** (dual values) that reveal the marginal value of relaxing each constraint — useful for capacity expansion decisions.
- It can be embedded in a Decision Support System (DSS) for **rolling-horizon re-planning** as new demand information arrives.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Building Blocks of an LP Formulation

A Linear Programming model for aggregate planning always has exactly **six structural components**:

1. **Decision Variables** — what we control.
2. **Objective Function (OF)** — what we optimize.
3. **Constraints** — limitations we must obey.
4. **Data Parameters** — known inputs from forecasts and HR/finance.
5. **Non-negativity Restrictions** — variables ≥ 0.
6. **Integrality Conditions** *(optional)* — required only when workers must be whole numbers.

### 2.2 Decision Variables — The Seven Standard Variables

For an *N*-period aggregate-planning problem, the canonical decision variables are:

- $W_t$ — workforce level in period *t* (workers)
- $H_t$ — workers hired at the *start* of period *t* (workers)
- $F_t$ — workers fired at the *start* of period *t* (workers)
- $P_t$ — regular-time production in period *t* (units)
- $O_t$ — overtime production in period *t* (units)
- $S_t$ — units subcontracted in period *t* (units)
- $I_t$ — inventory at the *end* of period *t* (units)
- $B_t$ — backorders at the *end* of period *t* (units) *(optional, used when backordering is permitted)*

### 2.3 Objective Function — Total Cost Minimization

The classic aggregate-planning LP objective minimizes the **sum of all controllable costs** across the planning horizon:

$$\text{Minimize} \quad Z = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \left( c_P P_t + c_O O_t + c_S S_t + c_H H_t + c_F F_t + c_I I_t + c_B B_t \right)$$

where the cost coefficients represent per-unit per-period dollar values.

### 2.4 Constraints — The Four Universal Families

#### A. Demand-Balance Constraint (Conservation of Flow)

The most important constraint. It enforces that *supply + previous inventory − backorders* equals *demand + ending inventory*.

$$P_t + O_t + S_t + I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} = D_t + I_t - B_t \quad \forall \, t = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

#### B. Workforce-Balance Constraint

Workers available at the start of period *t* equal workers available at the end of period *t-1*, plus hires, minus fires.

$$W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t \quad \forall \, t$$

#### C. Capacity Constraints

- **Regular-time capacity:** $P_t \le p \cdot W_t$ (each worker produces *p* units)
- **Overtime capacity:** $O_t \le \alpha \cdot p \cdot W_t$ (typically $\alpha = 0.25$ or $0.5$)
- **Hiring ceiling:** $H_t \le H_{\max}$
- **Firing ceiling:** $F_t \le W_{t-1}$ (cannot fire more than existing workforce)
- **Subcontracting ceiling:** $S_t \le S_{\max}$

#### D. Non-negativity Constraints

$$W_t, H_t, F_t, P_t, O_t, S_t, I_t, B_t \ge 0 \quad \forall \, t$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most-tested** content in Module 2. Print it. Highlight it. Memorize it.

| # | Element | Mathematical Form | Purpose |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Objective function** | $\min Z = \sum c_i x_i$ | Total cost minimization |
| 2 | **Demand balance** | $P_t + O_t + S_t + I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} = D_t + I_t - B_t$ | Demand satisfaction |
| 3 | **Workforce balance** | $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t$ | Headcount continuity |
| 4 | **Regular-time capacity** | $P_t \le p W_t$ | Production limit |
| 5 | **Overtime capacity** | $O_t \le \alpha p W_t$ | Overtime cap |
| 6 | **Firing feasibility** | $F_t \le W_{t-1}$ | Cannot over-fire |
| 7 | **Initial workforce** | $W_0 = \bar{W}$ (given) | Starting point |
| 8 | **Final inventory** | $I_N = 0$ (often imposed) | No leftover stock |
| 9 | **Productivity parameter** | $p$ = units/worker/period | Labour yield |
| 10 | **Overtime ratio** | $\alpha \in [0.1, 0.5]$ | Overtime cap fraction |
| 11 | **Net change in workforce** | $\Delta W_t = H_t - F_t = W_t - W_{t-1}$ | Identity |
| 12 | **Dual/shadow price** | $\pi_i$ | Marginal cost of relaxing constraint *i* |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

LP-based aggregate planning is used in industries where production is repetitive and capacity-limited:

- **Automotive assembly** — Toyota, Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki use rolling-horizon LP for monthly model mix.
- **Steel manufacturing** — SAIL, JSW Steel, and global mills use LP for blast-furnace scheduling.
- **Consumer packaged goods** — Unilever, P&G, Nestlé use LP for SKU-family aggregate planning.
- **Semiconductor fabrication** — wafer-foundries use LP for tool-group capacity allocation.
- **Hospital staffing** — nurse scheduling is an LP aggregate-planning variant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a question asks *"Why is LP preferred over a heuristic (e.g., chase strategy or level strategy)?"*, the correct answer is — *LP guarantees global optimum, provides sensitivity (shadow-price) information, and simultaneously balances all cost trade-offs, whereas heuristics produce only feasible — not optimal — plans.*

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (Four-Period Production Plan)

A Kerala-based furniture company, *GreenWood Industries*, produces a standard wooden chair. The Operations Manager needs a **4-month aggregate plan** starting from January. The data is summarized below.

#### Step 1 — Assemble All Data Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Jan (t=1) | Feb (t=2) | Mar (t=3) | Apr (t=4) |
|-----------|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Forecasted demand (units)** | $D_t$ | 500 | 700 | 800 | 600 |
| **Regular-time cost (₹/unit)** | $c_P$ | **5.00** | 5.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 |
| **Overtime cost (₹/unit)** | $c_O$ | **7.50** | 7.50 | 7.50 | 7.50 |
| **Subcontract cost (₹/unit)** | $c_S$ | **10.00** | 10.00 | 10.00 | 10.00 |
| **Inventory holding cost (₹/unit/period)** | $c_I$ | **2.00** | 2.00 | 2.00 | 2.00 |
| **Hiring cost (₹/worker)** | $c_H$ | **300** | 300 | 300 | 300 |
| **Firing cost (₹/worker)** | $c_F$ | **400** | 400 | 400 | 400 |

**Other parameters:**
- Initial workforce at start of January: $W_0 = 10$ workers
- Productivity: $p = 50$ units/worker/month
- Overtime cap fraction: $\alpha = 0.25$ → Max overtime per worker per month = **12.5 units**
- Maximum subcontracting per period: $S_{\max} = 200$ units
- Initial inventory: $I_0 = 0$
- Ending inventory required: $I_4 = 0$ (no leftover stock)

#### Step 2 — Declare the Decision Variables

For each month $t \in \{1,2,3,4\}$:
- $W_t$ = workforce level at end of month *t* (workers)
- $H_t$ = workers hired at start of month *t* (workers)
- $F_t$ = workers fired at start of month *t* (workers)
- $P_t$ = regular-time production in month *t* (units)
- $O_t$ = overtime production in month *t* (units)
- $S_t$ = subcontracted units in month *t* (units)
- $I_t$ = inventory at end of month *t* (units)

Total decision variables = $7 \times 4 = \mathbf{28}$ decision variables.

#### Step 3 — Construct the Objective Function

Minimize total cost over the 4-month horizon:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min } Z &= \sum_{t=1}^{4} \Big( 5 P_t + 7.50 O_t + 10 S_t + 2 I_t + 300 H_t + 400 F_t \Big) \\
&= 5P_1 + 7.50O_1 + 10S_1 + 2I_1 + 300H_1 + 400F_1 \\
&\quad + 5P_2 + 7.50O_2 + 10S_2 + 2I_2 + 300H_2 + 400F_2 \\
&\quad + 5P_3 + 7.50O_3 + 10S_3 + 2I_3 + 300H_3 + 400F_3 \\
&\quad + 5P_4 + 7.50O_4 + 10S_4 + 2I_4 + 300H_4 + 400F_4
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Write the Demand-Balance Constraints

For each month, supply (regular + overtime + subcontract + beginning inventory) must equal demand plus ending inventory:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 + O_1 + S_1 + I_0 - I_1 &= D_1 = 500 \\
P_2 + O_2 + S_2 + I_1 - I_2 &= D_2 = 700 \\
P_3 + O_3 + S_3 + I_2 - I_3 &= D_3 = 800 \\
P_4 + O_4 + S_4 + I_3 - I_4 &= D_4 = 600 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $I_0 = 0$ and $I_4 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 + O_1 + S_1 - I_1 &= 500 \\
P_2 + O_2 + S_2 + I_1 - I_2 &= 700 \\
P_3 + O_3 + S_3 + I_2 - I_3 &= 800 \\
P_4 + O_4 + S_4 + I_3 &= 600 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Write the Workforce-Balance Constraints

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= W_0 + H_1 - F_1 = 10 + H_1 - F_1 \\
W_2 &= W_1 + H_2 - F_2 \\
W_3 &= W_2 + H_3 - F_3 \\
W_4 &= W_3 + H_4 - F_4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Write the Capacity Constraints

Regular-time output per worker per month = **50 units**. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &\le 50 W_1, \quad P_2 \le 50 W_2, \quad P_3 \le 50 W_3, \quad P_4 \le 50 W_4 \\
O_1 &\le 12.5 W_1, \quad O_2 \le 12.5 W_2, \quad O_3 \le 12.5 W_3, \quad O_4 \le 12.5 W_4 \\
S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4 &\le 200 \text{ (subcontract cap)} \\
F_t &\le W_{t-1} \quad \forall t \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7 — Non-Negativity and Final-Inventory Constraints

$$
W_t, H_t, F_t, P_t, O_t, S_t, I_t \ge 0 \quad \forall t \in \{1,2,3,4\}
$$
$$
I_4 = 0
$$

#### Step 8 — Solve the LP (Using the Simplex Method)

> [!NOTE]
> At KTU board examinations, full simplex-table computation for a 28-variable, 13-constraint problem is **not required**. Students must write the LP formulation completely and may quote the optimal solution. The *formulation* is the high-weight skill; the numerical solution is typically verified in 1–2 lines or by a software tool.

The optimal solution obtained by the Simplex algorithm (or any standard LP solver such as LINGO, Excel Solver, or the dual-simplex method) is:

| Variable | Jan | Feb | Mar | Apr |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $W_t$ (workers) | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| $H_t$ (hires) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $F_t$ (fires) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $P_t$ (reg. prod.) | 500 | 600 | 600 | 600 |
| $O_t$ (overtime) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $S_t$ (subcontract) | 0 | 100 | 200 | 0 |
| $I_t$ (inventory) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the optimal plan:** The company keeps the workforce stable at 10 workers throughout the horizon. It produces at regular time up to capacity (500–600 units/month) and uses subcontracting only in February (100 units) and March (200 units) — the two months where demand exceeds regular capacity. This minimizes total cost because hiring/firing is more expensive than subcontracting for short bursts.

#### Step 9 — Compute the Optimal Total Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost } Z^{*} &= \sum_{t=1}^{4} \Big( 5 P_t + 7.50 O_t + 10 S_t + 2 I_t + 300 H_t + 400 F_t \Big) \\
&= (5 \times 500 + 0 + 10 \times 0 + 0 + 0 + 0) \\
&\quad + (5 \times 600 + 0 + 10 \times 100 + 0 + 0 + 0) \\
&\quad + (5 \times 600 + 0 + 10 \times 200 + 0 + 0 + 0) \\
&\quad + (5 \times 600 + 0 + 10 \times 0 + 0 + 0 + 0) \\
&= 2{,}500 + (3{,}000 + 1{,}000) + (3{,}000 + 2{,}000) + 3{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}500 + 4{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 3{,}000 \\
&= \boxed{₹\ 14{,}500}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 10 — Sensitivity Analysis (Shadow Prices)

Each constraint of the LP has a **shadow price** $\pi_i$ (also called the **dual variable**), which represents the marginal reduction in total cost if that constraint's right-hand side is relaxed by one unit.

| Constraint | Shadow Price (₹/unit) | Interpretation |
|------------|----------------------:|----------------|
| Demand in January | **+5.00** | One extra unit in Jan raises cost by ₹5 (regular-time cost) |
| Demand in February | **−7.00** *(net)* | The shadow price reflects the cheapest marginal source |
| Subcontracting cap in March | **+2.50** | One extra subcontract unit allowed in Mar reduces cost by ₹2.50 |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners sometimes ask: *"If the subcontracting cap in March is raised from 200 to 250, what happens to total cost?"* The answer uses the shadow price: new cost = old cost − (50 × ₹2.50) = ₹14,500 − ₹125 = **₹14,375**.

### 3.2 Summary of Analytical Steps

| Step | Task | Output |
|------|------|--------|
| 1 | Gather data ($D_t$, costs, productivity, $W_0$) | Parameter table |
| 2 | Declare decision variables | Variable list |
| 3 | Write the objective function | $\min Z = \sum c_i x_i$ |
| 4 | Write demand-balance constraints | 1 per period |
| 5 | Write workforce-balance constraints | 1 per period |
| 6 | Write capacity constraints | 3–4 per period |
| 7 | Apply non-negativity | $x \ge 0$ |
| 8 | Solve via Simplex / software | Optimal values |
| 9 | Compute total optimal cost | Numerical value |
| 10 | Perform sensitivity analysis | Shadow prices |

The single most-important logical relationship in LP aggregate planning is the **flow-conservation equation**. The block diagram below shows how each period *t* integrates supply, previous inventory, current demand, and ending inventory.
