# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define uncertainty in a supply chain. List any four sources of uncertainty.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Uncertainty in a supply chain is the gap between the information needed to plan and the information actually available. It refers to unpredictable variations in demand, supply, lead times, and costs.

**Four Sources (1.5 marks — 0.5 × 3 + 0 bonus):**
1. Forecast error in demand
2. Supplier yield and lead-time variability
3. Internal production breakdowns
4. External macro events (natural disasters, geopolitical risks)

### Q2. What is Risk Pooling? Why does it reduce safety stock?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Risk pooling is the strategy of consolidating inventory from multiple decentralized locations into a single centralized warehouse to reduce variability of aggregated demand.

**Why it reduces safety stock (2 marks):** Because individual demands are negatively correlated or independent, the aggregated standard deviation grows as $\sqrt{n}$, not $n$. Hence pooled safety stock $SS_{pooled} = SS_{separate}/\sqrt{n}$, providing the same service level with substantially less inventory.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **a (7 marks)** and **b (7 marks)**. Internal choice is mandatory within a module.

---

### QUESTION A — [14 Marks]

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** A retailer in Thrissur sells notebooks. Average daily demand is 200 units, $\sigma_D = 30$ units. Average supplier lead time is 4 days, $\sigma_{LT} = 1$ day. Management wants a **97.5% cycle service level**. Calculate:
   (i) Mean demand during lead time
   (ii) Standard deviation during lead time
   (iii) Safety stock
   (iv) Reorder point **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Postponement** in supply chain management. Discuss its three types with examples relevant to Indian industries. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Given:** $\bar{D} = 200, \sigma_D = 30, \bar{L} = 4, \sigma_{LT} = 1, z_{0.975} = 1.96$

**(i) Mean demand during lead time (2 marks):**

$$\mu_{LT} = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} = 200 \times 4 = 800 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Standard deviation during lead time (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{\bar{L}^2 \sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2 \sigma_{LT}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{(4)^2 (30)^2 + (200)^2 (1)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{16 \cdot 900 + 40000}$$

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{14400 + 40000} = \sqrt{54400}$$

$$\sigma_{LT} \approx 233.24 \text{ units}$$

**(iii) Safety stock (2 marks):**

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = 1.96 \times 233.24$$

$$SS \approx 457.15 \approx 458 \text{ units}$$

**(iv) Reorder point (1 mark):**

$$R = \mu_{LT} + SS = 800 + 458 = 1258 \text{ units}$$

**Final Answer:** Reorder Point ≈ **1258 notebooks**.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Postponement (2 marks):**
Postponement is a strategy where a firm delays the differentiation of a product into its finished form until the latest possible point in the supply chain, thereby reducing forecast error and inventory risk.

**Three Types (5 marks):**

1. **Form Postponement (2 marks):** Manufacturing a generic product centrally, then customising it regionally.  
   *Example:* ITC produces generic noodles centrally and applies regional spice-mix variants near demand zones.

2. **Time Postponement (1.5 marks):** Final labelling and packaging are done after order is received.  
   *Example:* Amazon India prints delivery labels at the last-mile sortation centre.

3. **Place Postponement (1.5 marks):** Holding inventory in a centralised warehouse until actual orders are confirmed.  
   *Example:* Reliance Retail's central DC supplying multiple regional store formats.

---

### QUESTION B — [14 Marks]

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A pharmaceutical distributor in Kerala operates 4 regional depots. Each depot has a demand with mean 800 units/day and standard deviation 120 units/day. Calculate the **total safety stock** required for a 95% service level:
   (i) When each depot operates independently.
   (ii) When inventory is consolidated into a single central warehouse.  
   (iii) Comment on the percentage reduction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **three strategic levers** used by supply chain managers to respond to uncertainty. Provide a real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Given:** $n = 4$ depots, $\bar{D} = 800, \sigma = 120$ each, $z_{0.95} = 1.65$

**(i) Independent decentralized safety stock (3 marks):**

$$SS_{each} = z \cdot \sigma = 1.65 \times 120 = 198 \text{ units}$$

$$SS_{total, dec} = 4 \times 198 = 792 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Pooled centralized safety stock (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_{pooled} = 120 \sqrt{4} = 240 \text{ units}$$

$$SS_{pooled} = 1.65 \times 240 = 396 \text{ units}$$

**(iii) Percentage reduction (1 mark):**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{792 - 396}{792} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Comment:** By consolidating into a central warehouse, the distributor achieves the same 95% service level with **50% less safety stock**, freeing up ₹X crore in working capital.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Three Strategic Levers (7 marks — 2 marks theory + 5 marks examples):**

**1. Inventory Lever (2 marks):**
Maintaining safety stock absorbs demand-supply mismatch.  
*Example:* Hindustan Unilever keeps finished-goods buffer stock in 40+ Indian warehouses to handle monsoon-driven demand spikes.

**2. Capacity Lever (2.5 marks):**
Keeping idle capacity in machines, labour, and warehouses allows surge response.  
*Example:* Maruti Suzuki maintains a flexible shift system at Gurgaon and Manesar plants to absorb festive-season demand surges.

**3. Lead-Time Lever (2.5 marks):**
Reducing lead time via process improvement, local sourcing, or expedited transport shrinks the planning window, hence less safety stock is needed.  
*Example:* Toyota Kirloskar's local vendor park near Bidadi plant reduces component lead time from weeks to hours, enabling JIT.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Missing Z-value reference:** Always mention the Z-score value (e.g., 1.65 for 95%) explicitly. Marks deducted if left blank.
> 2. **Wrong formula variant:** Students frequently confuse $SS = z \sigma_D \sqrt{L}$ (demand-only uncertainty) with the combined formula. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Unit omission:** Always write "units" or "bags" after numerical answers.
> 4. **Risk pooling math:** Writing $\sigma_{pooled} = \sigma \times n$ (linear instead of $\sqrt{n}$) is a **common 2-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Postponement examples:** Using textbook-only examples (HP printers, Benetton sweaters) is acceptable, but **KTU rewards India-specific examples** with extra marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Revision — Memorise These 12 Points:**

- [ ] Uncertainty = **gap between required and available information** (Chopra & Meindl definition).
- [ ] Two main categories: **Demand-side uncertainty** and **Supply-side uncertainty**.
- [ ] Three mitigation levers: **Inventory, Capacity, Lead Time** (Uncertainty Mitigation Triangle).
- [ ] Reorder Point formula: $R = \bar{D}\bar{L} + SS$.
- [ ] Combined uncertainty safety stock: $SS = z\sqrt{\bar{L}^2\sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2\sigma_{LT}^2}$.
- [ ] Z-scores to memorise: 90%→1.28, 95%→1.65, 97.5%→1.96, 99%→2.33.
- [ ] **Risk pooling reduces safety stock by factor $1/\sqrt{n}$**, NOT $1/n$.
- [ ] Postponement has **3 types**: Form, Time, Place.
- [ ] Bullwhip Effect: demand variability **amplifies** upstream — a process-level uncertainty amplifier.
- [ ] Cycle Service Level (Type 1) vs. Fill Rate (Type 2) — distinguish in exam.
- [ ] EOQ assumes **no uncertainty**; hence cannot be used when uncertainty is significant.
- [ ] Make-to-Stock (MTS) suits low-uncertainty, low-variety; Make-to-Order (MTO) suits high-uncertainty, high-variety products.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Inventory: Factors Affecting the Optimal Level of Product Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Inventory: Factors Affecting the Optimal Level of Product Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Inventory (Safety Stock / Buffer Stock):** *Safety inventory is the quantity of stock held in excess of expected demand during the lead time, specifically maintained to provide a buffer against demand and supply variability and to achieve a target level of product availability.*

Mathematically, it is the term added to the expected lead-time demand in the **Reorder Point (ROP)** formulation:

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + \text{Safety Stock (SS)}$$

where $\bar{d}$ is the **average daily demand** and $\bar{L}$ is the **average lead time** in days.

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Availability** is defined as the *fraction (or probability) of demand that is immediately satisfied from on-hand inventory*. The **optimal level of product availability** is the service level at which the marginal cost of carrying one additional unit of safety stock is exactly equal to the marginal benefit (reduction in expected shortage cost).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Water Tank" Model

Imagine a household overhead water tank feeding your taps:

- **Expected demand** = your daily water usage.
- **Lead time** = the time taken by the municipal tanker to refill the tank after you call.
- **Safety stock** = the extra water you keep *above* your expected daily consumption, so that even if usage suddenly spikes (guests arrive) or the tanker arrives late, you don't face a dry tap.

If you keep the tank absolutely full (100 % availability), your carrying cost (water wasted, tank corrosion, energy to pump) goes up. If you keep it nearly empty, you face dry-tap outages. **Optimal availability** is the "sweet-spot level" that balances *shortage risk* against *carrying cost* — exactly what safety stock optimisation does in supply chains.

---

### 1.3 Product Availability — Two Service-Level Paradigms

| Service Metric | Symbol | Definition | KTU Notation |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Cycle Service Level** (Type I) | $\alpha$ | Probability that demand during lead time does **not** exceed ROP (i.e., no stockout per replenishment cycle) | "fraction of cycles without stockout" |
| **Fill Rate** (Type II) | $\beta$ | Fraction of total demand that is satisfied **immediately** from stock on hand | "fraction of units filled" |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a numerical problem gives a service level of **95 %**, ask *“Type I or Type II?”* If Type I, use the **z-table** to get $z_{0.95} = 1.645$. If Type II, you must use the **fill-rate integral** — the z-value will be different and generally lower.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Pillars — Factors Affecting Optimal Level of Product Availability

The optimal level of product availability is *not* a fixed corporate policy; it is the output of a multi-factor optimisation. The seven key factors prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:

**1. Demand Variability (Forecast Error)**
- Quantified by the standard deviation of daily demand, $\sigma_d$.
- Higher $\sigma_d$ → higher safety stock for the same service level.
- Reflects both **inherent demand variability** and **forecast inaccuracy**.

**2. Lead Time Variability**
- Quantified by the standard deviation of replenishment lead time, $\sigma_L$.
- Caused by supplier unreliability, transportation delays, port congestion, customs variability.
- A firm with $\sigma_L > 0$ carries higher SS than one with deterministic lead times.

**3. Desired Service Level**
- The most critical **managerial lever** — driven by competitive positioning, customer expectations, contractual SLAs.
- Higher service level → higher $z$ → exponentially higher safety stock.

**4. Order Quantity (Q) and Lot-Sizing Policy**
- Affects only **Fill Rate (Type II)**; Cycle Service Level is independent of Q.
- Larger Q → more buffer-time → higher fill rate.

**5. Item Characteristics**
- **Item cost**: Low-value "C-items" get lower availability (e.g., 90 %); high-value "A-items" get higher (e.g., 99.9 %).
- **Criticality**: Items causing line stoppages → very high availability.
- **Perishability / Obsolescence**: Fresh produce/seasonal SKUs → low safety stock.

**6. Implied / Inherent Supply Uncertainty**
- Captured jointly via $\sigma_L$ and supplier reliability metrics.
- Often quantified as the **Probability of Supplier On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)**.

**7. Carrying Cost vs Stockout Cost Trade-off**
- Carrying cost $h$ (typically 15–30 % of unit cost per year).
- Stockout cost includes lost margin, lost goodwill, line-stoppage penalty.
- **Optimal SS** = SS at which $d(\text{Carrying Cost})/d(\text{SS}) = d(\text{Stockout Cost})/d(\text{SS})$.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master every row below — problems in KTU ESE and University Examinations are direct applications of these equations.

| # | Scenario | Safety Stock Formula | Key Assumption |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Variable demand, **constant** lead time | $\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$ | Daily demands are independent, $\bar{L}$ fixed |
| 2 | Constant demand, **variable** lead time | $\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_L \cdot \bar{d}$ | Demand deterministic, $\sigma_L$ known |
| 3 | **Both** demand and lead time variable | $\text{SS} = z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}\cdot\sigma_d^{2} \;+\; \bar{d}^{2}\cdot\sigma_{L}^{2}}$ | Independent random variables |
| 4 | Reorder Point (general) | $\text{ROP} = \bar{d}\cdot\bar{L} + \text{SS}$ | Order triggered when inventory drops to ROP |
| 5 | Cycle Service Level (Type I) | $\alpha = P(D_{LT} \le \text{ROP})$ | Convert to z via standard normal CDF |
| 6 | Fill Rate (Type II) | $\beta = 1 - \dfrac{E[\text{shortage per cycle}]}{Q}$ | Requires loss-function table |
| 7 | Expected shortage per cycle | $E[S] = \sigma_{LT}\cdot L(z)$ | $L(z)$ = standard normal loss function |

### 2.3 Variable Definitions & Units

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\bar{d}$ | Average daily demand | units / day |
| $\bar{L}$ | Average lead time | days |
| $\sigma_d$ | Std. dev. of daily demand | units / day |
| $\sigma_L$ | Std. dev. of lead time | days |
| $z$ | Standard normal deviate for service level $\alpha$ | dimensionless |
| $\sigma_{LT}$ | Std. dev. of demand over lead time | units |
| $Q$ | Order quantity | units |
| $h$ | Holding cost per unit per year | ₹ / unit-year |
| $\pi$ | Penalty / shortage cost per unit | ₹ / unit |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

- **Retail (FMCG, BigBasket, Amazon):** SS prevents "out-of-stock" lost sales — measured via **OTIF** and **Lost Sales Rate**.
- **Automotive OEMs:** Even 0.1 % stockout of a critical bolt can halt an assembly line costing ₹1 lakh/minute → very high safety stock on "A-items".
- **Pharma cold-chain:** Combines safety stock with **redundancy** (multiple suppliers) and **dual-sourcing**.
- **E-commerce last-mile:** Uses **dynamic SS** based on real-time demand sensing (e.g., during Big Billion Days).
- **Manufacturing cells:** JIT systems use *minimal* SS, compensating with **supplier reliability engineering**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Safety Stock with Variable Demand, Constant Lead Time

**Setup:**
- Daily demand $D_i \sim (\bar{d},\; \sigma_d)$, i.i.d.
- Lead time $L = \bar{L}$ (constant).
- Demand over lead time: $D_{LT} = \sum_{i=1}^{\bar{L}} D_i$.

Because daily demands are independent:

$$\sigma_{LT}^{2} = \text{Var}(D_{LT}) = \sum_{i=1}^{\bar{L}} \text{Var}(D_i) = \bar{L}\cdot\sigma_d^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$$

We need the ROP such that the probability of stockout equals $(1-\alpha)$:

$$\alpha = P(D_{LT} \le \text{ROP}) = P\!\left(\frac{D_{LT}-\bar{d}\cdot\bar{L}}{\sigma_d\sqrt{\bar{L}}} \le z\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{ROP} = \bar{d}\cdot\bar{L} + z\cdot\sigma_d\sqrt{\bar{L}}$$

Comparing with $\text{ROP} = \bar{d}\cdot\bar{L} + \text{SS}$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Safety Stock with Variable Lead Time, Constant Demand

**Setup:**
- Daily demand $d = \bar{d}$ (deterministic).
- Lead time $L \sim (\bar{L},\;\sigma_L)$.

Demand during lead time $D_{LT} = \bar{d}\cdot L$ :

$$\sigma_{LT} = \bar{d}\cdot\sigma_L$$

Setting ROP such that $P(D_{LT} \le \text{ROP}) = \alpha$ :

$$\boxed{\;\text{SS} = z \cdot \bar{d} \cdot \sigma_L\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Joint Variability (Combined Case)

With both random demand and random lead time, assuming **independence**:

$$D_{LT} = \sum_{i=1}^{L} D_i \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sigma_{LT}^{2} = \bar{L}\cdot\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\cdot\sigma_L^{2}$$

The first term captures demand variability propagated over average lead time; the second term captures lead-time variability scaled by average demand. Result:

$$\boxed{\;\text{SS} = z\cdot\sqrt{\bar{L}\cdot\sigma_d^{2} \;+\; \bar{d}^{2}\cdot\sigma_{L}^{2}}\;}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (14-Mark Style)

> **[Problem Statement — KTU ESE Style]**
>
> A distribution centre has the following demand and supply profile for an **A-class SKU**:
>
> - Average daily demand: $\bar{d} = 100$ units/day
> - Standard deviation of daily demand: $\sigma_d = 25$ units/day
> - Average lead time from supplier: $\bar{L} = 6$ days
> - Standard deviation of lead time: $\sigma_L = 1.5$ days
> - Target **Cycle Service Level** $\alpha = 95\,\%$
> - Order quantity $Q = 500$ units
>
> **Required:** (a) Calculate safety stock and reorder point. (b) If management targets a **Fill Rate** of $99\,\%$, recompute the safety stock and state which service metric gives the larger buffer.

**Step 1 — Look up the z-value for 95 % service level.**

$$z_{0.95} = 1.645$$

**Step 2 — Apply combined-variability safety stock formula.**

$$\text{SS} = z\cdot\sqrt{\bar{L}\cdot\sigma_d^{2} \;+\; \bar{d}^{2}\cdot\sigma_{L}^{2}}$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$\text{SS} = 1.645 \cdot \sqrt{(6)(25)^{2} + (100)^{2}(1.5)^{2}}$$

$$\text{SS} = 1.645 \cdot \sqrt{(6)(625) + (10000)(2.25)}$$

$$\text{SS} = 1.645 \cdot \sqrt{3750 + 22500}$$

$$\text{SS} = 1.645 \cdot \sqrt{26250}$$

$$\text{SS} = 1.645 \cdot 162.02$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{SS} \approx 266.5 \text{ units} \;\approx\; 267 \text{ units}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Reorder Point (ROP).**

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{d}\cdot\bar{L} + \text{SS} = (100)(6) + 267 = 600 + 267$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{ROP} = 867 \text{ units}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Type II (Fill Rate) requirement.**

For Fill Rate $\beta = 99\,\%$, the corresponding $z$ value is found using the standard normal loss function:

$$\beta = 1 - \frac{\sigma_{LT}\cdot L(z)}{Q} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{\sigma_{LT}\cdot L(z)}{Q} = 1-\beta = 0.01$$

$$\frac{162.02 \cdot L(z)}{500} = 0.01 \;\Rightarrow\; L(z) = \frac{0.01 \cdot 500}{162.02} = 0.0309$$

From standard loss function tables, $L(z) = 0.0309 \Rightarrow z \approx 1.75$.

$$\text{SS}_{\beta=99\%} = 1.75 \cdot 162.02 \approx 283.5 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{ROP}_{\beta=99\%} = 600 + 284 = 884 \text{ units}$$

**Interpretation:**

$$\Delta\text{SS} = 284 - 267 = 17 \text{ units} \;\Rightarrow\; +6.4\,\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The **Fill Rate** target (Type II) demands a *slightly larger* safety stock than the **Cycle Service Level** target (Type I) for the same nominal "95 % vs 99 %" labels. **Part (a) — Cycle Service Level** gives SS ≈ 267 units and ROP ≈ 867 units. **Part (b) — Fill Rate** gives SS ≈ 284 units and ROP ≈ 884 units.

---

### 3.5 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Service Level Metric

> [!TIP]
> Use this matrix in the exam to justify which service metric your chosen answer is using.

| Business Context | Recommended Metric | Why |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Assembly line B-O-M components | Cycle Service Level (Type I) | Stockout = line stop; "no stockout per cycle" is the right risk measure |
| Retail consumer demand | Fill Rate (Type II) | Customers tolerate occasional gaps; but want most demand met |
| Spare parts / MRO | Fill Rate (Type II) | Equipment downtime cost is proportional to *unmet* demand volume |
| Service parts (warranty) | Cycle Service Level (Type I) | Any single stockout = SLA breach |
| Perishable / short-life items | Fill Rate (Type II) + low target | Holding cost dominates |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Safety Stock Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Define SKU and Context]) --> node1[Collect Demand Data]
    node1 --> node2[Collect Lead Time Data]
    node2 --> node3[Compute d-bar and sigma-d]
    node3 --> node4[Compute L-bar and sigma-L]
    node4 --> node5{Service Level Type?}
    node5 -->|Type I Cycle| node6[Look up z from alpha via Z-table]
    node5 -->|Type II Fill Rate| node7[Look up z from beta via Loss Function L-z]
    node6 --> node8[Compute SS = z times sqrt of L-bar sigma-d squared plus d-bar squared sigma-L squared]
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9[Compute ROP = d-bar times L-bar plus SS]
    node9 --> node10{Annual Carrying Cost versus Stockout Cost Balanced?}
    node10 -->|No| node11[Adjust target service level and repeat]
    node10 -->|Yes| node12[Finalize SS and ROP for SKU]
    node11 --> node5
    node12 --> endA([End: Publish Inventory Policy])
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Seven Pillars Affecting Product Availability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_FACTORS[INPUT FACTORS]
        f1[Demand Variability sigma-d]
        f2[Lead Time Variability sigma-L]
        f3[Service Level Target alpha or beta]
        f4[Order Quantity Q]
        f5[Item Cost and Criticality]
        f6[Supply Chain Uncertainty OTIF]
        f7[Carrying Cost versus Stockout Cost]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[SAFETY STOCK ENGINE]
        proc1[Combine Variances]
        proc2[Apply Z Factor]
        proc3[Compute ROP]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OPTIMAL AVAILABILITY]
        out1[Safety Stock Level]
        out2[Reorder Point]
        out3[Service Level Achieved]
    end

    f1 --> proc1
    f2 --> proc1
    f3 --> proc2
    f4 --> proc2
    f5 --> proc2
    f6 --> proc1
    f7 --> proc2
    proc1 --> proc2
    proc2 --> proc3
    proc3 --> out1
    proc3 --> out2
    proc3 --> out3
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Type I vs Type II Service Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q{What is being measured?}
    q -->|Probability of NO stockout per cycle| t1[Type I - Cycle Service Level - alpha]
    q -->|Fraction of demand met from stock| t2[Type II - Fill Rate - beta]

    t1 --> c1[Uses Z-table directly]
    t2 --> c2[Uses Normal Loss Function L-z]

    c1 --> r1[SS depends on sigma-LT only]
    c2 --> r2[SS depends on sigma-LT and Q]

    r1 --> out1[Lower SS for same numeric value]
    r2 --> out2[Higher SS for same numeric value]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural interpretation:** The two block diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of a real-world **Inventory Optimisation Engine** used in SAP IBP, Oracle SCM, and Kinaxis Maestro. The inputs (demand/supply distributions, target service level) feed into a variance-combiner → z-lookup → ROP calculator, which outputs the recommended safety stock and reorder policy per SKU.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Distinguish clearly between **Cycle Service Level (Type I)** and **Fill Rate (Type II)**. Which of the two is more appropriate for measuring availability of an item used as a component in a car-assembly line, and why?

**Model Answer:**

- **Cycle Service Level (Type I, $\alpha$):** the probability that demand during lead time will *not* exceed inventory on hand — i.e., the fraction of replenishment cycles **without a stockout**.
- **Fill Rate (Type II, $\beta$):** the fraction of total demand that is met immediately from stock — i.e., the fraction of **units** (not cycles) satisfied.
- For an assembly-line component, **Cycle Service Level (Type I)** is preferred because **any single stockout halts the line** — the risk metric is per-cycle, not per-unit. Hence the relevant guarantee is "no stockout per cycle," which is exactly what Type I measures.

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** List any **six factors** that affect the optimal level of product availability in a supply chain.

**Model Answer:**
1. Demand variability (forecast error / $\sigma_d$)
2. Lead-time variability ($\sigma_L$)
3. Target service level (Type I or Type II)
4. Order quantity and lot-sizing policy ($Q$)
5. Item characteristics — cost, criticality, perishability
6. Implied supply uncertainty (supplier OTIF)
7. Carrying cost vs stockout cost trade-off

(Any six of the above earn full 3 marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Mis-identifying service level type** — many students default to $z = 1.645$ even when the problem states Fill Rate. Always re-read the question.
> 2. **Forgetting the √L factor** — for constant lead time, SS scales with $\sqrt{L}$, *not* with $L$. A common error is writing $\text{SS} = z\cdot\sigma_d\cdot L$.
> 3. **Mixing $\sigma_d$ with $\sigma_{LT}$** — daily demand std. dev. must be scaled by $\sqrt{L}$ to get lead-time std. dev.
> 4. **Skipping units** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unmarked final answers.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — independence of daily demands, normality assumption must be mentioned.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A retailer sells a fast-moving SKU with the following data:
- Average daily demand $\bar{d} = 200$ units/day, with $\sigma_d = 40$ units/day
- Lead time from supplier is **constant** at $\bar{L} = 8$ days
- The retailer wants a **Cycle Service Level** of $97.5\,\%$
- Order quantity $Q = 1500$ units
- Carrying cost $h = ₹30$ per unit per year; stockout cost $\pi = ₹250$ per unit

Calculate the **safety stock**, **reorder point**, and the **annual carrying cost of safety stock**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Suppose the lead time is no longer constant, but has $\bar{L} = 8$ days and $\sigma_L = 2$ days. Recalculate the safety stock and ROP. State with justification whether the inventory policy must be revised.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1.** For $97.5\%$ service level, from the standard normal table:

$$z_{0.975} = 1.96$$

**Step 2.** Apply the constant lead-time SS formula:

$$\text{SS} = z\cdot\sigma_d\cdot\sqrt{\bar{L}} = 1.96 \cdot 40 \cdot \sqrt{8}$$

$$\sqrt{8} = 2.828 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SS} = 1.96 \cdot 40 \cdot 2.828$$

$$\text{SS} = 1.96 \cdot 113.14 = 221.7 \approx 222 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3.** Compute ROP:

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{d}\cdot\bar{L} + \text{SS} = 200\cdot 8 + 222 = 1600 + 222 = 1822 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4.** Annual carrying cost of safety stock:

$$\text{Annual Carry Cost} = h \cdot \text{SS} = ₹30 \cdot 222 = ₹6{,}660 \text{ per year}$$

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1.** Apply combined-variability formula:

$$\text{SS}_{\text{new}} = z\cdot\sqrt{\bar{L}\cdot\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\cdot\sigma_L^{2}}$$

$$= 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{(8)(40)^{2} + (200)^{2}(2)^{2}}$$

$$= 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{(8)(1600) + (40000)(4)}$$

$$= 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{12800 + 160000}$$

$$= 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{172800}$$

$$= 1.96 \cdot 415.69 = 814.7 \approx 815 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2.** New ROP:

$$\text{ROP}_{\text{new}} = (200)(8) + 815 = 1600 + 815 = 2415 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3.** Justification — Policy revision:

$$\Delta\text{SS} = 815 - 222 = 593 \text{ units} \;(+267\,\% \text{ increase})$$

Yes, the inventory policy **must be revised**. The lead-time variability alone has *tripled* the required safety stock because suppliers are now unreliable. Management has two options: (i) increase SS to 815 units, accepting higher carrying cost (≈ ₹24,450/year), or (ii) **reduce $\sigma_L$** by qualifying a more reliable supplier (single-sourcing or contractual SLA penalties) — the second option is preferred if carrying cost is dominant.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **seven factors** that determine the optimal level of product availability in a supply chain. Illustrate your answer with two industry examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital pharmacy stocks an essential drug with:
- $\bar{d} = 50$ vials/day, $\sigma_d = 12$ vials/day
- $\bar{L} = 4$ days, $\sigma_L = 0.5$ day
- Order quantity $Q = 600$ vials
- Required Fill Rate $\beta = 99\,\%$

Determine the safety stock and reorder point. What would happen to the policy if the hospital changed to a Cycle Service Level of $99\,\%$ instead?

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Solution Outline (7 marks):**

The seven factors are:
1. **Demand variability** — captured by $\sigma_d$; increases SS linearly after z-multiplication. *Example:* Diwali demand for sweets at a sweet-shop chain.
2. **Lead-time variability** — captured by $\sigma_L$; affects SS quadratically through the term $\bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}$. *Example:* Imported electronic components delayed at customs.
3. **Target service level** — managerial lever; higher $\alpha/\beta$ → exponentially higher SS. *Example:* Amazon Prime's 99 % delivery promise.
4. **Order quantity Q** — affects only Type II; larger Q → lower SS for the same fill rate.
5. **Item characteristics** — ABC classification; A-items get 99.9 % service, C-items get 90 %. *Example:* Spare parts of aircraft engines vs stationery.
6. **Supply chain uncertainty** — supplier OTIF, geopolitical risk. *Example:* Semiconductor shortage 2020–2023.
7. **Carrying cost vs stockout cost** — optimisation lever. *Example:* Cold-chain pharma vs fast fashion apparel.

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1.** Combined lead-time standard deviation:

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{\bar{L}\cdot\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\cdot\sigma_L^{2}} = \sqrt{(4)(144) + (2500)(0.25)}$$

$$= \sqrt{576 + 625} = \sqrt{1201} = 34.66 \text{ vials}$$

**Step 2.** For $\beta = 99\,\%$ fill rate, expected shortage:

$$\frac{\sigma_{LT}\cdot L(z)}{Q} = 1 - \beta = 0.01 \;\Rightarrow\; L(z) = \frac{0.01\cdot 600}{34.66} = 0.1732$$

From standard loss function tables, $L(z) = 0.1732 \Rightarrow z \approx 1.10$.

$$\text{SS}_{\beta=99\%} = 1.10 \cdot 34.66 \approx 38.1 \approx 39 \text{ vials}$$

$$\text{ROP}_{\beta=99\%} = (50)(4) + 39 = 200 + 39 = 239 \text{ vials}$$

**Step 3.** Cycle Service Level $\alpha = 99\,\%$ requires $z = 2.33$:

$$\text{SS}_{\alpha=99\%} = 2.33 \cdot 34.66 \approx 80.8 \approx 81 \text{ vials}$$

$$\text{ROP}_{\alpha=99\%} = 200 + 81 = 281 \text{ vials}$$

**Step 4.** Comparative insight:

$$\Delta\text{SS} = 81 - 39 = 42 \text{ vials} \;(+108\,\%)$$

For a hospital pharmacy where a stockout may cost a life, the **Cycle Service Level of $99\,\%$** is preferable — even though it more than doubles the safety stock, it guarantees *no stockout in any replenishment cycle*, which is the clinically appropriate risk metric.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Safety Inventory**

- [x] **Safety stock = the buffer above expected lead-time demand** to absorb demand and supply variability.
- [x] **Three master formulas** to memorise:
  1. $\text{SS} = z\cdot\sigma_d\sqrt{\bar{L}}$ (variable demand, constant LT)
  2. $\text{SS} = z\cdot\bar{d}\sigma_L$ (constant demand, variable LT)
  3. $\text{SS} = z\cdot\sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}}$ (combined)
- [x] **ROP** always equals $\bar{d}\cdot\bar{L} + \text{SS}$ — never skip the lead-time demand term.
- [x] **Type I (Cycle SL)** uses the **z-table directly**; **Type II (Fill Rate)** uses the **standard normal loss function $L(z)$**.
- [x] **Fill Rate of 99 %** needs *less* safety stock than **Cycle SL of 99 %** for the same nominal value.
- [x] **Seven factors**: demand variability, lead-time variability, target SL, order quantity, item characteristics, supply uncertainty, carrying-vs-stockout cost.
- [x] **ABC policy**: A-items ≥ 99 %; B-items 95–97 %; C-items 90 %.
- [x] **Always state assumptions**: normality, independence, unit consistency.
- [x] **Units in final answer** — KTU examiners deduct for missing units.
- [x] **Real-world extensions**: dual sourcing, VMI, safety stock *positioning* in multi-echelon networks (e.g., base-surge model).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Inventory
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Inventory

# Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Inventory

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Safety Inventory (Safety Stock / Buffer Stock):** In the context of the KTU 2024 SCM framework, safety inventory is defined as the *extra inventory carried over and above the cycle stock to protect against the uncertainties of both demand and supply* during the lead-time replenishment cycle. It is the deliberate "cushion" a firm maintains to insulate its operations from stochastic fluctuations.

**Supply Uncertainty:** The unpredictability associated with the *inflow* of materials — manifesting as variability in **lead time**, **yield (quantity received)**, **quality**, or as discrete **disruptions** (e.g., strikes, transportation breakdowns, geopolitical events, supplier bankruptcy). Unlike demand uncertainty (an *outflow* phenomenon), supply uncertainty is an *inflow* phenomenon that directly threatens the firm's ability to meet service-level targets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Supply uncertainty, when compounded with demand variability, *significantly inflates* the safety inventory requirement. A 10% increase in supply lead-time variability may require a far greater proportional rise in safety stock than a 10% increase in demand variability — because supply shocks can simultaneously wipe out a full replenishment cycle.

### Two Distinct Faces of Supply Uncertainty

1. **Supply Variability (Continuous Uncertainty):** Random fluctuations in the *quantity received* or the *lead time* of each replenishment order. Statistical in nature (analogous to demand variability).
2. **Supply Disruption (Discrete Uncertainty):** A sudden, often total, halt in supply for a finite duration (e.g., a port strike lasting 10 days). This is a *catastrophic* event requiring a different inventory response.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Buckets" View

Imagine you are a **kirana shop owner** in Kerala stocking rice bags. You know your customers (demand) — they buy roughly 5–7 bags/day (with some randomness). That alone lets you set a basic safety stock. But your **wholesaler** (supplier) is *also* unpredictable:
- Sometimes the truck arrives in 1 day, sometimes in 3.
- Sometimes only 8 out of every 10 ordered bags actually reach you (yield loss).
- Once last year, the whole supplier's warehouse was flooded — no rice for 2 weeks.

This is **supply uncertainty**. The first two cases raise your safety stock continuously (you keep a few *extra* bags). The third case forces a *discontinuous jump* — you must stock enough to survive 2 weeks *with no inflow at all*. The **Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Inventory** is therefore the systematic quantification of these two effects and the trade-offs in responding to them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric (KPM):** The standard KTU 2024 measure for inventory cushioning is the **fill rate / in-stock probability** (Type-1 service level) or the **stockout probability per replenishment cycle** (Type-2 service level), normally expressed as **97%, 99%, or 99.5%** for high-service supply chains.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Supply Uncertainty Is Fundamentally Different from Demand Uncertainty

- **Demand variability** is an *outflow* variable — it can be smoothed somewhat by aggregating orders, postponement, and forecasting.
- **Supply variability** is an *inflow* variable — the firm has *zero direct control* over it. Mitigation requires either inventory, redundant suppliers, or contractual buffers.
- The **cycle stock** is sized using the *average* demand and *average* lead time. Safety stock must cover the *deviations* around these means.

## 2.2 Decomposing the Impact

The impact of supply uncertainty on safety inventory can be decomposed into **three operational channels**:

1. **Channel A — Lead-Time Variability (LT variance):** The replenishment order may arrive early or late. A longer-than-expected lead time depletes cycle stock without replenishment, forcing the firm to dip into safety stock.
2. **Channel B — Yield / Quantity Variability:** The supplier delivers fewer usable units than ordered. The shortfall must be absorbed by safety stock.
3. **Channel C — Disruption Risk (LS risk):** Complete cessation of supply for a discrete duration $R$. The safety stock must cover *all demand* during $R$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables / Units** | **KTU Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard normal safety factor | $z$ | dimensionless, from standard normal table | corresponds to desired service level $SL$ |
| Safety Stock (Demand Uncertainty only) | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{D} \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$ | $\sigma_D$ = std. dev. of daily demand (units/day); $\bar{L}$ = avg. lead time (days) | baseline safety stock |
| Effective Demand Std. Dev. (Combined DT & LT uncertainty) | $\sigma_{d} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_{D}^{2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}}$ | $\bar{d}$ = avg. daily demand; $\sigma_L$ = std. dev. of lead time (days) | demand+LT variability case |
| Safety Stock (Combined DT & LT Uncertainty) | $SS = z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_{D}^{2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}}$ | units | **most common KTU problem** |
| Impact of Supply Variability on SS | $\Delta SS = z \cdot \bar{d} \cdot \sigma_{L}$ (incremental term) | units | isolating supply effect |
| Safety Stock for Supply Disruption | $SS_{disrupt} = \bar{d} \cdot (L_{max} - \bar{L})$ | units | catastrophic loss case |
| Safety Stock for Yield Loss | $SS_{yield} = \dfrac{\bar{d} \cdot \bar{L}}{\bar{y}} - \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L}$ | $\bar{y}$ = average yield fraction (0–1) | supplier yield issues |
| Reorder Point with Uncertainty | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS$ | units | placement of replenishment order |
| Total Annual Holding Cost of SS | $H_{SS} = SS \cdot h$ | $h$ = holding cost (Rs/unit/yr) | economic trade-off |
| Type-1 Service Level Constraint | $P(\text{stockout in cycle}) = 1 - SL$ | probability | fill-rate / cycle-service level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The supply-uncertainty term contributes $\bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}$ to the variance *inside the square root*, which is **multiplied by $z$** *outside*. This means a doubling of $\sigma_L$ (the supply-side standard deviation) does **not** double safety stock — but for typical small $\sigma_L$ relative to $\bar{L}$, the marginal impact is *very large*. Always show this calculation explicitly for full KTU marks.

## 2.4 Engineering / Real-World Utility

- **Procurement managers** use these formulas to negotiate *supplier reliability SLAs* (service-level agreements), since every day of reduced $\sigma_L$ translates directly into *lower working-capital tied up in safety stock*.
- **Risk managers** quantify the financial exposure to disruption events (think COVID-19, Suez Canal blockage) and decide on *dual sourcing*, *near-shoring*, or *strategic safety stock*.
- **Operations strategists** benchmark their firm's supply uncertainty against industry medians (e.g., electronics: $\sigma_L \approx 1.5$ days; pharma: $\sigma_L \approx 0.5$ days).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Safety Stock with Combined Demand & Lead-Time Uncertainty

We model demand $D$ as a normal random variable with mean $\bar{d}$ (units/day) and standard deviation $\sigma_{D}$ (units/day). Lead time $L$ is independent, with mean $\bar{L}$ (days) and standard deviation $\sigma_{L}$ (days).

**Step 1.** The total demand over the lead time is:

$$
D_{LT} = D \cdot L
$$

**Step 2.** Taking the expectation (mean demand over lead time):

$$
E[D_{LT}] = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L}
$$

**Step 3.** Treating $D$ and $L$ as independent, the variance of the product is given by the standard identity:

$$
\text{Var}(XY) = (E[X])^{2} \cdot \text{Var}(Y) + (E[Y])^{2} \cdot \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y)
$$

For *small* coefficient of variation, the last term is negligible. Hence:

$$
\text{Var}(D_{LT}) \approx \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_{D}^{\,2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{\,2}
$$

**Step 4.** Standard deviation of lead-time demand:

$$
\sigma_{d} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_{D}^{\,2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 5.** Safety stock is the buffer needed to cover demand above the expected level, at the desired service level $z$:

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_{d} = z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_{D}^{\,2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{\,2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the "Impact of Supply Uncertainty" formula:** If $\sigma_L = 0$ (deterministic lead time), we recover the familiar $SS = z \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$. The *extra* term $z \cdot \bar{d} \cdot \sigma_L$ is precisely the *incremental* safety stock caused by supply uncertainty.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement:** A KTU-registered electronics assembly unit in Kochi sources a specific microchip from Singapore. Data:

- Average daily demand: $\bar{d} = 100$ units
- Std. dev. of daily demand: $\sigma_D = 20$ units/day
- Average lead time: $\bar{L} = 10$ days
- Std. dev. of lead time: $\sigma_L = 2$ days
- Desired cycle service level: $97.5\%$ → $z = 1.96$

**Find:** (i) The safety stock required. (ii) The *incremental* safety stock due to supply uncertainty. (iii) The Reorder Point (ROP).

**Solution (Step-by-step):**

**Step (i) — Compute the lead-time-demand standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{(10)(20^{2}) + (100^{2})(2^{2})}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{(10)(400) + (10{,}000)(4)}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{4{,}000 + 40{,}000} = \sqrt{44{,}000}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = 209.76 \text{ units (approx.)}
$$

**Step (ii) — Compute the safety stock:**

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_d = 1.96 \times 209.76
$$

$$
SS = 411.13 \text{ units (approx. } 412 \text{ units)}
$$

**Step (iii) — Compute the incremental safety stock from supply uncertainty:**

The safety stock with *zero* lead-time variability ($\sigma_L = 0$) is:

$$
SS_{demand-only} = z \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}} = 1.96 \times 20 \times \sqrt{10}
$$

$$
SS_{demand-only} = 1.96 \times 20 \times 3.162 = 123.96 \text{ units}
$$

Therefore, the **incremental** safety stock due to supply uncertainty:

$$
\Delta SS = SS - SS_{demand-only} = 412 - 124 = 288 \text{ units}
$$

This is a **~232% increase** over the demand-only baseline — a dramatic illustration of the impact of supply uncertainty.

**Step (iv) — Reorder Point:**

$$
ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS = (100)(10) + 412 = 1{,}412 \text{ units}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Safety Stock for Supply Disruption

For a *complete* disruption of duration $R$ days (a discrete event), the firm must hold enough safety stock to cover *all* demand during $R$:

$$
SS_{disrupt} = \bar{d} \cdot (L_{max} - \bar{L})
$$

where $L_{max}$ is the worst-case (e.g., 95th percentile) replenishment lead time. If the supply is *completely halted* for $R$ days:

$$
SS_{disrupt} = \bar{d} \cdot R
$$

**Example:** A pharmaceutical distributor in Thiruvananthapuram faces a 14-day strike risk at its sole supplier. Daily demand $\bar{d} = 50$ vials. The safety stock required is:

$$
SS_{disrupt} = 50 \times 14 = 700 \text{ vials}
$$

Compare this with the continuous-variability formula: for $\bar{L}=7$, $\sigma_L=1$, $\sigma_D=10$, $z=1.65$ (95%):

$$
SS_{continuous} = 1.65 \times \sqrt{(7)(10^{2}) + (50^{2})(1^{2})} = 1.65 \times \sqrt{700 + 2{,}500} = 1.65 \times 56.57 = 93 \text{ vials}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7.5x difference** (700 vs 93 vials) shows that **disruption risk dominates** continuous variability in driving safety inventory. This is why risk-aware firms use *scenario-based dual sourcing* rather than blindly applying continuous-variability formulas.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Managerial Levers

| **Lever** | **Effect on $\sigma_L$** | **Effect on $SS$** | **Trade-off** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switch to more reliable supplier | $\sigma_L \downarrow$ | $SS \downarrow$ | higher unit cost |
| Dual sourcing | $\sigma_L \downarrow$ (effectively) | $SS \downarrow$ | supplier mgmt. complexity |
| Safety lead time (use $L_{max}$ as $\bar{L}$) | $SS \uparrow$ | $SS \uparrow$ | higher holding cost |
| Vendor-managed inventory (VMI) | $\sigma_L \downarrow$, $\sigma_D \downarrow$ | $SS \downarrow$ | loss of control |
| Shorter / more frequent replenishments | $\bar{L} \downarrow$ | $SS \downarrow$ | higher ordering cost |
| Postponement / modular design | $\sigma_D \downarrow$ | $SS \downarrow$ | product-design constraint |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Causal Flow — Supply Uncertainty to Safety Inventory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply Uncertainty Sources] --> B[Lead Time Variability]
    A --> C[Yield / Quality Variability]
    A --> D[Disruption Events]
    B --> E[Variance Inflation of LT Demand]
    C --> F[Effective Demand Rises]
    D --> G[Catastrophic Stockout Risk]
    E --> H[Safety Stock SS Increases]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Disruption Safety Stock SS_disrupt]
    H --> J[Reorder Point ROP Shifts Up]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Higher Holding Cost h times SS]
    J --> L[Target Service Level Met]
    H --> M[Managerial Levers]
    I --> M
    M --> N[Dual Sourcing]
    M --> O[Supplier SLAs]
    M --> P[Safety Lead Time Policy]
    M --> Q[VMI Contracts]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Safety Inventory Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Data Block]
        I1[Average Daily Demand d bar]
        I2[Demand Std Dev sigma D]
        I3[Average Lead Time L bar]
        I4[Lead Time Std Dev sigma L]
        I5[Target Service Level percent]
        I6[Worst Case Disruption Days R]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Engine Block]
        P1[Variance Combination: L bar times sigma D squared plus d bar squared times sigma L squared]
        P2[Effective Std Dev sigma d]
        P3[Safety Factor Lookup: z from Normal Table]
        P4[Continuous SS Calculation: z times sigma d]
        P5[Disruption SS Calculation: d bar times R]
        P6[Max of Continuous and Disruption]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Decision Block]
        O1[Final Safety Stock SS]
        O2[Reorder Point ROP: d bar times L bar plus SS]
        O3[Annual Holding Cost: SS times h]
        O4[Service Level Achieved]
    end
    I1 --> P2
    I1 --> P5
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I3 --> O2
    I4 --> P1
    I5 --> P3
    I6 --> P5
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> O1
    I1 --> O2
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    P6 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Continuous vs Disruption Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify Supply Risk Type] --> T{Is supply lost entirely for R days?}
    T -- Yes --> P1[Disruption Pathway]
    T -- No --> P2[Variability Pathway]
    P1 --> Q1[Use SS_disrupt equals d bar times R]
    P2 --> Q2[Use SS equals z times sigma d]
    Q1 --> R1[Final SS equals max of Q1 and Q2]
    Q2 --> R1
    R1 --> END[Set ROP and Reorder Quantity]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between **demand uncertainty** and **supply uncertainty**. How does each affect safety inventory in a supply chain?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Demand uncertainty** is the unpredictable variation in *customer demand* (outflow from inventory). It is mitigated by forecasting, demand aggregation, and postponement. Impact: drives safety stock via $SS = z \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$ when lead times are deterministic.
- **Supply uncertainty** is the unpredictable variation in *incoming replenishment* (inflow to inventory) — including lead-time variability, yield loss, and disruption events. It is largely outside the firm's direct control.
- **Combined impact:** when both exist, the safety stock formula becomes $SS = z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}$, with the second term being the *supply-uncertainty contribution*. (3 marks: 1 mark for each distinction + 1 mark for combined formula)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State and explain the **two types of supply uncertainty** that a firm must consider when designing its safety inventory policy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Supply Variability (Continuous):** Random fluctuations in supplier lead time and delivered quantity. Quantified by $\sigma_L$ and yield variance. Handled statistically via the lead-time-demand variance formula. (1.5 marks)
2. **Supply Disruption (Discrete):** Sudden complete stoppage of supply for a defined duration $R$ (e.g., natural disaster, strike, supplier insolvency). Handled by $SS_{disrupt} = \bar{d} \cdot R$, which typically *dominates* continuous variability. (1.5 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU ESE Module 3):** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A Kerala-based spice exporter sources cinnamon from a single supplier in Sri Lanka. The exporter's procurement data are:

- Average daily demand: $\bar{d} = 200$ kg/day
- Std. dev. of daily demand: $\sigma_D = 40$ kg/day
- Average lead time: $\bar{L} = 15$ days
- Std. dev. of lead time: $\sigma_L = 3$ days
- Target service level: $98\%$ ($z = 2.05$)
- Holding cost: $h = \text{Rs. } 50$ per kg per year

**(a)** Compute the **safety stock** required, taking into account both demand and supply uncertainty. Also calculate the **Reorder Point (ROP)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The exporter is considering an alternate supplier (Supplier B) who can guarantee a *deterministic* lead time of 15 days (i.e., $\sigma_L = 0$) at a 5% higher unit cost. Determine the **annualized cost of safety stock** with each supplier. Should the exporter switch? Show the financial impact clearly. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution to (a):**

Step 1 — Compute lead-time-demand standard deviation:

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{(15)(40^{2}) + (200^{2})(3^{2})} = \sqrt{(15)(1600) + (40{,}000)(9)}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{24{,}000 + 360{,}000} = \sqrt{384{,}000} = 619.68 \text{ kg}
$$

Step 2 — Safety stock:

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_d = 2.05 \times 619.68 = 1{,}270.34 \approx 1{,}271 \text{ kg}
$$

Step 3 — Reorder Point:

$$
ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS = (200)(15) + 1{,}271 = 3{,}000 + 1{,}271 = 4{,}271 \text{ kg}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for $\sigma_d$ derivation + 2 marks for SS + 1 mark for ROP + 2 marks for substitution clarity)*

**Solution to (b):**

Step 1 — Safety stock with Supplier B (deterministic lead time):

$$
SS_B = z \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}} = 2.05 \times 40 \times \sqrt{15}
$$

$$
SS_B = 2.05 \times 40 \times 3.873 = 317.55 \approx 318 \text{ kg}
$$

Step 2 — Annual holding cost (Supplier A):

$$
H_A = SS_A \cdot h = 1{,}271 \times 50 = \text{Rs. } 63{,}550 \text{ per year}
$$

Step 3 — Annual holding cost (Supplier B):

$$
H_B = SS_B \cdot h = 318 \times 50 = \text{Rs. } 15{,}900 \text{ per year}
$$

Step 4 — **Annual savings in holding cost by switching:**

$$
\Delta H = 63{,}550 - 15{,}900 = \text{Rs. } 47{,}650 \text{ per year}
$$

Step 5 — **Cost of 5% price increase (Supplier B):**

Let the unit cost be $c$ Rs/kg. Annual purchase volume = $200 \times 365 = 73{,}000$ kg.

$$
\text{Additional purchase cost} = 0.05c \times 73{,}000 = 3{,}650c \text{ Rs/year}
$$

**Decision rule:** Switch if $3{,}650c < 47{,}650$, i.e., if $c < \text{Rs. } 13.05$/kg. For most spice-export scenarios, unit cost is well above this, so the *service-level cost trade-off must be evaluated*, but the **holding-cost savings alone are substantial**.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for $SS_B$ + 2 marks for $H_A$/$H_B$ + 2 marks for decision logic + 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

A furniture manufacturer in Ernakulam sources teak wood from a Mysore-based vendor. The procurement profile is:

- Average daily demand: $\bar{d} = 50$ units/day
- Std. dev. of daily demand: $\sigma_D = 8$ units/day
- Average lead time: $\bar{L} = 8$ days
- Std. dev. of lead time: $\sigma_L = 1.5$ days
- Target service level: $99\%$ ($z = 2.33$)

**(a)** Calculate the safety stock and the Reorder Point under the **current** supply uncertainty. Compute the **incremental** safety stock caused purely by supply variability. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The manufacturer has identified a **disruption risk**: a 12-day complete halt in supply is possible once every 5 years. Compute the **disruption safety stock** required and the **expected annual disruption cost** assuming a stockout penalty of Rs. 800 per unit per occurrence. Recommend an action plan. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution to (a):**

Step 1 — Effective demand standard deviation:

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{(8)(8^{2}) + (50^{2})(1.5^{2})} = \sqrt{(8)(64) + (2{,}500)(2.25)}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{512 + 5{,}625} = \sqrt{6{,}137} = 78.34 \text{ units}
$$

Step 2 — Safety stock:

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_d = 2.33 \times 78.34 = 182.53 \approx 183 \text{ units}
$$

Step 3 — Reorder Point:

$$
ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS = (50)(8) + 183 = 400 + 183 = 583 \text{ units}
$$

Step 4 — Incremental SS from supply variability:

$$
SS_{demand-only} = z \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}} = 2.33 \times 8 \times \sqrt{8} = 2.33 \times 8 \times 2.828 = 52.72 \approx 53 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\Delta SS = 183 - 53 = 130 \text{ units}
$$

This is a **245% inflation** in safety stock purely due to supply uncertainty.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for $\sigma_d$ + 2 marks for SS/ROP + 2 marks for incremental $\Delta SS$ + 1 mark for percentage insight)*

**Solution to (b):**

Step 1 — Disruption safety stock:

$$
SS_{disrupt} = \bar{d} \cdot R = 50 \times 12 = 600 \text{ units}
$$

Step 2 — Compare with continuous-variability SS (183 units):

The disruption case **dominates** → final SS should be $\max(183, 600) = 600$ units.

Step 3 — Expected annual disruption cost (without holding disruption stock):

Expected stockout units during a 12-day halt ≈ $\bar{d} \cdot R = 600$ units. (Assuming complete stockout.)

$$
E[\text{annual penalty}] = \frac{1}{5} \times 600 \times 800 = \text{Rs. } 96{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

Step 4 — Cost of holding disruption stock:

$$
H_{disrupt} = SS_{disrupt} \cdot h = 600 \times h \text{ Rs/year}
$$

If $h = 100$ Rs/unit/yr, $H_{disrupt} = \text{Rs. } 60{,}000$/yr, which is *less* than the expected penalty of Rs. 96,000.

**Recommendation:** Hold the disruption-level safety stock of **600 units**. The annual savings (Rs. 36,000) and the service-level protection during a major disruption strongly justify the policy. Consider *dual sourcing* as a long-term alternative if $h$ exceeds Rs. 160/unit/yr.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for $SS_{disrupt}$ + 2 marks for comparison with continuous SS + 2 marks for expected penalty + 1 mark for recommendation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\bar{d}^{2} \sigma_L^{2}$ term** when computing $\sigma_d$ — this is the *only* place where supply uncertainty enters, and skipping it loses **3–4 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Using $z$ values from the wrong tail** of the standard normal table (e.g., $z=1.65$ for 95% one-tail vs $z=1.96$ for 97.5%). KTU examiners *will* deduct.
> 3. **Confusing service-level definitions** — Type-1 (fill rate) vs Type-2 (cycle service level) require different $z$ lookups.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units** (e.g., $\bar{d}$ in units/day, $\bar{L}$ in days — they must be consistent).
> 5. **Skipping the Reorder Point calculation** — even if the question does not explicitly ask, ROP is the natural follow-up and earns easy marks.
> 6. **Not comparing disruption vs continuous SS** — KTU 2024 questions increasingly test the student's ability to take the $\max$ of the two.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Safety inventory** protects against both demand *and* supply variability.
- Supply uncertainty has **two forms**: **variability** (continuous, statistical) and **disruption** (discrete, catastrophic).
- The **master formula** combining both demand and lead-time variability is:
$$
SS = z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}
$$
- The **incremental** supply-uncertainty contribution to SS is $z \cdot \bar{d} \cdot \sigma_L$ — often the *dominant* term in practice.
- **Disruption safety stock** is $SS_{disrupt} = \bar{d} \cdot R$ and frequently *exceeds* continuous-variability SS by an order of magnitude.
- **Reorder Point** = $\bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS$ — always compute and quote in numerical answers.
- **Holding cost** of safety stock = $SS \cdot h$ — the financial lever in supplier-switching decisions.
- **Managerial levers**: dual sourcing, supplier SLAs, safety lead time, VMI, and postponement.
- For KTU ESE numericals: **always show $z$ value**, **always include units**, and **compare both continuous and disruption SS** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Aggregation on Safety Inventory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Impact of Aggregation on Safety Inventory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Safety Inventory** (also termed *safety stock* or *buffer stock*) is the inventory carried by a firm to safeguard against the **uncertainties** associated with both **customer demand fluctuations** and the **firm's supply lead time**. It is the extra inventory held above the expected demand during the lead time so that the desired **cycle service level (CSL)** or **fill rate** can be maintained.

**Aggregation**, in the context of supply chain inventory, refers to the **strategic consolidation** of inventory — whether across **products** (inventory aggregation), **locations** (spatial aggregation / centralization), or **time periods** (temporal aggregation) — so that safety inventory can be pooled and shared.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Anchor):**
> *Aggregation exploits the statistical principle that the variability of the sum of independent random variables grows with the **square root** of the number of variables, not linearly. Hence, consolidating demand streams reduces the total safety inventory required to achieve a given service level.*

The mathematical backbone of this concept is the **Square-Root Law of Safety Inventory**, which states:

$$
SS_{aggregated} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SS_{single}
$$

where $N$ is the number of aggregated entities (locations or products).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rain Bucket Principle"

Imagine **5 friends**, each living in a different city, and each carrying a personal **rain bucket** (their safety stock) to catch unexpected rainfall (demand surge).

- If each friend carries a bucket sized for the **worst possible rain** in *their* city alone, the **total water carried = 5 × worst-case rain**.
- Now suppose all 5 friends agree to **share one giant bucket** placed centrally. The rains in the 5 cities are *mostly independent* — it rarely pours in all 5 cities on the same day.
- Statistically, the **shared bucket** only needs to be large enough for the **average peak rainfall × √5** — because the highs and lows cancel each other out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Aggregation does NOT mean "more inventory goes to waste." It means **fewer total units of safety stock** are needed to deliver the **same protection** against uncertainty. The savings follow the famous **√N curve** — a classic example of *risk-pooling* in operations management.

---

## 1.3 Core Metrics to Remember

- **$z$** — Number of standard deviations from the mean corresponding to the desired service level (e.g., $z = 1.65$ for 95 % CSL, $z = 2.33$ for 99 % CSL).
- **$\sigma_{DL}$** — Standard deviation of demand during lead time.
- **$L$** — Replenishment lead time.
- **$N$** — Number of aggregated entities (locations/products).
- **$CV$** — Coefficient of Variation = $\sigma / \mu$ — a normalized measure of demand variability.
- **CSL** — Cycle Service Level = Probability of not stocking out during a cycle.
- **Aggregation Factor** — $\sqrt{N}$ — the multiplier that captures risk-pooling benefit.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Cue:** Always state the **assumptions of independence** explicitly when using the square-root formula. KTU examiners award marks for explicitly listing the assumptions of the Square-Root Law.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of the Concept

### Step 1 — Establish Baseline Safety Inventory (No Aggregation)

When a single location (or single product) faces uncertain demand during lead time $L$, the safety inventory required to achieve a service level of $z$ standard deviations is given by the classical formula:

$$
SS_1 = z \cdot \sigma_{DL}
$$

where $\sigma_{DL}$ is the standard deviation of demand **over the lead time**.

If demand is stationary and lead time is deterministic, then:

$$
\sigma_{DL} = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

so that:

$$
SS_1 = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

> **Why this matters:** This is the "single-bucket" cost. Every location operating **independently** must hold this much safety stock.

---

### Step 2 — Aggregating Demand Across N Independent Locations

Suppose we now consolidate $N$ previously independent retail locations into a **single centralized warehouse**. Each location $i$ has demand $D_i$ with mean $\mu_i$ and standard deviation $\sigma_i$, assumed **mutually independent**.

- **Total Mean Demand:** $\mu_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i$
- **Total Standard Deviation (since independent):**

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sigma_i^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Square Root:** Variances add for *independent* random variables, but standard deviations add in **quadrature** — i.e., we sum variances, then take the square root. This is the **mathematical engine** behind the entire aggregation benefit.

If all $N$ locations are *identical* ($\sigma_i = \sigma_d$ for all $i$):

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{N \cdot \sigma_d^2} = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{N}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Safety Inventory With Aggregation

The safety inventory required at the centralized location to achieve the **same service level** $z$ is:

$$
SS_{centralized} = z \cdot \sigma_{total} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{N} \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

Equivalently, in terms of the **per-location** safety stock before aggregation:

$$
SS_{centralized} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SS_1
$$

where $SS_1 = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$.

---

### Step 4 — Comparing Aggregated vs. Decentralized Systems

| System Type | Total Safety Inventory Held | Inventory Multiple |
|---|---|---|
| **Decentralized** (N independent locations) | $N \cdot SS_1$ | $N$ times |
| **Centralized** (one shared warehouse) | $\sqrt{N} \cdot SS_1$ | $\sqrt{N}$ times |
| **Savings from Aggregation** | $(N - \sqrt{N}) \cdot SS_1$ | $N - \sqrt{N}$ times |

> **Engineering Intuition:** Centralization does **not** reduce safety stock per unit of demand; it reduces it **per unit of risk**. Because pooled demands fluctuate less in relative terms, the same hedge costs less in absolute units.

---

### Step 5 — Effect of Correlation (Critical for KTU)

The square-root law assumes **zero correlation** between the demands being aggregated. In reality:

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{\sum_i \sigma_i^2 + 2 \sum_{i < j} \rho_{ij} \sigma_i \sigma_j}
$$

- If $\rho = 0$ (independent) → Maximum pooling benefit, $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_d\sqrt{N}$.
- If $\rho = 1$ (perfectly correlated) → $\sigma_{total} = N \sigma_d$, **no benefit**.
- If $\rho < 0$ (negatively correlated) → Benefit is **even greater** than $\sqrt{N}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "aggregation always reduces safety inventory." This is FALSE. Aggregation benefits **vanish** when demands are perfectly correlated. The square-root law is a **best-case** benchmark.

---

### Step 6 — Information's Role in Substitution

Better forecasting and information systems can reduce demand variability $\sigma_d$ directly. Combining **information improvement** with **aggregation** yields a *multiplicative* reduction in safety inventory:

$$
SS_{new} = z \cdot \sigma_d' \cdot \sqrt{N} \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

where $\sigma_d' < \sigma_d$. Information is therefore a **partial substitute** for safety inventory.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SS_1 = z \cdot \sigma_{DL} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Safety stock at **one** location | Units = demand units |
| 2 | $\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sigma_i^2}$ | Aggregated std. dev. (independent demands) | Used before applying $z$ |
| 3 | $SS_{aggregated} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{N} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Safety stock after **spatial aggregation** | $\sqrt{N}$ multiplier |
| 4 | $SS_{aggregated} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SS_1$ | Aggregation gain expressed as a multiple | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | $SS_{inventory\,agg} = z \cdot \sqrt{\sum_j \sigma_j^2} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Across-**product** aggregation | $j$ indexes SKUs |
| 6 | $\sigma_{total}^2 = \sum_i \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i<j} \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$ | With **correlation** $\rho_{ij}$ | $\rho=0 \Rightarrow$ max benefit |
| 7 | Savings $ = (N - \sqrt{N}) \cdot SS_1$ | Inventory units freed up by centralization | Positive for $N \ge 1$ |
| 8 | $\rho = \text{Corr}(D_i, D_j)$ | Pearson correlation between demands | $\rho \in [-1, 1]$ |
| 9 | $CV = \sigma_d / \mu_d$ | Coefficient of Variation (normalized variability) | Dimensionless |

> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "what is the effect of aggregation?", always mention **(a) the square-root law, (b) the assumption of independence, (c) the role of correlation, and (d) the role of information** — this is a complete 7-mark answer.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility

- **Retail Networks:** Walmart and Amazon centralize slow-moving SKUs in regional DCs and use the $\sqrt{N}$ logic to justify inventory pooling.
- **Spare Parts Logistics:** Aircraft and heavy-industry firms pool MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Overhaul) parts across hubs — a 10-hub network needs only $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$ times the safety stock of a single hub, not 10 times.
- **Blood Bank Management:** Cross-regional aggregation of blood inventories reduces wastage while preserving service levels — a textbook medical-supply application.
- **Pharmaceutical Cold Chains:** Risk pooling across pharmacies reduces total cold-storage buffer needed.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Square-Root Law of Safety Inventory

### Derivation Objective
Show that if $N$ independent, identically distributed demand streams are aggregated, the total safety inventory required grows as $\sqrt{N}$, not as $N$.

### Given
- $N$ locations, each with daily demand $D_i$, $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$.
- Each $D_i$ is normally distributed: $D_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_d, \sigma_d^2)$.
- All $D_i$ are **pairwise independent**, i.e., $\text{Cov}(D_i, D_j) = 0$ for $i \ne j$.
- Replenishment lead time $L$ is deterministic and identical at all locations.
- Target cycle service level: probability of no stockout in a cycle = CSL, corresponding to a $z$-value.

### Step 1 — Demand Over Lead Time at a Single Location
The total demand during lead time $L$ at location $i$ is the sum of $L$ independent daily demands:

$$
DL_i = D_{i,1} + D_{i,2} + \dots + D_{i,L}
$$

Because the $D_{i,k}$ are i.i.d. normal with mean $\mu_d$ and variance $\sigma_d^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[DL_i] &= L \cdot \mu_d \\
\text{Var}(DL_i) &= L \cdot \sigma_d^2 \\
\sigma_{DL_i} &= \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Safety Stock at a Single Location
Safety stock is the $z$-multiple of the standard deviation of demand during lead time:

$$
SS_1 = z \cdot \sigma_{DL_i} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Demand Across N Aggregated Locations
When we consolidate $N$ locations into one centralized pool, total demand during lead time is:

$$
DL_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} DL_i
$$

Mean:

$$
E[DL_{total}] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L \cdot \mu_d = N \cdot L \cdot \mu_d
$$

Variance (using independence — cross-product terms vanish):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(DL_{total}) &= \text{Var}\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} DL_i \right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Var}(DL_i) + 2 \sum_{i<j} \text{Cov}(DL_i, DL_j) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} L \sigma_d^2 + 0 \\
&= N \cdot L \cdot \sigma_d^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Standard deviation:

$$
\sigma_{DL_{total}} = \sqrt{N \cdot L} \cdot \sigma_d = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{N \cdot L}
$$

### Step 4 — Safety Stock at the Centralized Location
Applying the same $z$-value to maintain the same CSL:

$$
SS_{centralized} = z \cdot \sigma_{DL_{total}} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{N \cdot L}
$$

### Step 5 — Expressing in Terms of $SS_1$
Dividing the centralized safety stock by the single-location safety stock:

$$
\frac{SS_{centralized}}{SS_1} = \frac{z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{N \cdot L}}{z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}} = \sqrt{N}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{SS_{centralized} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SS_1}
$$

This is the celebrated **Square-Root Law of Safety Inventory**.

### Step 6 — Magnitude of the Savings
- Total decentralized safety stock: $N \cdot SS_1$
- Total centralized safety stock: $\sqrt{N} \cdot SS_1$
- Absolute savings: $N \cdot SS_1 - \sqrt{N} \cdot SS_1 = (N - \sqrt{N}) \cdot SS_1$
- **Percentage savings:** $\dfrac{N - \sqrt{N}}{N} \times 100\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right) \times 100\%$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A retail chain has 4 independent stores. Each store has daily demand $D \sim \mathcal{N}(100,\, 20^2)$ and lead time $L = 9$ days. Target CSL = 95 % (so $z = 1.65$).

**Solution — Step by step:**

1. $\sigma_d = 20$, $L = 9$, $z = 1.65$, $N = 4$.

2. Safety stock at one store:

$$
SS_1 = 1.65 \times 20 \times \sqrt{9} = 1.65 \times 20 \times 3 = 99 \text{ units}
$$

3. Safety stock for all 4 stores **independently**:

$$
SS_{decentralized} = 4 \times 99 = 396 \text{ units}
$$

4. Safety stock at a **centralized** DC:

$$
SS_{centralized} = 1.65 \times 20 \times \sqrt{4 \times 9} = 1.65 \times 20 \times 6 = 198 \text{ units}
$$

5. Or simply: $SS_{centralized} = \sqrt{4} \times 99 = 2 \times 99 = 198$ units ✓

6. **Savings:** $396 - 198 = 198$ units (a 50 % reduction!).

7. **Sanity check using percentage formula:**

$$
\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{4}}\right) \times 100\% = 50\% \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 50 % and not 25 %?** A common student error is to confuse "savings" with "remaining stock." When $N = 4$, you save $1 - 1/\sqrt{4} = 1 - 0.5 = 50\%$ of original safety stock, retaining only 50 %.

### Special Case: Impact of Positive Correlation

Suppose all 4 stores have $\rho = 0.5$ pairwise correlation (e.g., common weather/seasonality effect). Then:

$$
\sigma_{total}^2 = 4 \cdot 20^2 + 2 \cdot \binom{4}{2} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 20 \cdot 20
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{total}^2 &= 4 \cdot 400 + 12 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 400 \\
&= 1600 + 2400 = 4000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{4000} \approx 63.25
$$

$$
SS_{centralized} = 1.65 \times 63.25 \times \sqrt{9} = 313 \text{ units (vs. 198 uncorrelated)}
$$

> The benefit of aggregation **shrinks** as correlation rises — a critical KTU concept.

> **Interpretation:** The marginal benefit of adding a new location to a centralized pool **diminishes** rapidly — the first 4 locations save 50 %, the next 5 add only 17 % more. This is the *diminishing returns* of aggregation.
