---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
Define the term "Transportation Infrastructure Policy" and list its four key pillars as applicable to a developing economy.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Transportation Infrastructure Policy refers to the comprehensive set of governmental, regulatory, financial, and strategic frameworks that govern the planning, development, operation, and maintenance of physical and digital transport networks — including roadways, railways, waterways, airways, and pipelines — to facilitate the efficient movement of goods and people.

The **four key pillars** are:
1. **Regulatory and Institutional Framework** — Licensing, safety, tariff norms.
2. **Investment and Financing Framework** — PPP, VGF, sovereign funding.
3. **Multi-Modal Integration Framework** — Gati Shakti, MMLPs, intermodal coordination.
4. **Digital and Sustainability Framework** — FASTag, GPS tracking, green fuel mandates.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing pillars with brief relevance.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
What is the Logistics Cost-to-GDP ratio, and why is it considered a critical policy KPI?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Logistics Cost-to-GDP Ratio (LCR)** is defined as the total expenditure incurred on logistics activities (transportation, warehousing, inventory carrying, packaging, etc.) expressed as a percentage of a nation's Gross Domestic Product.

$$
LCR = \frac{\text{Total Logistics Cost}}{\text{GDP}} \times 100
$$

It is a critical policy KPI because:
- It reflects the **overall efficiency** of a nation's supply chain.
- A **lower LCR** indicates competitive pricing of goods in global markets.
- Developed economies typically maintain LCR at **8–9%**, while India targeted reducing its LCR from **~10% (2022)** to **~8% (2030)** under the National Logistics Policy.
- It directly impacts **industrial competitiveness, export viability, and consumer prices**.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for relevance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the four pillars of transportation infrastructure policy. Discuss how the **PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan** integrates these pillars to reduce logistics costs in India. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Transportation infrastructure policies are built on four interconnected pillars. Effective logistics governance requires simultaneous advancement of all four, as they exhibit strong interdependencies.

**Pillar 1 — Regulatory and Institutional Framework (1.5 marks):**
This pillar provides the legal architecture for transport operations. In India, the key agencies include MoRTH (roadways), Ministry of Railways, Ministry of Ports and Shipping, and the DGCA (aviation). The Logistics Division under the Department of Commerce acts as the apex coordinating body. The **Motor Vehicles Act, Railways Act, and Multimodal Transportation Act** form the legislative backbone.

**Pillar 2 — Investment and Financing Framework (1.5 marks):**
Infrastructure development is capital-intensive. India employs a hybrid model combining public investment (e.g., ₹10 lakh crore in **PM Gati Shakti**) with PPP frameworks. The **VGF (Viability Gap Funding)** mechanism bridges the financial viability gap for private concessionaires, capped at **20%** of the total project cost (40% for strategic projects in difficult areas).

**Pillar 3 — Multi-Modal Integration Framework (1.5 marks):**
This pillar addresses the historical fragmentation between transport modes. Programs like **Bharatmala** (roads), **Sagarmala** (ports), and **UDAN** (airways) are coordinated under one digital platform.

**Pillar 4 — Digital and Sustainability Framework (1.5 marks):**
This modern pillar mandates **FASTag-based tolling**, GPS-enabled vehicle tracking, RFID container tracking, and adoption of **green fuels (CNG, LNG, hydrogen)**. The National Logistics Policy introduces the **Unified Logistics Interface Platform (ULIP)** for real-time data exchange.

**PM Gati Shakti Integration (Bonus for completeness):**
Launched in 2021 with an investment outlay of ₹100 lakh crore, PM Gati Shakti is a **GIS-based digital platform** integrating **16 ministries** including railways, roads, ports, aviation, and telecom. It enables synchronized planning of infrastructure projects, reducing duplication and ensuring last-mile connectivity. It directly targets reducing logistics cost-to-GDP ratio from 14% (older estimates) to **~8%** by 2030.

---

**(b)** A government policy reduces railway freight tariffs by **20%**. Currently, road handles **55%** of freight and rail handles **30%**. The modal shift elasticity is **1.5**. Calculate the new modal shares assuming the entire shift comes from road. State two policy implications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $\%\Delta C = -20\%$
- $\eta = 1.5$
- $S_R^0 = 55\%$
- $S_{Rail}^0 = 30\%$

**Step 1 — Percentage change in rail share (2 marks):**

$$
\%\Delta S_{Rail} = \eta \times \%\Delta C = 1.5 \times (-20\%) = -30\%
$$

Interpreting the magnitude as a positive shift toward rail (since cost decreased):

$$
\Delta S_{Rail}^{rel} = +30\%
$$

**Step 2 — Absolute change (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta S_{Rail}^{abs} = S_{Rail}^0 \times \frac{30}{100} = 30 \times 0.30 = 9 \text{ percentage points}
$$

**Step 3 — New modal shares (1 mark):**
- New rail share: $30\% + 9\% = 39\%$
- New road share: $55\% - 9\% = 46\%$
- Other modes: $15\%$ (unchanged)

**Step 4 — Policy implications (1 mark):**
1. **Implication 1:** Despite a 20% tariff cut, the realized modal shift is constrained by logistical frictions (terminal capacity, last-mile road connectivity). Therefore, **infrastructure investment** must accompany tariff policy.
2. **Implication 2:** The shift reduces road congestion, lowers emissions, and improves overall logistics cost. Policymakers should consider **complementary incentives** for rail-based containerized movement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common error is treating the elasticity result as a direct percentage-point change in modal share. Always remember: $\%\Delta S_{Rail}$ is a **relative change** applied to the **base share**, not an absolute shift. Marks are deducted for skipping the multiplication step. Also, in policy questions, **name the specific Indian schemes** (Bharatmala, Sagarmala, Gati Shakti) — generic answers lose marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Alternative]

**(a)** Compare the policy frameworks for **roadways, railways, and waterways** in India. Highlight the lead ministry, key schemes, and one quantitative target for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Roadways (2.5 marks):**
- **Lead Ministry:** Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH).
- **Key Schemes:** **Bharatmala Pariyojana** (Phase I approved at ₹5.35 lakh crore), **NHDP**, **PMGSY** for rural roads.
- **Quantitative Target:** Expand National Highway network to **2 lakh km** by 2025; construct **65,000 km** of highways under Bharatmala.

**Railways (2.5 marks):**
- **Lead Ministry:** Ministry of Railways.
- **Key Schemes:** **Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFC)** — Eastern and Western corridors covering **3,300 km**; **PM Gati Shakti** rail expansion; **Kavach** (indigenous train protection system).
- **Quantitative Target:** Achieve freight loading of **3,000 MT** by 2030 (from ~1,500 MT currently); reduce transit time by **30%**.

**Waterways (2 marks):**
- **Lead Agency:** Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI); Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways.
- **Key Schemes:** **Sagarmala Programme**, **Jal Marg Vikas Project** on National Waterway-1 (Ganga).
- **Quantitative Target:** Increase waterways' modal share from **6%** to **12%** by 2030; develop **23,000 km** of navigable waterways.

---

**(b)** India's GDP is **₹300 lakh crore** and the current logistics cost is **₹27 lakh crore**. The National Logistics Policy targets a **2.5 percentage point reduction** in the LCR. Compute the new LCR, the target logistics cost, and the absolute savings. Comment on the implications for industrial competitiveness. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Current LCR (1.5 marks):**

$$
LCR_{current} = \frac{27}{300} \times 100 = 9\%
$$

**Step 2 — New Target LCR (1.5 marks):**

$$
LCR_{target} = 9\% - 2.5\% = 6.5\%
$$

**Step 3 — Target Logistics Cost (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Logistics Cost}_{target} = 0.065 \times 300 = 19.5 \text{ lakh crore}
$$

**Step 4 — Absolute Savings (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Savings} = 27 - 19.5 = 7.5 \text{ lakh crore} = \text{₹ 7.5 trillion}
$$

**Step 5 — Implications for Industrial Competitiveness (1 mark):**
- A 2.5 percentage point reduction frees **₹7.5 trillion** annually for productive investment.
- This makes Indian exports more price-competitive (lower landed cost in destination markets).
- It enhances the **Ease of Doing Business** ranking by reducing supply chain friction.
- Aligns India with global benchmarks (developed economies maintain LCR of **8–9%**), positioning India as a viable **alternative manufacturing hub to China**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Always express logistics cost in the **same unit** as GDP (both in lakh crore or both in trillion). Mixing units loses 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Interpretation of "percentage point" vs. "percent":** A reduction from 9% to 6.5% is a **2.5 percentage point** drop, not a 2.5% relative reduction. Students often confuse these.
> 3. **In policy comparison questions,** simply listing schemes without naming the **lead ministry** results in partial credit loss.
> 4. **In elasticity-based modal shift problems,** the most common error is failing to convert the **relative percentage change** into an **absolute share change** via multiplication with the base share.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Transportation Infrastructure Policy = Strategic framework governing the development, regulation, and operation of transport networks for efficient, safe, and sustainable movement of goods and people.
- **Four Pillars:** Regulatory & Institutional | Investment & Financing | Multi-Modal Integration | Digital & Sustainability.
- **Key Indian Policy Initiatives:** **National Logistics Policy 2022**, **PM Gati Shakti Master Plan**, **Bharatmala**, **Sagarmala**, **UDAN**, **Dedicated Freight Corridors**.
- **Critical KPI:** **Logistics Cost-to-GDP Ratio** — current ~9–10%, target ~8% by 2030.
- **VGF Formula:** $VGF = TPC - CC - \text{IRR Threshold Component}$. Capped at **20%** of TPC (40% for strategic projects).
- **Modal Shift Elasticity:** $\eta = \frac{\%\Delta \text{ Share}}{\%\Delta \text{ Cost}}$ — must be converted to **absolute** share change.
- **Transport Mode Hierarchy by Cost:** Waterways (cheapest per tonne-km) < Railways < Pipelines < Roadways < Airways (most expensive).
- **Sustainability Focus:** **Green logistics**, **alternative fuels (CNG, LNG, hydrogen)**, **EV freight corridors**, **carbon-neutral ports**.
- **Digital Infrastructure:** **FASTag**, **ULIP** (Unified Logistics Interface Platform), **RFID container tracking**, **PM Gati Shakti GIS platform**.
- **Global Benchmarking:** Compare India's LCR with **developed economies (~8–9%)** and **ASEAN benchmarks**.
- **Exam Tip:** Always cite a **named scheme + lead ministry + quantitative target** in policy questions for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Options for a Transportation and Logistics Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Options for a Transportation and Logistics Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Design Options for a Transportation and Logistics Network

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Transportation and Logistics Network** is the integrated configuration of facilities (plants, warehouses, distribution centers, cross-docks, retail outlets), transportation links, inventory positioning points, and information flows that collectively move raw materials, work-in-process (WIP) inventory, and finished goods from **suppliers → manufacturers → distributors → customers** at the lowest total landed cost while meeting target service levels.

The **Design Options** of such a network refer to the strategic structural alternatives a supply chain architect can choose from, namely the number, location, and capacity of facilities; the assignment of customer zones to facilities; the **mode(s) of transport** employed on each lane; the **inventory deployment policy**; and the degree of information-system integration. In Chopra & Meindl's canonical framework, network design is treated as a *long-term strategic decision* that locks in 70–80% of a firm's total supply chain cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 expects students to (a) enumerate design options, (b) justify a chosen configuration on cost–service trade-offs, and (c) compute the optimal number of facilities using the **Square Root Law**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kerala Postal Network

Imagine Kerala's postal system. If every post office had to deliver every letter directly to every household in the state, trucks would crisscross endlessly. Instead, the system uses a **Hub-and-Spoke** design: small sub-offices (spokes) collect mail and feed it to regional sorting hubs (Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, Kozhikode), which then dispatch it efficiently to distant spokes. Now imagine a competing design — a **Direct Shipping** model — where each post office uses its own van to deliver locally only. Both designs deliver mail, but at very different cost, speed, and reliability. Choosing between them — and many other options in between — is exactly what **transportation and logistics network design** is about.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Network Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Four Pillars:**
> 1. **Facility Location Decision** — Where to place plants, warehouses, DCs.
> 2. **Capacity Allocation** — How much capacity to build at each facility.
> 3. **Market-Assignment Decision** — Which customer zones each facility will serve.
> 4. **Sourcing Decision** — Whether to make or buy, and which supplier(s) to use.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Vocabulary)

| Metric | Symbol / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Landed Cost** | $C_{T}$ (₹) | Sum of facility, inventory, transportation, and information costs |
| **Service Level** | $\beta$ (%) | Fraction of demand fulfilled on time from inventory |
| **Lead Time** | $L$ (days) | Time from order placement to receipt |
| **Network Density** | $n$ (nodes) | Number of facilities in the network |
| **Fill Rate** | $\eta$ (%) | Probability demand is met immediately from stock |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Industrial Constant:** A full truckload (FTL) in Indian road transport typically carries **14–16 tonnes** at an average freight of **₹2.5–4.5 per tonne-km**. These are ballpark benchmarks used by KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strategic Decisions vs. Operational Decisions

Network design choices are **strategic**, **tactical**, and **operational**:

```
STRATEGIC (years)   → facility number & location
TACTICAL  (months)  → inventory policy, transportation mode mix
OPERATIONAL (days)  → truck routing, daily dispatch
```

## 2.2 The Six Major Design Options

### 2.2.1 Option 1 — Direct Shipping (Manufacturer → Customer)

- The manufacturer ships directly to the end customer, bypassing intermediate warehouses.
- **Inventory**: held mostly at the manufacturer.
- **Transportation**: LTL (less-than-truckload) or parcel shipments.
- **Best when**: customer density is high near the plant; order sizes are small; products are high-value.

### 2.2.2 Option 2 — Direct Shipping with Milk Runs

- One truck visits multiple suppliers (inbound) or multiple customers (outbound) on a fixed circular route.
- **Reduces** transportation cost per drop by consolidating volume.
- **Best when**: suppliers/customers are geographically clustered.

### 2.2.3 Option 3 — All Inventory at the Manufacturer (Drop-Shipping from Plant)

- Warehouse/retailer does **not** hold inventory; the manufacturer ships directly upon retailer order.
- **Information intensity is very high** (EDI/real-time linkage required).

### 2.2.4 Option 4 — Distributor / Wholesaler Network

- Inventory pooled at third-party distributors located between manufacturer and retailer.
- **Reduces** manufacturer lead time to retailer; increases retailer fill rate.
- **Cost**: additional handling & margin.

### 2.2.5 Option 5 — Manufacturer → Distribution Center (DC) → Retailer

- Classic **Hub-and-Spoke** for finished goods.
- DCs **consolidate**, **break-bulk**, and **replenish** retailers using full-truckload (FTL) economics.
- **Most common** in FMCG, electronics, apparel.

### 2.2.6 Option 6 — Manufacturer → Centralised DC → Multiple Spoke Warehouses → Retailer

- Two-echelon distribution; spokes act as forward stocking points.
- **Used for** very high service-level commitments (next-day delivery).

## 2.3 Transportation Mode Comparison

| Mode | Cost (₹/tonne-km) | Speed (km/day) | Reliability | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Air** | 25–50 | 1500+ | High | High-value, low-weight, urgent |
| **Road** | 2.5–4.5 | 400–500 | Moderate | Short-to-medium haul, door-to-door |
| **Rail** | 1.0–2.0 | 250–300 | Moderate | Bulk, long-haul, point-to-point |
| **Water** | 0.5–1.0 | 200–250 | Low | Bulky, non-urgent (cement, coal) |
| **Pipeline** | 0.3–0.6 | Continuous | Very High | Liquids & gases only |
| **Intermodal** | 1.5–3.0 | Varies | Moderate-High | Long distances, cost–speed balance |

## 2.4 The Trade-off Curve — Why More Facilities is NOT Always Better

| # Facilities | Facility Cost | Inventory Cost | Transportation Cost | **Total Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Few (1) | Low | High | High (long hauls) | **High** |
| Moderate (n*) | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | **Minimum** |
| Many (n → ∞) | Very High | Low | Low (short hauls) | **High** |

The optimum lies where **marginal facility cost = marginal reduction in inventory + transportation cost**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_T = C_F + C_I + C_{Tr} + C_{Info}$ | Total landed cost | ₹/year |
| 2 | $C_I = h \cdot \frac{D \cdot L}{2}$ | Cycle inventory (avg. pipeline stock) | ₹/year |
| 3 | $C_{Tr} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ | Outbound transportation cost | ₹/year |
| 4 | $h = c \cdot i$ | Holding cost per unit per year | ₹/unit-year |
| 5 | $EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{h}}$ | Economic Order quantity | units |
| 6 | $n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot c \cdot L}{2 \cdot F \cdot (1-\beta)}}$ | Optimal number of facilities (Square Root Law) | facilities |
| 7 | $R = \frac{L_{old}}{L_{new}} = \sqrt{\frac{n_{new}}{n_{old}}}$ | Square-root law for lead-time scaling | ratio |
| 8 | $C_{Tr/tonne} = \frac{F_c \cdot d}{Q}$ | Per-unit transport cost (variable) | ₹/tonne |
| 9 | $\text{Reorder Point} = d \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | Inventory trigger level | units |
| 10 | $\text{Safety Stock} = z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Buffer against demand variability | units |

**Legend (symbols):**
- $D$ = annual demand (units/yr) · $Q$ = order quantity · $S$ = fixed ordering/transport cost per shipment (₹)
- $h$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹) · $c$ = unit value (₹) · $i$ = holding rate (%)
- $F$ = annual fixed cost per facility (₹) · $\beta$ = target service level · $L$ = replenishment lead time (yr)
- $F_c$ = freight cost per tonne-km (₹) · $d$ = distance (km) · $z$ = z-score for service level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:** Question 4(b) of Module 4 (Part B, 14 marks) almost always evaluates the **Square Root Law** or the **Total Cost Trade-off Curve**. Memorise the trade-off curve shape and the $n^{*}$ derivation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce giants** (Amazon, Flipkart) use a hybrid of Options 5 & 6 — regional mother hubs + city-level sortation centers.
- **Cement industry** in India prefers Option 5 with rail + road intermodal.
- **FMCG firms** (HUL, ITC) use milk-runs (Option 2) for inbound raw-material collection from clusters of contract farmers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Square-Root Law for Optimal Number of Facilities

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 4 and is virtually guaranteed to appear as a Part-B sub-question.

### 3.1.1 Set-up and Assumptions

Assume $n$ identical warehouses serve a region with total annual demand $D$ distributed uniformly. Each warehouse holds safety stock and cycle stock, replenished from a central plant with lead time $L$ (in years). Each warehouse has fixed annual operating cost $F$.

**Total relevant cost** (ignoring constant terms) is:

$$
C_T(n) = n \cdot F \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{D}{n} \cdot h \cdot \frac{L}{2}}_{\text{inventory cost}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{D}{n} \cdot s \cdot d_{\text{inbound}}}_{\text{inbound transport}}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Minimisation

To find the optimum $n^{*}$, take the derivative of $C_T$ with respect to $n$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{dC_T}{dn} = F \;-\; \frac{D \cdot h \cdot L}{2 n^{2}} \;-\; \frac{D \cdot s \cdot d_{\text{inbound}}}{n^{2}} \;=\; 0
$$

Combine the two negative terms:

$$
F \;=\; \frac{D}{n^{2}}\left(\frac{h L}{2} + s \cdot d_{\text{inbound}}\right)
$$

Solve for $n^{2}$:

$$
n^{2} \;=\; \frac{D \left(\frac{h L}{2} + s \cdot d_{\text{inbound}}\right)}{F}
$$

Take the square root of both sides:

$$
n^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{D \left(\frac{h L}{2} + s \cdot d_{\text{inbound}}\right)}{F}}
$$

Expressed in compact KTU notation:

$$
n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot c \cdot L}{2 \cdot F \cdot (1-\beta)}}
$$

This is the **Square-Root Law**. Notice that $n^{*}$ grows as $\sqrt{D}$, not as $D$ — meaning **doubling demand increases the optimal number of facilities by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$**.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A firm has annual demand $D = 100{,}000$ units. Lead time $L = 0.1$ year. Unit cost $c = ₹500$. Holding rate $i = 20\%$. Target service level $\beta = 0.95$, so $(1-\beta) = 0.05$. Fixed cost per facility $F = ₹200{,}000$/yr.

**Step 1 — Holding cost per unit per year:**

$$
h = c \cdot i = 500 \times 0.20 = ₹100/\text{unit-year}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Square-Root Law:**

$$
n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{100{,}000 \times 100 \times 0.1}{2 \times 200{,}000 \times 0.05}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
100{,}000 \times 100 \times 0.1 = 1{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
2 \times 200{,}000 \times 0.05 = 20{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Final ratio and square root:**

$$
n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}000{,}000}{20{,}000}} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07
$$

**Result:** Round up to **8 warehouses**. The answer must be rounded **UP** (never down), because the integer constraint requires the firm to serve demand with at least $n^{*}$ facilities; rounding down would violate the service-level guarantee.

## 3.2 The Square-Root Scaling of Lead Time

If a firm replaces its existing $n_{old}$ warehouses with $n_{new}$ consolidated facilities, the new average inbound lead time scales as:

$$
L_{new} = L_{old} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{n_{old}}{n_{new}}}
$$

### 3.2.1 Quick Numerical Illustration

A firm currently has **16 warehouses** and an average lead time of **4 days** to customers. It consolidates to **4 warehouses**. New lead time:

$$
L_{new} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{16}{4}} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{4} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ days}
$$

**Trade-off:** Lead time doubles, but facility cost reduces by 75%. The firm must compensate by raising safety stock — illustrating the *facility-vs-inventory* trade-off central to Module 4.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Among Design Options

| Decision Criterion | Direct Ship | Milk Run | Distributor | Hub-and-Spoke (DC) | Multi-Echelon |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Order size | Small | Small–Med | Small | Medium–Large | Large |
| Customer density | High local | Clustered | Low | Dispersed | Highly dispersed |
| Product value | High | Med–High | Med | Med | Low–Med |
| Service level required | Very high | High | Moderate | High | Very high |
| Information intensity | Very high | High | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| Capital investment | Low | Low | None (outsourced) | High | Very High |
| Typical lead time | 1–3 days | 1–2 days | 3–7 days | 2–5 days | 1–2 days |
| Best Indian example | Amazon Now | ITC e-Choupal | FMCG wholesalers | BigBasket DCs | Reliance Jio infra |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> Explain the **Square Root Law** for the optimal number of facilities in a logistics network. Why does doubling demand **not** double the optimal facility count?

**Model Answer (3 marks — key points):**

1. **Statement (1 mark):** The Square Root Law states that the optimal number of facilities $n^{*}$ serving a region with annual demand $D$ is given by:

$$
n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot c \cdot L}{2 \cdot F \cdot (1-\beta)}}
$$

where $c$ is unit cost, $L$ lead time, $F$ fixed facility cost and $\beta$ service level.

2. **Why non-linear scaling (1 mark):** Because $n^{*} \propto \sqrt{D}$, a four-fold increase in demand only doubles the number of facilities — reflecting diminishing returns from each additional warehouse due to inventory pooling effects.

3. **Practical implication (1 mark):** Firms can serve larger demand growth with proportionally smaller facility expansion, but must hold correspondingly more safety stock at each remaining facility.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> Differentiate between **Direct Shipping** and **Hub-and-Spoke** network design options. State one situation where each is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Shipping | Hub-and-Spoke |
|---|---|---|
| Inventory location | At the plant | At central DC |
| Transport type | LTL / parcel | FTL to DC, then LTL to retailer |
| Cost structure | High transport, low facility | Lower long-haul cost, high DC cost |
| Best suited for | High-value, low-volume, dense urban demand | Bulky, medium-value, dispersed demand (e.g. FMCG, e-commerce) |
| Lead time | 1–3 days typical | 2–5 days typical |

**Preferred when (1 mark):**
- **Direct Shipping** → E-grocery in a metro, high product value, same-day delivery promise.
- **Hub-and-Spoke** → Pan-India distribution of FMCG or electronics via mother DCs at Kochi, Delhi, Mumbai.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Students answer **ONE** of two alternatives.

---

### Question B1 (A) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice 1]`

#### (a) Discuss the **six major design options** for a transportation and logistics network. Highlight the trade-offs between inventory, transportation, and facility costs in each case. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Option 1 — Direct Shipping:** Manufacturer ships directly to end customer. Inventory pooled at plant; transportation is LTL. Reduces facility cost but raises inbound freight and parcel cost. **Used by Amazon Now, BigBasket.** *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Option 2 — Direct Shipping via Milk Runs:** A single truck loops across multiple clustered customers. **Reduces transport cost per drop**, but increases delivery time and requires route optimisation. Used by ITC for agri-input collection. *(1 mark)*

3. **Option 3 — All Inventory at Manufacturer (Drop-Ship):** No intermediate warehouse; retailer acts as a virtual storefront. **Very low facility cost**, but requires real-time information systems and depends on the manufacturer's production schedule. *(1 mark)*

4. **Option 4 — Distributor Network:** Inventory held by independent wholesalers. **Reduces retailer lead time**, but adds a margin layer and complicates coordination. *(1 mark)*

5. **Option 5 — Manufacturer → DC → Retailer (Hub-and-Spoke):** Centralised DC consolidates full-truckload shipments from plant and dispatches to retailers. **Optimal balance** for most FMCG firms. *(1.5 marks)*

6. **Option 6 — Multi-Echelon (Mother Hub + Spokes):** Two-tier network. Highest service level and shortest final-mile lead time, but highest facility and inventory cost. Used by Reliance, Amazon Pan-India. *(1 mark)*

**Trade-off conclusion:** As the network moves from Option 1 → 6, **facility and inventory cost rise**, **transportation cost falls**, and **service level improves**. The optimum is found at minimum $C_T = C_F + C_I + C_{Tr} + C_{Info}$.

---

#### (b) An e-commerce firm has the following data: Annual demand $D = 1{,}00{,}000$ units, unit cost $c = ₹1{,}000$, holding rate $i = 25\%$, lead time $L = 0.08$ year, fixed facility cost $F = ₹5{,}00{,}000$/yr, target service level $\beta = 0.95$. **(i)** Find the optimal number of warehouses. **(ii)** If demand doubles, what is the new optimum? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Compute holding cost per unit:**

$$
h = c \cdot i = 1000 \times 0.25 = ₹250/\text{unit-year}
$$

**Apply the Square-Root Law:**

$$
n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot c \cdot L}{2 \cdot F \cdot (1-\beta)}} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}00{,}000 \times 250 \times 0.08}{2 \times 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05}}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
1{,}00{,}000 \times 250 \times 0.08 = 20{,}00{,}000
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
2 \times 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = 50{,}000
$$

**Result:**

$$
n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000}} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32
$$

Round up to **7 warehouses**. *(3 marks)*

**Part (ii) — Double demand to $D' = 2{,}00{,}000$:**

$$
n^{*}_{new} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}00{,}000 \times 250 \times 0.08}{50{,}000}} = \sqrt{80} \approx 8.94
$$

Round up to **9 warehouses**. *(2 marks)*

**Interpretation:** Demand doubled, but warehouses increased from 7 to 9 — only a 28.6% rise, demonstrating the $\sqrt{2}$ scaling rule. *(2 marks)*

---

### Question B1 (B) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice 2]`

#### (a) Compare the **five major modes of transportation** (air, road, rail, water, pipeline) on cost, speed, reliability, and capacity. State the criteria for selecting a mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Mode | Cost (₹/tonne-km) | Speed (km/day) | Reliability | Capacity per shipment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Air | 25–50 | 1500+ | Very High | 5–15 tonnes |
| Road | 2.5–4.5 | 400–500 | Moderate | 14–16 tonnes (FTL) |
| Rail | 1.0–2.0 | 250–300 | Moderate | 2000+ tonnes |
| Water | 0.5–1.0 | 200–250 | Low | 50,000+ tonnes |
| Pipeline | 0.3–0.6 | Continuous | Very High | Continuous |

*(4 marks for table)*

**Selection Criteria (3 marks):**
1. **Product characteristics** — perishables, hazardous, value-to-weight ratio.
2. **Speed requirement** — promised customer lead time.
3. **Cost** — total landed cost trade-off.
4. **Reliability & variability** — JIT compatibility.
5. **Capacity & lot size** — FTL vs LTL vs parcel.
6. **Distance & geography** — landlocked regions favour road/rail; coastal regions use sea.
7. **Environmental & regulatory constraints.**

---

#### (b) A company is evaluating whether to reduce its warehouses from **9 to 4**. The current average customer lead time is **3 days**. **(i)** Estimate the new lead time using the Square Root Law. **(ii)** If safety stock must be raised by the same ratio to maintain service, and current safety stock is 800 units, compute the new safety stock and explain the cost implication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Lead time scaling:**

$$
L_{new} = L_{old} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{n_{old}}{n_{new}}} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{9}{4}} = 3 \cdot 1.5 = 4.5 \text{ days}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Part (ii) — Safety stock scaling:**

The Square Root Law implies that consolidated networks require proportionally more safety stock at each facility because each facility now serves more demand variability. Scaling factor:

$$
k = \sqrt{\frac{n_{old}}{n_{new}}} = \sqrt{\frac{9}{4}} = 1.5
$$

**New safety stock:**

$$
SS_{new} = 800 \times 1.5 = 1{,}200 \text{ units}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Cost implication (3 marks):**

- Facility cost reduces by $\frac{9-4}{9} \approx 44.4\%$ (5 fewer warehouses).
- Inventory carrying cost **rises** by $1{,}200 \times c \cdot i$ minus $800 \times c \cdot i$. If $c = ₹500, i = 25\%$, additional holding cost ≈ ₹50,000 per year.
- **Net effect**: Total cost falls only if the facility-cost saving > inventory-cost increase. This illustrates the *facility-vs-inventory trade-off* — a KTU favourite.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Module 4:**
> 1. **Rounding direction (–1 mark):** Always round $n^{*}$ **UP**, never down. A demand of 6.32 warehouses means **7**, not 6.
> 2. **Mis-stating the trade-off (–2 marks):** Saying "more warehouses always reduces cost" loses full credit. The correct statement: *Total cost is U-shaped; the optimum is at $n^{*}$*.
> 3. **Forgetting the $(1-\beta)$ term (–1 mark):** Service level is in the denominator — a 95% target uses $(1-0.95) = 0.05$.
> 4. **Mixing up square-root lead time vs facility scaling:** Lead time scales **up** as $\sqrt{n_{old}/n_{new}}$ when consolidating; facility count scales as $\sqrt{D_{new}/D_{old}}$ when demand rises. Do not swap the formulas.
> 5. **Ignoring qualitative reasoning in design-option questions:** A 7-mark sub-question on six design options requires a **minimum of 5–6 lines of trade-off commentary per option**; bullet-point-only answers lose up to 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- ☐ **Network design** is a *strategic* decision fixing 70–80% of supply chain cost.
- ☐ The **four pillars** are: facility location, capacity allocation, market assignment, sourcing.
- ☐ The **six design options** are: Direct Ship, Milk Run, Drop-Ship, Distributor, Hub-and-Spoke DC, Multi-Echelon.
- ☐ **Total Cost Trade-off Curve** is U-shaped — facility cost ↑ with $n$, but inventory + transport ↓.
- ☐ **Square Root Law:** $n^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot c \cdot L}{2 F (1-\beta)}}$ — memorise this verbatim.
- ☐ $n^{*}$ scales as $\sqrt{D}$ — **doubling demand → ~1.41× facilities**.
- ☐ Lead time scales as $\sqrt{n_{old}/n_{new}}$ on consolidation.
- ☐ Always round $n^{*}$ **UP** to satisfy the service-level constraint.
- ☐ Five transport modes: Air, Road, Rail, Water, Pipeline — recall cost/speed ranking.
- ☐ Intermodal transport combines rail/road/sea to optimise cost and reach.
- ☐ Milk runs and cross-docking reduce transport cost per drop via consolidation.
- ☐ Information intensity rises as we move toward Direct-Ship and Drop-Ship options.
- ☐ Multi-echelon networks maximise service level but minimise cost efficiency.
- ☐ Remember **EOQ** $Q = \sqrt{2DS/h}$ — used in transport-cost-per-shipment calculations.
- ☐ In KTU valuation: justify *why* an option is chosen, not just *what* it is.
- ☐ Always state assumptions: uniform demand, identical facilities, single sourcing, deterministic lead time.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Design Options for a Transportation and Logistics Network**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trade-offs in Transportation Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trade-offs in Transportation Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Trade-offs in Transportation Design** refer to the deliberate balancing of conflicting performance criteria—primarily **cost, speed, reliability, flexibility, capacity, and frequency**—when designing a transportation network or selecting a transport mode. Because no single mode of transport simultaneously minimizes cost *and* maximizes speed *and* guarantees reliability, supply chain managers must make informed compromises based on product characteristics, customer service-level requirements, and total landed-cost objectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET418 Module 4):**
> Transportation design involves selecting the optimal transport mode (Road, Rail, Air, Water, Pipeline, or Intermodal) and network structure (Direct shipping vs. Consolidated shipping, Hub-and-Spoke vs. Point-to-Point) by evaluating the **economic, operational, and service-level trade-offs** inherent in each option.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Delivery Dilemma"

Imagine you need to send a fragile, time-sensitive birthday gift from Kochi to Delhi. You essentially have three options:

| Option | Analogy | Trade-off Faced |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Courier** | The Sports Car | 🏎️ Fastest, but you pay 10× the fuel cost |
| **Trucks (Roadways)** | The Family Sedan | 🚗 Balanced speed and cost, good for most trips |
| **Indian Railways / Sea** | The Cargo Ship / Train | 🚂 Cheapest per kg, but takes 5–10× longer |

Now imagine you are a **logistics manager** deciding how to ship 10,000 smartphones every week. The same dilemma applies, but the stakes are now in **lakhs of rupees**. The "Trade-off" is the **engineering compromise**: *"How do I minimize total supply chain cost while honoring my promised delivery date to the customer?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The cheapest mode of transport is **not always** the lowest-cost option for the entire supply chain. A slow, cheap mode forces you to carry more **pipeline inventory**, which adds hidden holding costs. **Total Cost = Transportation Cost + Inventory Carrying Cost + Other Costs.**

### 1.3 The Six Core Evaluation Criteria (Chopra & Meindl Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these **Six Criteria** — they appear almost every KTU exam under the heading *"Criteria for Evaluating Transport Modes."*

1. **Cost** — Per-unit shipping expense (₹/ton-km).
2. **Speed (Transit Time)** — Door-to-door delivery duration in **days** or **hours**.
3. **Reliability (Consistency)** — Variance in transit time from shipment to shipment.
4. **Capability** — Ability to handle specific product size, weight, temperature, or hazmat requirements.
5. **Accessibility (Reach)** — Number of origin–destination pairs the mode can serve.
6. **Frequency** — How often the mode departs / arrives at a given location.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics to Remember

- **Pipeline Inventory** = Average Daily Demand × Transit Time
- **Average Inventory in Transit** = Q/2 only when Q is the lot size; full lot is in transit
- **Cube Utilization** = Weight × Volume relationship (limits vehicle capacity)
- **Truckload (TL)** vs. **Less-than-Truckload (LTL)** rate break threshold
- **Break-Even Distance** = Distance at which two modes cost the same
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Transportation Cost Structure

The **Total Logistics Cost (TLC)** of a transportation decision can be decomposed as:

$$
\text{TLC} = C_{transport} + C_{inventory} + C_{facility} + C_{processing} + C_{service\_failure}
$$

For transportation design trade-offs, the two most critical cost components are:

$$
\text{Total Relevant Cost} = C_{transport} + C_{pipeline\_inventory}
$$

Where:
- $C_{transport}$ = Line-haul + Pickup/Delivery + Handling charges
- $C_{pipeline\_inventory}$ = In-transit holding cost charged on the goods while they are being shipped

### 2.2 The Seven Major Trade-offs in Transportation Design

#### Trade-off 1 — **Cost vs. Speed**
- **Air** is fast but expensive (₹40–80 per kg-km)
- **Water** is slow but cheap (₹2–5 per kg-km)
- A faster mode *reduces* pipeline inventory, partially offsetting the freight premium.

#### Trade-off 2 — **Cost vs. Reliability**
- Premium carriers charge 15–30% more for guaranteed delivery windows.
- Unreliable modes force **safety stock** in the receiver's warehouse, raising inventory cost.

#### Trade-off 3 — **Direct Shipping vs. Consolidated Shipping**
- **Direct**: Faster, but each shipment carries fixed costs.
- **Consolidation**: Lower per-unit cost via full-truck loads, but longer lead time.

#### Trade-off 4 — **Single Mode vs. Intermodal**
- Intermodal (e.g., Rail + Road) exploits the **cost advantage of long-haul rail** with the **flexibility of last-mile trucking**.

#### Trade-off 5 — **Vehicle Capacity vs. Operating Frequency**
- Larger vehicles = lower cost/unit, but require higher demand to fill.
- Small vehicles = more flexible dispatch, but higher per-unit cost.

#### Trade-off 6 — **Inventory Carrying Cost vs. Transportation Cost**
- Increasing the **Order Quantity (Q)** lowers transport cost per unit (better truck utilization) but **increases cycle stock** in warehouses.

#### Trade-off 7 — **Network Density vs. Service Level**
- More warehouses (or cross-docks) shorten delivery distance, but raise facility and inventory costs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{pipeline} = D \times T$ | Pipeline Inventory (Chopra & Meindl) | units |
| 2 | $C_{pipeline} = I_{pipeline} \times h$ | Pipeline inventory carrying cost | ₹/year |
| 3 | $TC = C_{transport} + h \cdot D \cdot T$ | Total relevant cost for one mode | ₹/year |
| 4 | $T_{break} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{transport}}{\Delta C_{inv}/unit}$ | Break-even transit time difference | days |
| 5 | $Q_{TL} = \dfrac{\text{Truck Capacity (kg)}}{\text{Product Density}}$ | Truckload order quantity | units |
| 6 | $TL_{rate} < LTL_{rate}$ when $Q \geq Q_{break}$ | TL/LTL break-even threshold | units |
| 7 | $VR = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Vehicle utilization ratio | % |
| 8 | $t_{door-to-door} = t_{pickup} + t_{line-haul} + t_{delivery}$ | Total transit time | hours/days |
| 9 | $D_{break} = \dfrac{F_{TL} - F_{LTL}}{(r_{LTL} - r_{TL})}$ | Break-even distance between TL and LTL | km |

Where:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $T$ = Transit time (years, usually days converted to years)
- $h$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $F$ = Fixed cost of a shipment
- $r$ = Variable cost rate per km
- $Q$ = Order quantity

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not confuse $Q$ with flow rate.** In pipeline inventory, $Q$ is **not** EOQ. The full shipment lot is "frozen" in transit, so the average pipeline inventory equals the **full shipment size** only when continuous flow is assumed, else it is $Q/2$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Amazon's Two-Day Delivery Network** uses a **trade-off algorithm** balancing air freight cost vs. regional warehouse inventory.
- **Reliance Fresh** uses **Hub-and-Spoke cold-chain trucking** to balance perishability (speed) with rural reach (cost).
- **Cochin Shipyard's inbound steel logistics** evaluates Rail vs. Road vs. Sea trade-offs using exactly the TLC framework above.

### 2.5 Decision Logic — The Trade-off Decision Tree

Step 1: Identify **product attributes** — weight, volume, hazmat, perishability, value density.
Step 2: Identify **customer requirements** — promised lead time, service level %.
Step 3: List the **feasible modes** capable of meeting the requirement.
Step 4: For each feasible mode, compute **Total Relevant Cost** = Transport + Pipeline Inventory.
Step 5: Select the mode with the **minimum Total Relevant Cost** *provided* service level constraints are met.
Step 6: If tie, select the mode with **lower variance (higher reliability)** as a risk-averse default.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pipeline Inventory Cost Comparison Between Two Modes

**Problem Setup:**
A firm ships 50,000 units/year of a product from Plant A to Customer B.
- Mode 1 (Truck): Transit time = 4 days, Rate = ₹5/unit
- Mode 2 (Rail): Transit time = 10 days, Rate = ₹3/unit
- Holding cost $h$ = ₹2/unit/year
- Working days per year = 250

**Step 1 — Convert transit time to years:**

$$
T_1 = \dfrac{4}{250} = 0.016 \text{ years}
$$

$$
T_2 = \dfrac{10}{250} = 0.040 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Transportation Cost for each mode:**

$$
C_{trans,1} = 50{,}000 \times 5 = ₹2{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
C_{trans,2} = 50{,}000 \times 3 = ₹1{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Pipeline Inventory and its Carrying Cost:**

$$
I_{1} = D \times T_1 = 50{,}000 \times 0.016 = 800 \text{ units}
$$

$$
I_{2} = D \times T_2 = 50{,}000 \times 0.040 = 2{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

$$
C_{inv,1} = 800 \times 2 = ₹1{,}600
$$

$$
C_{inv,2} = 2{,}000 \times 2 = ₹4{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Relevant Cost (TRC):**

$$
TRC_1 = C_{trans,1} + C_{inv,1} = 2{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}600 = ₹2{,}51{,}600
$$

$$
TRC_2 = C_{trans,2} + C_{inv,2} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}000 = ₹1{,}54{,}000
$$

**Conclusion:** Even though Trucks are faster, **Rail is the lower-total-cost mode** because the freight savings (₹1,00,000) far outweigh the additional pipeline inventory holding cost (₹2,400).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU examiners often provide a setup where the *faster* mode has *higher* transport cost, but the trade-off still favours it when $h$ is very high (e.g., pharmaceuticals, electronics). Always recompute — **do not assume** that "cheapest freight = cheapest total."

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Break-Even Analysis for Mode Selection

**Problem Setup:**
A firm must choose between **Air** and **Truck** for a 1,500 km lane.
- Air: $T_{air} = 1$ day, $R_{air} = ₹40$/unit
- Truck: $T_{truck} = 3$ days, $R_{truck} = ₹20$/unit
- Annual demand $D = 100{,}000$ units, $h = ₹5$/unit/year, 365 working days.

**Step 1 — Express TRC for each mode as a function of $T$:**

$$
TRC_{air} = D \cdot R_{air} + h \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{T_{air}}{365}
$$

$$
TRC_{truck} = D \cdot R_{truck} + h \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{T_{truck}}{365}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute TRC for Air:**

$$
TRC_{air} = (100{,}000 \times 40) + (5 \times 100{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{365})
$$

$$
TRC_{air} = 40{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}369.86 = ₹40{,}01{,}370
$$

**Step 3 — Compute TRC for Truck:**

$$
TRC_{truck} = (100{,}000 \times 20) + (5 \times 100{,}000 \times \dfrac{3}{365})
$$

$$
TRC_{truck} = 20{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}109.59 = ₹20{,}04{,}110
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Break-Even Holding Cost** $h^*$, where $TRC_{air} = TRC_{truck}$:

$$
D \cdot R_{air} + h^* \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{T_{air}}{365} = D \cdot R_{truck} + h^* \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{T_{truck}}{365}
$$

$$
h^* \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{(T_{truck} - T_{air})}{365} = D \cdot (R_{truck} - R_{air})
$$

$$
h^* = \dfrac{(R_{air} - R_{truck})}{\dfrac{(T_{truck} - T_{air})}{365}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the numbers:**

$$
h^* = \dfrac{(40 - 20)}{\dfrac{(3 - 1)}{365}} = \dfrac{20 \times 365}{2} = ₹3{,}650 \text{ / unit / year}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision Rule:**

$$
\text{If } h > h^* = ₹3{,}650 \text{, then Air is cheaper (high-value goods).}
$$

$$
\text{If } h < h^* = ₹3{,}650 \text{, then Truck is cheaper (low-value bulk goods).}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This break-even concept is the **heart** of KTU Module 4. Be prepared to derive the break-even *holding cost*, *demand*, or *transit time difference* in a 14-mark Part B question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — TL vs. LTL Consolidation Trade-off

**Problem Setup:**
- Annual demand = 24,000 units
- LTL rate = ₹15/unit, but includes a fixed pickup/delivery fee of ₹2,000 per shipment
- TL rate = ₹10/unit, no fixed fee, but minimum shipment = 4,000 units
- Order quantity $Q$ = 1,200 units

**Step 1 — Number of Shipments per Year:**

$$
N = \dfrac{D}{Q} = \dfrac{24{,}000}{1{,}200} = 20 \text{ shipments/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Cost using LTL:**

$$
TC_{LTL} = (D \times 15) + (N \times 2{,}000) = 3{,}60{,}000 + 40{,}000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — LTL is not feasible for TL** (since $Q = 1{,}200 < 4{,}000$ minimum).

**Step 4 — What if the firm increases $Q$ to 4,000?**

$$
N_{new} = \dfrac{24{,}000}{4{,}000} = 6 \text{ shipments/year}
$$

$$
TC_{TL} = (24{,}000 \times 10) + 0 = ₹2{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}40{,}000 = ₹1{,}60{,}000 \text{ / year}
$$

**Step 6 — However, increased $Q$ raises Cycle Stock:**

$$
\text{Extra Cycle Stock} = \dfrac{Q_{new} - Q_{old}}{2} = \dfrac{4{,}000 - 1{,}200}{2} = 1{,}400 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\text{Extra Holding Cost} = 1{,}400 \times h
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $h = ₹10$/unit/year, extra holding cost = ₹14,000, so **net savings = ₹1,60,000 − ₹14,000 = ₹1,46,000**. The trade-off is favourable toward **consolidation into TL**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode Comparison

| Mode | Cost (₹/ton-km) | Speed | Reliability | Flexibility | Capacity | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Air** | 40–80 | Very High | High | Medium | Low | High-value, low-weight, urgent goods (electronics, pharma) |
| **Road (Truck)** | 8–15 | High | High | Very High | Medium | Short-to-medium haul, FMCG, last-mile |
| **Rail** | 3–6 | Medium | Medium | Low | Very High | Bulk commodities, long-haul, low-value goods |
| **Water (Sea/Inland)** | 1–4 | Low | Medium | Low | Very High | International, heavy bulk (coal, grain, oil) |
| **Pipeline** | 2–5 | Very Low | Very High | Very Low | High | Liquids & gases (oil, gas, water, slurry) |
| **Intermodal** | 5–12 | Medium-High | Medium | High | High | Long-distance, cost-sensitive, containerized |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Transportation Mode Trade-off Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transportation Design Problem] --> B{Product Profile Analysis}
    B --> B1[Value Density High]
    B --> B2[Value Density Low]
    B --> B3[Perishable or Time Critical]
    B1 --> C1[Air or Express Road]
    B2 --> C2[Rail or Water or Pipeline]
    B3 --> C3[Air or Refrigerated Road]
    C1 --> D[Total Relevant Cost Comparison]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Cost plus Pipeline Inventory]
    D1 --> E{Service Level Constraint Met}
    E -- Yes --> F[Select Optimal Mode]
    E -- No --> G[Compromise by Buffer Stock or Premium Carrier]
    F --> H[Implement and Monitor Variance]
    G --> H
```

### 4.2 Cost vs. Service Level Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Cost] -->|Increases| B[Service Level]
    B --> C[High Cost]
    C -->|Increases| D[Customer Satisfaction]
    D --> E[Higher Revenue]
    A -.->|Trade-off| F[Total Supply Chain Cost Curve]
    C -.->|Trade-off| F
    F --> G[Optimum Service Level]
```

### 4.3 Mode Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Selection] --> P1{Is product perishable or hazmat}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2{Is lead time under 24 hours}
    P1 -- No --> P3{Is shipment weight under 100 kg}
    P2 -- Yes --> M1[Air Freight]
    P2 -- No --> M2[Refrigerated Road or Rail]
    P3 -- Yes --> M3[Air or Express Parcel]
    P3 -- No --> P4{Is distance under 500 km}
    P4 -- Yes --> M4[Road TL or LTL]
    P4 -- No --> P5{Is shipment over 10 tons}
    P5 -- Yes --> M5[Rail or Sea]
    P5 -- No --> M6[Intermodal Container]
    M1 --> OUT[Mode Selected]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
    M5 --> OUT
    M6 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Total Cost Decomposition in Transportation Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TLC[Total Logistics Cost] --> C1[Transportation Cost]
    TLC --> C2[Inventory Carrying Cost]
    TLC --> C3[Facility Cost]
    TLC --> C4[Processing and IT Cost]
    TLC --> C5[Service Failure Cost]
    C1 --> C1a[Line Haul]
    C1 --> C1b[Pickup and Delivery]
    C1 --> C1c[Handling Charges]
    C2 --> C2a[Cycle Stock]
    C2 --> C2b[Pipeline Inventory]
    C2 --> C2c[Safety Stock]
    C2a -.->|Increases with Q| C1
    C2b -.->|Decreases with Speed| C1
    C2c -.->|Decreases with Reliability| C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Mermaid Diagrams?** KTU does not require software-generated drawings. A clean block diagram (as above) demonstrates analytical thinking, satisfies the "diagram" instruction in the syllabus, and is examiner-friendly when drawn in the answer booklet. Replicate these as labelled boxes connected by arrows during your exam.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Pipeline Inventory" in the context of supply chain transportation. Why is it considered a key trade-off factor in transportation design?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Pipeline inventory refers to the quantity of goods that are in transit between the supplier and the customer at any point in time. Formally, it is the product of the **average demand rate (D)** and the **transit time (T)**, i.e., $I_{pipeline} = D \times T$. It is essentially "inventory in motion" that is not available at either the source or the destination warehouse.

**Trade-off Significance (1 mark):** Pipeline inventory is a key trade-off factor because a faster (and more expensive) transport mode reduces pipeline inventory, while a slower (and cheaper) mode increases it. The optimal mode balances freight cost against the carrying cost of this in-transit inventory.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any **six** criteria used to evaluate transportation modes in supply chain design.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Cost** — Per-unit transportation expense.
2. **Speed / Transit Time** — Door-to-door delivery duration.
3. **Reliability / Consistency** — Variance in actual vs. promised delivery time.
4. **Capability** — Ability to handle product-specific constraints (size, weight, temperature).
5. **Accessibility / Reach** — Number of origin–destination pairs served.
6. **Frequency** — Number of departures/arrivals per unit time.

> [!TIP]
> KTU's official answer key accepts the **Chopra & Meindl six-criteria framework** verbatim. Memorize the exact wording above.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often select a mode based **only on freight rate**, ignoring pipeline inventory. This is the most common reason for losing 4–5 marks on transportation trade-off problems. **Always compute Total Relevant Cost (TRC) = Transport Cost + Pipeline Inventory Cost.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 4]*

A company ships **40,000 units/year** of an automotive component from its plant in Pune to its distribution center in Chennai.

| Parameter | Truck (Road) | Rail |
|---|---|---|
| Transit time | 3 days | 7 days |
| Freight rate | ₹8 per unit | ₹5 per unit |
| Damage/loss rate | 1% | 3% |
| Product value | ₹500 per unit | ₹500 per unit |

- Holding cost $h$ = 20% of product value per year
- Working days = 300

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Compute the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** for each mode and identify the economically optimal mode.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Now introduce a **damage-cost component** equal to (damage % × product value × annual demand) into the TRC. Re-evaluate and comment on whether the optimal choice changes.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Convert transit time to years (1 mark):**

$$
T_{truck} = \dfrac{3}{300} = 0.01 \text{ year}
$$

$$
T_{rail} = \dfrac{7}{300} = 0.0233 \text{ year}
$$

**Step 2 — Holding cost per unit per year (1 mark):**

$$
h = 0.20 \times 500 = ₹100 \text{ / unit / year}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual transportation cost (1 mark):**

$$
C_{trans,truck} = 40{,}000 \times 8 = ₹3{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
C_{trans,rail} = 40{,}000 \times 5 = ₹2{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Pipeline inventory and its carrying cost (2 marks):**

$$
I_{pipeline,truck} = 40{,}000 \times 0.01 = 400 \text{ units}
$$

$$
I_{pipeline,rail} = 40{,}000 \times 0.0233 = 933.33 \text{ units}
$$

$$
C_{inv,truck} = 400 \times 100 = ₹40{,}000
$$

$$
C_{inv,rail} = 933.33 \times 100 = ₹93{,}333
$$

**Step 5 — Total Relevant Cost (2 marks):**

$$
TRC_{truck} = 3{,}20{,}000 + 40{,}000 = ₹3{,}60{,}000
$$

$$
TRC_{rail} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 93{,}333 = ₹2{,}93{,}333
$$

**Conclusion (within 7 marks):** **Rail is the economically optimal mode** with a cost advantage of ₹66,667 per year.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Annual damage cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{damage,truck} = 0.01 \times 40{,}000 \times 500 = ₹2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{damage,rail} = 0.03 \times 40{,}000 \times 500 = ₹6{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Revised Total Relevant Cost (2 marks):**

$$
TRC'_{truck} = 3{,}60{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹5{,}60{,}000
$$

$$
TRC'_{rail} = 2{,}93{,}333 + 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹8{,}93{,}333
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the revised gap (1 mark):**

$$
\text{New Savings of Truck over Rail} = 8{,}93{,}333 - 5{,}60{,}000 = ₹3{,}33{,}333 \text{ / year}
$$

**Conclusion & Comment (2 marks):** Yes, the optimal choice **flips from Rail to Truck** once damage cost is incorporated. This demonstrates a classic **cost-vs-reliability trade-off**: rail was cheaper in pure freight + inventory terms, but its higher damage exposure makes truck the lower-total-cost option when quality loss is monetized. In real supply chains, the **damage/loss component is often the deciding factor** for fragile or high-value components.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 4, Alternative Choice]*

A logistics manager is evaluating **three transport modes** for a 2,000-km lane carrying industrial valves worth ₹1,200 per unit. Annual demand is **80,000 units**, $h$ = 25% of value, and the firm operates **360 days/year**.

| Mode | Rate (₹/unit) | Transit Time (days) | Reliability Score (1–10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air | 60 | 1 | 9 |
| Truck | 25 | 4 | 7 |
| Rail | 12 | 8 | 5 |

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Compute the TRC for each mode assuming **only freight + pipeline inventory** are considered. Which mode is optimal?

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**
If a **safety stock of 800 units** must be maintained for Rail shipments due to its poor reliability, and each safety stock unit costs the firm ₹300/year in additional holding, recompute the TRC for Rail and determine if it remains optimal.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Holding cost per unit per year (1 mark):**

$$
h = 0.25 \times 1{,}200 = ₹300 \text{ / unit / year}
$$

**Step 2 — Transit times in years (1 mark):**

$$
T_{air} = \dfrac{1}{360}, \quad T_{truck} = \dfrac{4}{360}, \quad T_{rail} = \dfrac{8}{360}
$$

**Step 3 — Transport cost for each mode (1 mark):**

$$
C_{air} = 80{,}000 \times 60 = ₹48{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{truck} = 80{,}000 \times 25 = ₹20{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{rail} = 80{,}000 \times 12 = ₹9{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Pipeline inventory cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{inv,air} = 300 \times 80{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{360} = ₹66{,}667
$$

$$
C_{inv,truck} = 300 \times 80{,}000 \times \dfrac{4}{360} = ₹2{,}66{,}667
$$

$$
C_{inv,rail} = 300 \times 80{,}000 \times \dfrac{8}{360} = ₹5{,}33{,}333
$$

**Step 5 — Total Relevant Cost (2 marks):**

$$
TRC_{air} = 48{,}00{,}000 + 66{,}667 = ₹48{,}66{,}667
$$

$$
TRC_{truck} = 20{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}66{,}667 = ₹22{,}66{,}667
$$

$$
TRC_{rail} = 9{,}60{,}000 + 5{,}33{,}333 = ₹14{,}93{,}333
$$

**Conclusion (within 7 marks):** **Rail is optimal** in pure cost terms, with TRC of ₹14,93,333. However, the manager must verify whether 8-day transit time meets customer service-level agreements.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Add safety stock holding cost to Rail TRC (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Extra Safety Stock Cost} = 800 \text{ units} \times ₹300 = ₹2{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Revised TRC for Rail (2 marks):**

$$
TRC'_{rail} = 14{,}93{,}333 + 2{,}40{,}000 = ₹17{,}33{,}333
$$

**Step 3 — Compare all three modes again (2 marks):**

$$
TRC_{air} = ₹48{,}66{,}667, \quad TRC_{truck} = ₹22{,}66{,}667, \quad TRC'_{rail} = ₹17{,}33{,}333
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Rail **remains optimal** because its revised TRC (₹17,33,333) is still lower than Truck (₹22,66,667). However, the **margin of savings shrinks** from ₹7,73,334 to ₹5,33,334, illustrating that **reliability penalties erode the cost advantage of cheap modes**. The manager should also evaluate whether 8-day transit time is acceptable; if not, the trade-off forces a switch to Truck despite the higher cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Recap — How Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert transit time to years** (cost of 1–2 marks per sub-part).
> 2. **Ignoring the safety stock component** when reliability is explicitly mentioned in the question.
> 3. **Concluding "Truck is best" because it has '7 days' "** without computing actual TRC.
> 4. **Not stating the final recommendation** in a single sentence — KTU deducts 1 mark for ambiguous conclusions.
> 5. **Mixing up units** — using ₹/km instead of ₹/unit, or using 250 days instead of 360 days.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Six criteria for mode evaluation (Chopra & Meindl):** Cost, Speed, Reliability, Capability, Accessibility, Frequency.
- **Pipeline inventory formula:** $I_{pipeline} = D \times T$ — this is the **invisible** cost that turns a "cheap mode" into an expensive one.
- **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** is the **gold standard** for comparing modes, not freight rate alone.
- **Break-even holding cost $h^*$** dictates the mode choice: high $h$ → faster mode; low $h$ → cheaper mode.
- **Consolidation trade-off:** Larger $Q$ = lower transport cost/unit but higher cycle stock. Optimal $Q$ balances both.
- **TL vs. LTL:** Truckload saves fixed pickup/delivery cost but requires minimum shipment size.
- **Intermodal transport** combines cost efficiency of rail/water with flexibility of road for first/last mile.
- **Damage/loss and safety stock** are the two most overlooked components that frequently flip the optimal mode.
- **Air** is justified only for **high value density** (₹/kg) and **time-critical** shipments.
- **Pipeline** is the most reliable and lowest cost for liquids/gases but has zero flexibility.
- **Decision Rule of Thumb:** Compute TRC → Check service-level constraint → Choose minimum TRC among feasible modes.
- **Always convert all times to the same unit (years)** before plugging into formulas.
- **KTU Mark Distribution Pattern (Typical):** 7-mark question = 4–5 marks for computation + 2–3 marks for interpretation/recommendation.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tailored Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET418 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tailored Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Tailored Transportation** refers to the strategic design and deployment of customized transportation solutions, networks, and service offerings that are specifically engineered to align with the unique requirements of a product, customer segment, geographical footprint, or business model within a supply chain ecosystem. It moves beyond generic, one-size-fits-all freight movement by integrating mode selection, network design, carrier relationships, technology platforms, and service-level specifications into a coherent, demand-specific logistics architecture.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for PEMET418, tailored transportation is positioned as the operational layer that converts a supply chain strategy into physical product flow, ensuring that **service differentiation, cost efficiency, and responsiveness** are jointly optimized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> "Tailored Transportation is the customization of transportation strategies, modes, routes, and service levels to precisely match the cost, speed, reliability, and handling requirements of specific products, markets, or customers, thereby converting supply chain intent into optimized physical distribution."

### Key Terminology Anchor Points

- **Service Level Customization:** Adjusting speed, reliability, and handling based on customer commitment.
- **Mode-Mix Engineering:** Combining road, rail, air, sea, and pipeline to balance cost and lead time.
- **Network Topology Personalization:** Designing hub-and-spoke, point-to-point, or hybrid distribution webs.
- **Carrier Portfolio Specialization:** Selecting 3PL, 4PL, dedicated, or common carriers based on capability match.
- **Value-Added Logistics Services (VALS):** White-glove delivery, reverse logistics, cold chain, and kitting.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bespoke tailor versus a clothing mall**. A mall sells standardized shirts in fixed sizes — they fit *most* people reasonably well but rarely *perfectly*. A bespoke tailor, however, takes measurements, understands the customer's posture, the climate they live in, and the occasions they dress for, and then constructs a garment that is uniquely theirs.

Tailored Transportation applies the same philosophy to logistics. Off-the-shelf freight (a standard truckload from point A to B) is the "mall shirt." Tailored Transportation, however, considers:
- The **fragility** of the product (glassware vs steel rods),
- The **urgency** of the customer (perishable pharma vs seasonal apparel),
- The **geography** (mountainous terrain vs coastal plains),
- The **sustainability mandate** (low-emission routing),
- The **return flow** (reverse logistics for e-commerce),

…and stitches together a movement solution that *fits the product-customer pair perfectly*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric in Industry Use:**
> The most universally tracked benchmark is **Cost per Ton-Kilometer (₹/ton-km)**, complemented by **On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)** percentage and **Perfect Order Fulfillment (POF)** index.

### Why Tailored Transportation Matters in KTU Context

- It is the **bridge between supply chain strategy and execution**.
- It directly influences the **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** of a product.
- It enables **mass customization** in distribution without sacrificing scale economies.
- It is a primary lever for achieving the KTU Course Outcome (CO4): *"Design logistics and transportation networks aligned with strategic objectives."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Tailored Transportation

1. **Product-Profile Mapping**
   - Weight, volume, dimensions, hazard class, perishability, value density.
   - Determines the *physical* constraints of the transport plan.

2. **Customer-Segment Differentiation**
   - B2B vs B2C vs B2G (Government).
   - Service-level agreements (SLAs) with varying penalty structures.

3. **Network Architecture Selection**
   - Point-to-Point, Hub-and-Spoke, Milk Run, Cross-Docking, Hybrid.
   - Each pattern yields a different cost-service curve.

4. **Mode and Carrier Alignment**
   - Air, road, rail, sea, pipeline, intermodal/multimodal combinations.
   - Carrier type: 1PL, 2PL, 3PL, 4PL.

5. **Technology and Visibility Layer**
   - Transportation Management System (TMS), GPS tracking, IoT sensors, EDI/API integration.
   - Real-time decision support and exception management.

### 2.2 Transportation Network Patterns (Detailed Breakdown)

- **Direct Shipping (Point-to-Point):** Each supplier ships directly to each customer. High inventory in transit, low handling cost per shipment, suitable for high-value or hazardous goods.

- **Hub-and-Spoke:** All shipments consolidate at a central hub before final dispatch. Economies of scale in long-haul, lower inventory, but increased handling and hub-bottleneck risk.

- **Milk Run:** A single vehicle makes multiple stops on a scheduled route, picking up or delivering at each. Suited to JIT environments and clustered suppliers.

- **Cross-Docking:** Goods move from inbound to outbound vehicles with minimal or zero storage. Reduces warehousing cost, accelerates throughput, ideal for fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG).

- **Drop-Shipping:** Manufacturer ships directly to the end consumer, bypassing retailer warehousing. The ultimate "tailored" form for e-commerce.

### 2.3 Cost Components of a Tailored Transportation Plan

- **Fixed Transportation Cost (F):** Vehicle acquisition, driver salary, permits, insurance — incurred regardless of load.
- **Variable Cost per Kilometer (v):** Fuel, tires, maintenance.
- **Loading/Unloading Cost (L):** Per shipment or per unit.
- **Inventory-in-Transit Cost (I):** Capital tied up in goods moving through the network.
- **Service Penalty Cost (P):** Late delivery, damage, lost shipment.
- **Sustainability/Emissions Cost (E):** Carbon tax, green compliance.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Transportation Cost | $TC = F + v \cdot d + L \cdot Q$ | $F$=₹ fixed; $v$=₹/km; $d$=km; $L$=₹/unit; $Q$=units | Basic cost build-up |
| Cost per Ton-Kilometer | $C_{tk} = \dfrac{TC}{W \cdot d}$ | $W$=tonnes | Industry benchmark |
| Break-Even Quantity (FTL vs LTL) | $Q_{be} = \dfrac{F_{FTL} - F_{LTL}}{L_{LTL} - L_{FTL}}$ | Costs in ₹; Q in units | Mode selection decision |
| Vehicle Utilization Index | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Both in units/tonnes | Operational efficiency |
| Inventory-in-Transit Cost | $I_{transit} = C_{unit} \cdot Q \cdot \dfrac{d}{s} \cdot i$ | $C_{unit}$=unit cost ₹; $s$=speed km/day; $i$=holding rate | Working capital impact |
| Total Landed Cost | $TLC = C_{prod} + TC + I_{transit} + P + E$ | All in ₹ per unit | Strategic cost view |
| Milk-Run Cost Model | $C_{MR} = F_{truck} + v \cdot d_{total} + n \cdot C_{stop}$ | $n$=stops; $C_{stop}$=₹/stop | Multi-stop routing |
| OTIF Score | $OTIF = \dfrac{N_{OTIF}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Both in shipment count | Service quality KPI |
| Cross-Dock Throughput Time | $T_{cd} = T_{receive} + T_{sort} + T_{dispatch}$ | All in hours | Velocity optimization |
| Carbon Cost (Linear) | $E = e \cdot d \cdot C_{CO2}$ | $e$=kgCO2/km; $C_{CO2}$=₹/kgCO2 | Green logistics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In any cost-comparison problem, always state assumptions clearly — load factor, return-trip utilization, tolls, and waiting time. Examiners award marks for **assumption articulation**, not just the final number.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Firms:** Use tailored transportation to feed assembly lines Just-In-Time, minimizing Work-In-Process (WIP).
- **Pharmaceutical & Cold-Chain:** Temperature-controlled tailored solutions prevent spoilage; a 1°C deviation can destroy entire batches.
- **E-Commerce & Retail:** Last-mile tailoring via gig-economy, electric vehicles, and locker networks.
- **Automotive (JIT/Lean):** Milk-run inbound logistics synchronizes hundreds of parts to the assembly line every hour.
- **Disaster-Response Supply Chains:** Tailored emergency routes bypass damaged infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Work 1 — Deriving the Break-Even Quantity for FTL vs LTL

**Problem Context:** A firm must choose between hiring a Full Truckload (FTL) and shipping Less-than-Truckload (LTL). We need the exact quantity at which the two options cost the same.

**Step 1: Write the cost functions**

The FTL cost is largely a fixed cost $F_{FTL}$ paid for the whole truck, independent of how full it is (within capacity). The LTL cost is a per-unit charge $L_{LTL}$ since the carrier consolidates the load with others.

$$
C_{FTL} = F_{FTL}
$$

$$
C_{LTL} = L_{LTL} \cdot Q
$$

**Step 2: Equate to find the break-even point**

At break-even, the cost of both options is identical:

$$
F_{FTL} = L_{LTL} \cdot Q_{be}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $Q_{be}$**

$$
Q_{be} = \frac{F_{FTL}}{L_{LTL}}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

- If actual quantity $Q > Q_{be}$ → **FTL is cheaper** (economies of scale on fixed cost).
- If $Q < Q_{be}$ → **LTL is cheaper** (no need to pay for unused capacity).

**Step 5: Add fixed per-shipment LTL handling cost for the more rigorous form**

If LTL has an additional pickup/handling fee $F_{LTL}$:

$$
C_{LTL} = F_{LTL} + L_{LTL} \cdot Q
$$

Equating again:

$$
F_{FTL} = F_{LTL} + L_{LTL} \cdot Q_{be}
$$

$$
Q_{be} = \frac{F_{FTL} - F_{LTL}}{L_{LTL}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivative Work 2 — Cost per Ton-Kilometer Optimization

**Step 1: Express total cost**

$$
TC = F + v \cdot d
$$

**Step 2: Express total work performed (ton-km)**

$$
W = W_{load} \cdot d
$$

**Step 3: Define cost per ton-km**

$$
C_{tk} = \frac{TC}{W} = \frac{F + v \cdot d}{W_{load} \cdot d}
$$

**Step 4: Investigate behavior at limits**

- As $d \to \infty$ (very long haul):

$$
\lim_{d \to \infty} C_{tk} = \frac{v}{W_{load}}
$$

The fixed cost $F$ gets amortized over more ton-kilometers → the marginal cost approaches the variable cost per ton-km.

- As $d \to 0$ (very short haul):

$$
\lim_{d \to 0} C_{tk} = \infty
$$

Fixed cost dominates → short hauls are *extremely* expensive per ton-km. This is why tailor-designed last-mile solutions (e-rickshaws, cargo bikes) replace trucks for urban distribution.

### 3.3 Derivative Work 3 — Milk-Run Cost Function and Optimal Stop Count

**Step 1: Define parameters**

- $n$ = number of stops
- $d_i$ = distance from depot to stop $i$ and back
- $C_{stop}$ = fixed cost per stop (loading/unloading)
- $v$ = per-km cost
- $F_{truck}$ = daily fixed cost of operating one truck

**Step 2: Total milk-run cost**

$$
C_{MR}(n) = F_{truck} + v \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i + n \cdot C_{stop}
$$

**Step 3: Assumed geometry (depot at center, stops on a circle of radius $r$)**

The route distance grows sub-linearly with $n$ (TSP-like) but stop costs grow linearly:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \approx 2 \pi r \cdot \sqrt{n} \quad \text{(heuristic)}
$$

So:

$$
C_{MR}(n) \approx F_{truck} + 2 \pi r v \sqrt{n} + n \cdot C_{stop}
$$

**Step 4: Take the derivative with respect to $n$ and set to zero for optimum**

$$
\frac{d C_{MR}}{d n} = \frac{2 \pi r v}{2 \sqrt{n}} + C_{stop} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\pi r v}{\sqrt{n^{*}}} = -C_{stop}
$$

This indicates an interior minimum only exists if $C_{stop}$ is negative (impossible) — meaning cost is monotonically *increasing* if we add stops naively. Hence, real-world milk runs cap $n$ via **clustered geography** so $\sum d_i$ grows slowly.

**Step 5: Practical redesign**

Cluster stops within a small radius to keep the route tight, then add stops only when revenue per stop exceeds marginal cost:

$$
n_{opt} = \arg\min_{n} C_{MR}(n)
$$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Board-Exam Style)

**Question:** A manufacturer must transport 400 units from Plant A to Warehouse B (600 km). Two options:
- **Option X (FTL):** ₹18,000 flat, capacity 500 units.
- **Option Y (LTL):** ₹50 per unit, plus ₹2,000 handling.

**Step 1: Compute FTL cost**

$$
C_X = ₹18{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Compute LTL cost**

$$
C_Y = 2000 + 50 \cdot 400 = 2000 + 20{,}000 = ₹22{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Compare**

$$
C_X < C_Y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FTL is cheaper by ₹4,000}.
$$

**Step 4: Compute cost per unit-km**

$$
C_{X,uk} = \frac{18{,}000}{400 \cdot 600} = ₹0.075 / \text{unit-km}
$$

$$
C_{Y,uk} = \frac{22{,}000}{400 \cdot 600} \approx ₹0.0917 / \text{unit-km}
$$

**Step 5: Verify break-even quantity**

$$
Q_{be} = \frac{F_{FTL} - F_{LTL}}{L_{LTL}} = \frac{18{,}000 - 2{,}000}{50} = \frac{16{,}000}{50} = 320 \text{ units}
$$

Since $Q = 400 > Q_{be} = 320$, **FTL is the correct tailored choice**. The model's recommendation aligns with the direct cost comparison — a consistency check examiners love to see.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Decision Criterion | FTL Preferred When | LTL Preferred When | Intermodal Preferred When | Air Preferred When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shipment size | $Q > Q_{be}$ | $Q < Q_{be}$ | Large, regular lane | Emergency, perishables |
| Lead time tolerance | Moderate | Loose | Moderate | Very tight |
| Product value | High (less handling) | Low–Medium | High bulk, low value | Very high value density |
| Distance | Long-haul | Short–Medium | Very long domestic | Any, if critical |
| Cost sensitivity | Low | High | Moderate | Low |
| Sustainability | Truck fleet emissions | Higher per-unit emissions | Rail/water greener | Highest emissions |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tailored Transportation Design Framework (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strategic Business Objective] --> B[Analyze Product Profile]
    B --> B1[Weight, Volume, Hazard Class, Perishability]
    B1 --> C[Define Customer Service Levels]
    C --> C1[Lead Time, OTIF Target, White Glove Needs]
    C1 --> D[Map Demand Geography]
    D --> D1[Origin Destination Matrix, Volume Density]
    D1 --> E[Select Network Pattern]
    E --> E1[Point to Point, Hub Spoke, Milk Run, Cross Dock]
    E1 --> F[Choose Transportation Mode Mix]
    F --> F1[Road, Rail, Air, Sea, Intermodal]
    F1 --> G[Select Carrier Strategy]
    G --> G1[1PL, 3PL, 4PL, Dedicated Fleet]
    G1 --> H[Deploy TMS and Visibility]
    H --> H1[Real Time Tracking, IoT, API Integration]
    H1 --> I[Execute and Monitor KPIs]
    I --> I1[OTIF, Cost per Ton Km, Carbon, Damage Rate]
    I1 --> J[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    J --> B
```

### 4.2 Mode-Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Shipment Need Identified] --> Q{Is Lead Time Below 24 Hours}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Select Air Freight or Express Courier]
    Q -- No --> S{Is Shipment International}
    S -- Yes --> T[Select Sea Freight with Port Handling]
    S -- No --> U{Is Distance Over 500 km}
    U -- No --> V[Select Road Transport, FT or LT]
    U -- Yes --> W{Is Volume Above 15 Tonnes per Load}
    W -- Yes --> X[Consider Rail or Intermodal]
    W -- No --> Y[Select Road FT or LT]
    T --> Z[Apply Customs and Documentation Layer]
    R --> AA[Apply High Security and Tracking]
    X --> AB[Add Hub Transfer Point]
    V --> AC[Apply Route Optimization]
    Y --> AC
    Z --> AD[Final Tailored Solution]
    AA --> AD
    AB --> AD
    AC --> AD
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tailored Transportation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Demand and Supply Layer]
        I1[Customer Orders]
        I2[Production Schedule]
        I3[Inventory Levels]
    end

    subgraph PLANNING[Planning and Optimization Layer]
        P1[Demand Forecasting Module]
        P2[Network Design Engine]
        P3[Vehicle Routing Solver]
        P4[Mode Selection Algorithm]
    end

    subgraph EXECUTION[Execution Layer]
        E1[Carrier Allocation]
        E2[Dock and Yard Management]
        E3[In Transit Tracking with IoT]
        E4[Customs and Compliance]
    end

    subgraph SUPPORT[Support and Analytics Layer]
        S1[Cost Analytics Dashboard]
        S2[Carbon Emissions Reporter]
        S3[OTIF and Damage Tracker]
        S4[Continuous Improvement Engine]
    end
```
